built with pdoc

FORAGER.embedder

What this file does: Using the jsonl file with each line being a separate json representing a chunk...

  1. Embeds each chunk
  2. Stores each embedded chunk in FAISS database
  3. Includes search function

Potential next steps: Modify so embedder can take chunks from another file besides just chunks.jsonl, if necessary

  1"""
  2What this file does:
  3Using the jsonl file with each line being a separate json representing a chunk...
  41. Embeds each chunk
  52. Stores each embedded chunk in FAISS database
  63. Includes search function
  7
  8Potential next steps:
  9Modify so embedder can take chunks from another file besides just chunks.jsonl, if necessary
 10"""
 11
 12import os 
 13from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
 14import numpy as np
 15import faiss
 16import json
 17
 18# Initialize global variables
 19dim = 768 # Length of vectors generated by this embedding model
 20faiss_db = faiss.IndexFlatIP(dim)
 21chunks = []
 22embeddings = []
 23prefix = "Represent this sentence for retrieval: "
 24
 25# Start by preparing the embedding model
 26model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")
 27
 28# Important filepaths listed here
 29chunk_filepath = "FORAGER_corpus/heterogenous_integration/chunks/chunks.jsonl"
 30
 31test_chunk_filepath = "FORAGER_corpus/heterogenous_integration/chunks/test_chunks.jsonl"
 32
 33faiss_db_filepath = "FORAGER/vector_database/index_db.faiss"
 34os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(faiss_db_filepath), exist_ok=True)
 35
 36def embed_chunks(data, m, n):
 37    """
 38    Takes one chunk at a time from the new additions to chunks.jsonl file (or other inputted file) and embeds it using BGE model
 39
 40    Arguments: 
 41        filepath: The file path of the JSONL document containing the chunks genereated by ingestor.py and chunker.py
 42        m: Starting chunk entry
 43        n: Ending chunk entry
 44    Returns:
 45        embeddings: A 2-D numpy array of vectors, each representing a chunk
 46
 47    Variable type info: 
 48        chunk - dictionary (and each line in the JSONL file is a chunk)
 49        list of chunks - Python list
 50        text in chunk - one value in the chunk dictionary associated with "text" key
 51        "embeddings" - 2-D array (specifically "np.ndarray") with all the vectors. Each vector has a length of 768 using the BGE model
 52        Each element of "embeddings" is also of the type np.ndarray
 53        The dimensions of the "embeddings" array are [(length of chunk file), 768]
 54    """
 55
 56    global prefix
 57    global embeddings
 58
 59    # Select chunks to embed
 60    chunks_to_embed = data[m:n]
 61    print(f"\033[1;94mEmbedding {len(chunks_to_embed)} chunks...\033[0m\n")
 62
 63    # Extract and prefix the text
 64    texts = [prefix + d["text"] for d in chunks_to_embed] #The BGE model expects the prefix to come before what we want to embed
 65    len_before_embed = len(embeddings)
 66
 67    # Embed each chunk and normalize the resulting vector. Convert to float32 for insertion into FAISS index
 68    embeddings = model.encode(texts, normalize_embeddings = True).astype("float32")
 69    print(f"\033[1;92m✅ {len(embeddings) - len_before_embed} chunks successfully embedded!\033[0m\n")
 70
 71    # Return the 2-D array of vectors
 72    return embeddings
 73
 74def initial_embed_and_build(filepath):
 75    """
 76    Run this once when first running FORAGER to embed the first set of chunks and create the FAISS database
 77
 78    Arguments:
 79        filepath: path to the JSONL file containing the chunks to be embedded
 80
 81    Returns:
 82        embeddings: 2-D array of vectors
 83        faiss_db: New FAISS index stored in "FORAGER/vector_database/index_db.faiss"
 84    """
 85    global faiss_db
 86    global chunks
 87
 88    print(f"Filepath being used: {filepath}\n")
 89    # Initially opens the JSONL file, making each line a dictionary
 90
 91    with open(filepath, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
 92        chunks = [json.loads(line) for line in f if line.strip()]
 93
 94    # Embed all chunks initially in the JSONL and put them into the FAISS database
 95    embeddings = embed_chunks(chunks, 0, len(chunks))
 96
 97    faiss_db.add(embeddings)
 98    print(f"\033[1;92m✅ {faiss_db.ntotal} chunks successfully inserted into new FAISS database!\033[0m\n")
 99
100    # Write to the .faiss file to save the database
101    faiss.write_index(faiss_db, "FORAGER/vector_database/index_db.faiss")
102    print(f"\033[1;92m✅ FAISS database stored in {faiss_db_filepath}!\033[0m\n")
103    return embeddings, faiss_db
104
105def add_to_FAISS(new_embedded_chunks):
106    """
107    Function to embed and add chunks from newly uploaded documents into FAISS
108
109    Arguments:
110        new_embedded_chunks: New vectors to add to FAISS database
111    """
112
113    faiss_db.add(new_embedded_chunks)
114    faiss.write_index(faiss_db, faiss_db_filepath)
115
116def search_database(query, num_vectors):
117    """
118    Function to search the FAISS database for the closest k chunks to the query (based on dot product)
119    
120    Arguments:
121        query: User query or LLM response
122        num_vectors: Number of closest vectors to return
123    """
124
125    global prefix
126    global chunks
127    global embeddings
128
129    # Embed query
130    query_with_prefix = [prefix + query]
131    query_emb = model.encode(query_with_prefix, normalize_embeddings = True).astype("float32")
132
133    # Ensure k <= length of index
134    num_vectors = min(num_vectors, faiss_db.ntotal)
135
136    # Search index
137    scores, indices = faiss_db.search(query_emb, num_vectors)
138    # print(f"Scores: \n{scores}\n")
139    # print(f"indices: \n{indices}\n")
140
141    # Show results
142    print(f"\n\033[1;94mReturning the {num_vectors} most similar chunks for query: \"{query}\"\033[0m\n")
143    # print(chunks)
144    for rank, idx in enumerate(indices[0]):
145        print(f"{rank+1}. Source: {chunks[idx]["source_filename"]} \nChunk: {chunks[idx]["chunk_id"]} \nScore: {scores[0][rank]:.4f}\n")
146        print(f"{chunks[idx]["text"]}\n")
147
148def print_vector_in_array(embeddings, n):
149    """
150    Prints a vector in the 2-D array of chunk vectors at entry n
151
152    Arguments:
153        embeddings: 2-D array of chunk vectors
154        n: row of array to print (which vector)
155    """
156    print(f"Printing embedded vector at entry {n}...")
157    print(f"{embeddings[n]}\n")
158
159def print_vector_in_FAISS(n):
160    """
161    Prints a specified vector in the FAISS database
162
163    Arguments: 
164        n: id of vector to print
165    """
166    print(f"Printing vector in FAISS index at entry {n}...")
167    print(f"{faiss_db.reconstruct(n)}\n")
168
169def clear_database():
170    """
171    Takes everything out of the FAISS database, if necessary.
172    """
173    faiss_db.reset()
174
175#----------------------------#
176# Execution                  #
177#----------------------------#
178
179# Read in the FAISS database if it exists, or embed chunks and make a new one if it doesn't
180if os.path.exists(faiss_db_filepath):
181    faiss_db = faiss.read_index(faiss_db_filepath)
182    print(f"\033[1.96mFAISS database size: {faiss_db.ntotal}\033[0m\n")
183else:
184    embed_array, faiss_db = initial_embed_and_build(chunk_filepath)
185    print(f"\033[1.96mFAISS database size: {faiss_db.ntotal}\033[0m\n")
186
187# Read in chunks.jsonl
188with open(chunk_filepath, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
189    chunks = [json.loads(line) for line in f if line.strip()]
190
191# Check if more has been added to chunks.jsonl
192if len(chunks) > faiss_db.ntotal:
193    new_chunks = embed_chunks(chunks, faiss_db.ntotal, len(chunks))
194    add_to_FAISS(new_chunks)
195    print(f"\033[1.96mFAISS database size: {faiss_db.ntotal}\033[0m\n")
196else:
197    print(f"\033[1;92m✅ Database is up to date.\033[0m\n")
198
199
200# Test search
201test_query = "What is heterogeneous integration?"
202print(test_query)
203search_database(test_query, 5)
dim = 768
faiss_db = <faiss.swigfaiss.IndexFlatIP; proxy of <Swig Object of type 'faiss::IndexFlatIP *'> >

One of the solutions is to partition the SoC into chiplets,\nthe large FPGA into four smaller chiplets as shown in Fig. 7 reserving the'}, {'source_filename': '40307-state-of-the-art-and-outlooks-of-chiplets-heterogeneous-integration-and-hybrid-bonding.txt', 'title': '40307-state-of-the-art-and-outlooks-of-chiplets-heterogeneous-integration-and-hybrid-bonding', 'chunk_id': 11, 'start_token': 2750, 'end_token': 3050, 'text': ' very\nsize the yield was very poor. Then, Xilinx redesigned and split costly. One of the solutions is to partition the SoC into chiplets,\nthe large FPGA into four smaller chiplets as shown in Fig. 7 reserving the expensive leading-edge silicon for CPU core\nand TSMC manufactured the chiplets at high yield and pack- while leaving the I/Os and memory interfaces in n-1 generation\naged them on their CoWoS. On October 20, 2013 Xilinx and silicon [1, 2]. Another solution is to split the CPU core into\nTSMC [14] have jointly announced production release of the smaller chiplets. In this case, each CCD (core complex die or\nVirtex-7 HT family with 28nm process technology, what the CPU compute die) is split into two smaller chiplets. The HVM of SoIC,\nSoIC1CoWoS, and SoIC1InFO is expected in 2022.\nCHIPLETS LATERAL INTERCONNECTS\nOne of the horizontal communications between chiplets in a\nchiplet design and heterogeneous integration package is\nthrough bridges [17] such as Intel’s EMIB (embedded multidie\ninterconnect bridge). Fig. 16 shows one of Intel’s patents and\nthe Agilex FPGA (field programmable gate array) module. The\nFPGA and other chips are attached on top of a build-up pack-\nage substrate with EMIB [34, 35] with fine metal line width\n'}, {'source_filename': '40307-state-of-the-art-and-outlooks-of-chiplets-heterogeneous-integration-and-hybrid-bonding.txt', 'title': '40307-state-of-the-art-and-outlooks-of-chiplets-heterogeneous-integration-and-hybrid-bonding', 'chunk_id': 25, 'start_token': 6250, 'end_token': 6550, 'text': ' patents and\nthe Agilex FPGA (field programmable gate array) module. The\nFPGA and other chips are attached on top of a build-up pack-\nage substrate with EMIB [34, 35] with fine metal line width\nand spacing (L/S) RDLs (redistribution-layers). The C4 (con-\ntrolled collapse chip connection) bumps and C2 (chip connec-\ntion or micro) bumps are on the chiplets. One of the challenges\nof the EMIB technology is to fabricate the organic build-up\npackage substrate with cavities for the silicon bridges and then\nlaminate (with pressure and temperature) another build-up\nlayer on top (to meet the substrate surface flatness requirement)\nfor chiplets (with both C2 and C4 bumps) bonding.\nVery recently, IBM proposed a method called DBHi (direct\nbonded heterogeneous integration) [36]. DBI was invented by Research Triangle Institute\n(RTI) and patented it as ZiBond (a direct oxide to oxide bond-\ning that involves wafer-to-wafer processing at low tempera-\ntures to initiate high bond strengths). Between 2000 and 2001,\nFountain, Enguist, Tong, and several other colleagues founded\nZiptronic as a spin-out of RTI. Between 2004 and 2005,\nbased on their ZiBond technology, Ziptronic combined the\ndielectric bond with embedded metal to simultaneously bond\nwafers and form the interconnects at low temperature (so called\nDBI) [43, 44]. Ziptronic was acquired by Tessera on August\n28, 2015. Tessera has changed its name to Xperi on February\n23, 2017.\nThe breakthrough for Ziptronic DBI technology came in the\nspring of 2015 when Sony, already using its “Zibond” oxide\nto oxide bonding technology extended its license to include\nDBI'}, {'source_filename': '40307-state-of-the-art-and-outlooks-of-chiplets-heterogeneous-integration-and-hybrid-bonding.txt', 'title': '40307-state-of-the-art-and-outlooks-of-chiplets-heterogeneous-integration-and-hybrid-bonding', 'chunk_id': 33, 'start_token': 8250, 'end_token': 8550, 'text': '\n23, 2017.\nThe breakthrough for Ziptronic DBI technology came in the\nspring of 2015 when Sony, already using its “Zibond” oxide\nto oxide bonding technology extended its license to include\nDBI. [88] https://en.wikichip.org/wiki/chiplet,27March,2020.\n[78] K. Shie, P. Hsu, Y. Li, K. Tu, B. Lin, C. Chang, and C. Chen, [89] S. Sukegawa, T. Umebayashi, T. Nakajima, H. Kawanobe, K. Koseki, I.\n“Electromigration and temperature cycling tests of Cu-Cu joints fabricated Hirota, and T. Haruta, ““A 1/4-inch 8Mpixel Back-Illuminated Stacked\nby instant copper direct bonding,” IEEE/ECTC Proceedings, pp. 995- CMOS Image Sensor,” Proceedings of IEEE/ISSCC, San Francisco, CA,\n1000,June2021. pp.484-486,February2013.\nDownloaded\nfrom\nhttp://meridian.allenpress.com/jmep/article-pdf/18/4/145/2996507/i1551-4897-18-4-145.pdf\nby\nguest\non\n02\nNovember\n2022'}, {'source_filename': 'HeterogenousIntegration.txt', 'title': 'HeterogenousIntegration', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': 'HIEA: Heterogeneous Integration of Everything onto Anything\nCore faculty: Eric Pop and H.-S. Philip Wong (EE, co-leads), Jonathan Fan (EE), Kenneth\nGoodson (ME), Subhasish Mitra (EE), Yoshio Nishi (EE), James Plummer (EE), Krishna Saras-\nwat (EE), Debbie Senesky (AA), Xiaolin Zheng (ME)\nIt is apparent that if one goes by the technology node number, the semiconductor industry\nwill reach the size of the atom in a short number of years. However, device technology progress\nis not simply measured by how small a transistor is. New generations of technology will offer\nbenefits, such as energy efficiency, for exciting emerging applications via a variety of means.\nThe underlying device technology will become increasingly heterogeneous, with Si CMOS serv-\ning as a platform for the integration of a variety of application-specific, architecture-specific de-\nvice technologies. Industrial partners often do not have the breadth to tackle the full range from\nnanomaterials to systems.\nThe HIEA theme of SystemX comprises three (deliberately) overlapping areas of inquiry,\nwhich go beyond what individual companies can address: 1) 3D monolithic integration of logic\nand memory, including thermal challenges of such dense systems, 2) heterogeneous integration\nof novel materials on Si and on flexible platforms, and 3) autonomous sensors and energy har-\nvesting that enable processing of'}, {'source_filename': 'HeterogenousIntegration.txt', 'title': 'HeterogenousIntegration', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': 'D monolithic integration of logic\nand memory, including thermal challenges of such dense systems, 2) heterogeneous integration\nof novel materials on Si and on flexible platforms, and 3) autonomous sensors and energy har-\nvesting that enable processing of data in an energy-efficient fashion at the source.\n3D monolithic integration\nMonolithic 3D\nintegration of logic & Applications of Ge and III-Vs on Si Heterogeneous integration of\nmemory for energy- for MOSFETs, photonics and Ge and III-Vs on Si\nefficient computation photovoltaics (Saraswat) (Saraswat and Plummer)\n(Mitra, Wong)\nFully Integrated Thermal Monolithic integration\nManagement of 2D/3D Chips of AlGaN/GaN high\nArchitectural studies of (Goodson) X-\xad‐on-\xad‐Si electron mobility\nmonolithic 3D N/MEMS (Senesky)\nintegration benefits for\nenergy-efficient Electronic synaptic devices for\ncomputation (Mitra) brain-inspired computing (Wong) Integration of\nTransfer Printing\nAtomically Thin 2D\nMethods for\nwith 3D Materials and\nIntegrating Thin Film\nDevices (Pop)\nElectronics onto Any\nSubstrates (Zheng)\nSkin-like electrodes for X-\xad‐on-\xad‐flex\nlong-term\nelectrophysiological Flexible electronic\nmonitoring (Fan) devices\nautonomous sensors & energy (N'}, {'source_filename': 'HeterogenousIntegration.txt', 'title': 'HeterogenousIntegration', 'chunk_id': 2, 'start_token': 500, 'end_token': 800, 'text': 'Electronics onto Any\nSubstrates (Zheng)\nSkin-like electrodes for X-\xad‐on-\xad‐flex\nlong-term\nelectrophysiological Flexible electronic\nmonitoring (Fan) devices\nautonomous sensors & energy (Nishi)\nAutonomous wireless\nMid- & near-field Chip-integrated sensor nodes for harsh\nBody-Heat-Powered\nwireless power with thermoelectric power environments via\nAutonomous Wearable\nstretchable electronics for wireless sensor AlGaN/GaN\nElectronics (Pop)\n(Fan) networks (Goodson) heterostructures\n(Senesky)\n1\n1) Monolithic 3D Integration\nMemory will play a central role in the 21st century\ndata-centric computing environments. Developing new 3D RRAM\nMain memory,\nways to achieve high-bandwidth, low latency, low en-\nstorage\nergy memory access will take center stage. New tech-\n1D/2D FETs\nniques to store and retrieve data that are commensurate ALU & flip-flops,\nregister files,\nwith the system architecture will need to be explored.\ncache(L1)\nWong, Pop, and Mitra will create new generations of\nOn-chip heat spreading\nnanosystems that demonstrate 3D monolithic integra-\nSTTRAM\ntion of computation units and memory in a fine-grained\nmanner. They will develop device, fabrication, and de-\nsign technologies that'}, {'source_filename': 'HeterogenousIntegration.txt', 'title': 'HeterogenousIntegration', 'chunk_id': 3, 'start_token': 750, 'end_token': 1050, 'text': '-chip heat spreading\nnanosystems that demonstrate 3D monolithic integra-\nSTTRAM\ntion of computation units and memory in a fine-grained\nmanner. They will develop device, fabrication, and de-\nsign technologies that allow monolithic 3D integration\n1D/2D FETs\nof 1D carbon nanotube (CNT) and 2D transition metal\ndichalcogenide (TMD) transistors with memories such\nOn-chip heat spreading\nas STT-MRAM, RRAM, PCM, and CBRAM. Mitra, local ILVs, no TSVs process T < 250 oC\nPop, and Wong will create models and perform sys-\ntem-level analysis of new architectures (as well as create new architectures and corresponding\ndesign techniques) that capitalize on this tight integration of memory and logic with high band-\nwidth communication made possible by highly dense inter-level vias. Wong will further explore\ninnovative use of emerging memories for use in alternative computing architectures targeted for\nspecific tasks such as machine learning and classification.\nIn addition to the electronic research, Goodson will pursue a monolithic thermal platform\n(MTP) to address thermal challenges in dense 3D monolithic systems. The MTP will combine\nheat conduction solutions at nanoscale dimensions (e.g. using nanomaterials like graphene and\nBN, provided by Pop and Wong) with phase-change'}, {'source_filename': 'HeterogenousIntegration.txt', 'title': 'HeterogenousIntegration', 'chunk_id': 4, 'start_token': 1000, 'end_token': 1300, 'text': ' thermal challenges in dense 3D monolithic systems. The MTP will combine\nheat conduction solutions at nanoscale dimensions (e.g. using nanomaterials like graphene and\nBN, provided by Pop and Wong) with phase-change material (PCM) solutions at microscale di-\nmensions including the use of Cu nanomesh or nanowire heat exchangers with paraffin wax\nPCMs to suppress extreme temperature transients. Adding to the utility of the monolithic 3D sys-\ntems, Saraswat will explore the use of on-chip optical interconnects and on-chip detectors, la-\nsers and modulators, based on his pioneering work to induce a direct band gap in Ge via strain.\n2) Heterogeneous Integration of X-on-Si and X-on-Flex\nThe Plummer group has pioneered the use of microcrucibles to create high-quality crystal-\nline nanomaterials on Si for device applications. Example demonstrations to date include Ge,\nGaSb, InAs and GaAs, and future possible demonstrations include InSb, AlSb and InP, as long\nas the melting temperatures of the nanomaterial is lower than that of the Si substrate (~1400 C).\nPop and Wong will explore the challenges of material and device integration of TMDs like\nMoS with Si and flexible substrates. For instance, atomically thin 2D materials like MoS (<1\n'}, {'source_filename': 'HeterogenousIntegration.txt', 'title': 'HeterogenousIntegration', 'chunk_id': 5, 'start_token': 1250, 'end_token': 1550, 'text': ' Si substrate (~1400 C).\nPop and Wong will explore the challenges of material and device integration of TMDs like\nMoS with Si and flexible substrates. For instance, atomically thin 2D materials like MoS (<1\n2 2\nnm thickness) could enable transistor length scaling below the so-called 3 nm technology node.\nKey challenges for enabling such technology include the making of low-resistance contacts to\natomically thin layers, the science of which could be applicable to other materials as well. An-\nother challenge is the integration of high-quality high-K dielectrics with the 2D devices.\nAt the low end of the temperature spectrum, J. Fan plans to explore the integration of hard\nmaterials (e.g. metals, semiconductors or dielectrics) with soft substrates (e.g. elastomers) to\nconstruct multi-functional skin-like devices. Similar techniques can also lead to stretchable RF\nelectromagnetic systems, which can be used as components for antennas and can be integrated\n2\ninto skin-like power harvesters. Nishi\nThin film electronics\nplans to examine device and nano-\nmaterial integration on simple paper\n(cellulose) substrates. Cellulose is the\nmost abundant polymer in the world,\nwith good mechanical properties in\naddition to being renewable and biode-\ngradable. Devices comprised of P3HT\nor CNTs as well as their top gate die-\nlectrics can'}, {'source_filename': 'HeterogenousIntegration.txt', 'title': 'HeterogenousIntegration', 'chunk_id': 6, 'start_token': 1500, 'end_token': 1800, 'text': 'ose is the\nmost abundant polymer in the world,\nwith good mechanical properties in\naddition to being renewable and biode-\ngradable. Devices comprised of P3HT\nor CNTs as well as their top gate die-\nlectrics can be inkjet printed at room temperature and promising early results already exist.\nZheng will focus on transfer printing from rigid, expensive substrates onto flexible and cheap\nsubstrates. Important milestones already include cheap and light solar cells, as well as other “peel\nand stick” electronics achieved by transfer printing techniques. Future work will focus on ul-\ntrathin biosensors, flexible memory devices, and collaborations within SystemX to enable trans-\nferring atomically thin nanomaterials such as CNTs and TMDs onto flexible substrates.\n3) Autonomous Sensors and Energy Harvesting\nHeterogeneous system integration is complemented\nOptimization\nby the availability of sensors that can be monolithically\nContours\nintegrated with electronics. Senesky works on the\nnanofabrication of GaN heterostructures for M/NEMS\nsensors, and her work leverages a brand new MOCVD\ntool that was installed in Allen-X. Sensors are suitable\nfor harsh environment operation (e.g. high temperature,\nchemically corrosive, even radiation-rich environments)\nand can be integrated for temperature sensing with power\nelectronics modules. Powering such sensors and ubiqui-\ntous electronics in general, will'}, {'source_filename': 'HeterogenousIntegration.txt', 'title': 'HeterogenousIntegration', 'chunk_id': 7, 'start_token': 1750, 'end_token': 2050, 'text': '\nfor harsh environment operation (e.g. high temperature,\nchemically corrosive, even radiation-rich environments)\nand can be integrated for temperature sensing with power\nelectronics modules. Powering such sensors and ubiqui-\ntous electronics in general, will require thermoelectric\n(TE) energy scavenging from a variety of heat sources\nsuch as HVAC, water pipes, windows, allowing “truly wireless” sensors networks, e.g. in Smart\nBuildings. Goodson will focus on low-power (µW to mW) and low-temperature (ΔT = 5-10 K)\nTE conversion, which demand revolutionary new materials and designs, unlike traditional TE\npower modules that are tailored for high-power and higher-temperature harvesting. In addition,\nPop will pursue the integration of nanomaterials (like CNTs and TMDs) with flexible materials\n(like polymers) for the realization of flexible TE energy harvesters. Such new devices will opti-\nmize and exploit nanomaterial TE properties for energy harvesting from low-grade heat sources\nas common as the human body (e.g., calculations show up to 0.5 W could be captured from the\nbody heat of a single person, sufficient to power a large array of sensors and wearable, low-\npower electronics).\nIn summary, the HIEA SystemX theme recognizes that heterogeneous integration could enable\nextreme advances in electronics, much beyond the end of “traditional” Moore'}, {'source_filename': 'HeterogenousIntegration.txt', 'title': 'HeterogenousIntegration', 'chunk_id': 8, 'start_token': 2000, 'end_token': 2104, 'text': ' person, sufficient to power a large array of sensors and wearable, low-\npower electronics).\nIn summary, the HIEA SystemX theme recognizes that heterogeneous integration could enable\nextreme advances in electronics, much beyond the end of “traditional” Moore’s law. The HIEA\ntheme will pursue monolithically 3D integrated low-power logic and memory, thermal engineer-\ning, sensors and energy harvesters, and the heterogeneous integration of nanomaterials onto Si\nand unconventional, flexible platforms.\n3'}, {'source_filename': 'EDMS_TC_-_Chiplet_Technology_and_Heterogenous_Integration.txt', 'title': 'EDMS_TC_-_Chiplet_Technology_and_Heterogenous_Integration', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': '1\nChiplet Technology and H eterogeneous Integration\nWendemagegnehu T. Beyene, Senior Member, IEEE\nChiplets and heterogeneous integration are changing the design of modern electronic systems. Instead of only relying on\nprocess shrinks as the primary driver of product design and system performance, the heterogenous multi-chiplet architectures can\npotentially provide a much lower cost alternative to the latest design nodes. Packaging technology is poised to play a key role in\nthe performance of the next generation systems. The chiplet-based design can be built on various materials such as silicon, glass,\nand organic laminate. The resulting single-package-based integration allows multiple silicon dies of various technology and\ncomplexity to be integrated efficiently using next-level interconnects, such as silicon interposers and bridges.\nAs the systems evolve from single monolithic devices to multi-chiplet architectures, the electrical analysis become more critical\nto guarantee the performance of such heterogenous systems. The second-level interconnects provide a low-impedance power\ndelivery path between multiple independent power domains and short inter-die interconnects. The physical layer can either be\nparallel or serial interface trading power, latency and area or beachfront. Since these interconnects are short, the signal integrity\nmay not initially pose a challenge. However, elevated transient currents of multiple dies and their unique clocking architecture\nmake the supply noise, jitter, and latency the limiting factors in designing high-performance multi-d'}, {'source_filename': 'EDMS_TC_-_Chiplet_Technology_and_Heterogenous_Integration.txt', 'title': 'EDMS_TC_-_Chiplet_Technology_and_Heterogenous_Integration', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': ' interconnects are short, the signal integrity\nmay not initially pose a challenge. However, elevated transient currents of multiple dies and their unique clocking architecture\nmake the supply noise, jitter, and latency the limiting factors in designing high-performance multi-die heterogenous systems.\nIn this paper, chiplet packaging technology as well as the design and analysis of the heterogeneous systems are reviewed.\nChiplet integration, as a solution to the yield issues in larger\nI. INTRODUCTION chips, facilitates splitting the design and implementing sub-\nNE XT generation of electronic systems face multiple systems into separate smaller dies. Assuming a simple yield\nmodel that defects scatter randomly across a wafer, and that a\nchallenges to meet the demands for increased\ndefect anywhere on the die renders it unusable, a large die is\nperformance, lower power, higher bandwidth, smaller\nmuch more likely to contain a defect than a small die. For\nform factor, scalability and functionality owing to various\ninstance, a smaller die improves the yield per wafer for eight\napplications in the era of Artificial Intelligence (AI),\nbackground random defects, as shown in Figure 2.\nneuromorphic cognition, Internet-of-Things (IoT), wearable\ndevice, 5G and autonomous computing. These challenges are\nmore critical in low-power processors implemented in different\ntechnology process nodes supporting a wide range of data rates.\nThe current complex designs require adapting, as quickly as\npossible, to the most advanced process'}, {'source_filename': 'EDMS_TC_-_Chiplet_Technology_and_Heterogenous_Integration.txt', 'title': 'EDMS_TC_-_Chiplet_Technology_and_Heterogenous_Integration', 'chunk_id': 2, 'start_token': 500, 'end_token': 800, 'text': " 5G and autonomous computing. These challenges are\nmore critical in low-power processors implemented in different\ntechnology process nodes supporting a wide range of data rates.\nThe current complex designs require adapting, as quickly as\npossible, to the most advanced process nodes, high-levels\nintegration, and larger die sizes. However, due to Moore's law\nslowdown, the semiconductor industry can no longer scale as it\nruns through barriers to advance performance improvement.\nThus, the normalized cost for incremental technology node\nadvancement has recently been increasing on a faster pace as\nshown in Figure 1. The small geometries demand numerous\ncomplex process steps to manufacture the chips, leading to\nexpensive mask-sets for lithography. For example, the cost-per-\nyielded die doubles from 16 nm to 7 nm.\n𝑵𝒖𝒎𝒃𝒆𝒓 𝒐𝒇 𝒈𝒐𝒐𝒅 𝒅𝒊𝒆𝒔\nFigure 2: 𝒀𝒊𝒆𝒍𝒅 = ; (a) Wafer with larger dies\n𝑻𝒐𝒕𝒂𝒍 𝒏𝒖𝒎𝒃𝒆𝒓 𝒐𝒇 𝒅𝒊𝒆𝒔\n(yield ="}, {'source_filename': 'EDMS_TC_-_Chiplet_Technology_and_Heterogenous_Integration.txt', 'title': 'EDMS_TC_-_Chiplet_Technology_and_Heterogenous_Integration', 'chunk_id': 3, 'start_token': 750, 'end_token': 1050, 'text': '�𝒕𝒂𝒍 𝒏𝒖𝒎𝒃𝒆𝒓 𝒐𝒇 𝒅𝒊𝒆𝒔\n(yield = 50%) (b) wafers with smaller die (yield = 90.2%). Small dies\nproduce higher yields.\nAs a result, chiplet integration enables a large ASIC to be\npartitioned into multiple dies and then interconnected together\nwithin a package to build a heterogenous system. Figure 3\nshows a conceptual view of a chiplet-based system integration.\nEven though High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) is the first type\nof heterogenous integration that is being widely used, various\ndies from different process technologies can be integrated using\ndense parallel package interconnects to enable efficient\nFigure 1: Trends of normalized cost per yielded die for 45nm to 5 nm; communications between the chiplets.\n[Source: AMD].\nThis is based on the author’s work at Intel Corporation, San Jose, CA 95134,\nUSA (email: wendem@gmail.com).\n2\nRecognizing the key role of packaging in heterogonous\nintegration, the IEEE Electronics Packaging Society (EPS) has\nbeen working on a heterogeneous integration roadmap to guide\nand accelerate the projected needs and opportunities for\ninnovation [3].\nA conceptual drawing of the cross-section of a\nheter'}, {'source_filename': 'EDMS_TC_-_Chiplet_Technology_and_Heterogenous_Integration.txt', 'title': 'EDMS_TC_-_Chiplet_Technology_and_Heterogenous_Integration', 'chunk_id': 4, 'start_token': 1000, 'end_token': 1300, 'text': 'ous\nintegration, the IEEE Electronics Packaging Society (EPS) has\nbeen working on a heterogeneous integration roadmap to guide\nand accelerate the projected needs and opportunities for\ninnovation [3].\nA conceptual drawing of the cross-section of a\nheterogeneous system with organic or silicon interposer and\nEMIB, 2.5D/3D IC with ASIC/FPGA dies and HBM\ntechnology is illustrated in Figure 5. Most common\nimplementations of 3D system-in-package are designed and\nmanufactured with a large organic interposer (substrate) with\nFigure 3: A conceptual heterogenous system using an interposer for\nchiplet integration. fine-pitch and fine-line interconnections. Silicon interposer and\nEMIB are also used for high-end and high-performance\nII. PACKAGING TECHNOLOGY FOR HETEROGENEOUS systems.\nINTEGRATION\nThe physical implementation of die interconnect in chiplet-\nbased heterogenous systems depend on the chip packaging\ntechnology. Although there are many packaging options, the\nthree common implementations of heterogonous systems:\nFigure 5: Heterogenous integration using (a) EMIB technology. and\norganic substrate and silicon substrate with and without\n(b) organic or silicon interposer.\nThrough-Silicon Via (TSV), are reviewed [1]. The\nperformance, cost, and maturity of the packaging technology\nIII. III. SIGNAL AND POWER INTEGRITY OF CHIPLET'}, {'source_filename': 'EDMS_TC_-_Chiplet_Technology_and_Heterogenous_Integration.txt', 'title': 'EDMS_TC_-_Chiplet_Technology_and_Heterogenous_Integration', 'chunk_id': 5, 'start_token': 1250, 'end_token': 1550, 'text': ' without\n(b) organic or silicon interposer.\nThrough-Silicon Via (TSV), are reviewed [1]. The\nperformance, cost, and maturity of the packaging technology\nIII. III. SIGNAL AND POWER INTEGRITY OF CHIPLET\naffect the adoption of chaplets.\nINTERCONNECT\nOrganic and silicon interposer as well as Intel’s Embedded\nMulti-die Interconnect Bridge (EMIB) or other similar silicon The scale of interconnect dimensions achievable in a silicon\nbridges are among the most popular interconnecting solutions interposer is finer than what is realizable in organic packages\noften used to implement chip-to-chip communications [2]. and the signal conductor loss in organic substrate is\nThese three approaches have slightly different tradeoffs in significantly less than that in silicon interconnect. The\nterms of the density of the embedded metal routings and dimensions of interconnects used in silicon interposer or EMIB\npower/ground layers, presence, or absence of TSV, and overall is similar to that of silicon metallization, thus the resistive loss\ncost and performance. Depending on the\npackaging type, the density of the interconnects, the bandwidth,\npower and the latency requirements, there are a few interfaces\ncurrently used, as shown in Table 2. Almost all of these\ninterfaces are parallel because the power requirement and\nlatency of serial interfaces are generally higher due to the\nSerDes’s inherent serialization and deserialization. However,\nthe SerDes options should not be disregarded prior to analyzing\nthe trade-offs for the application of interest. CONCLUSIONS\nChiplet-based heterogenous integration has received\nwidespread attention as a solution to break the slowdown of\nMoore’s law.'}, {'source_filename': 'EDMS_TC_-_Chiplet_Technology_and_Heterogenous_Integration.txt', 'title': 'EDMS_TC_-_Chiplet_Technology_and_Heterogenous_Integration', 'chunk_id': 11, 'start_token': 2750, 'end_token': 3050, 'text': ' and\nVDD2.\nTable 2: Common chip-to-chip interfaces.\nIV. CONCLUSIONS\nChiplet-based heterogenous integration has received\nwidespread attention as a solution to break the slowdown of\nMoore’s law. Packaging technology plays a key role in\nimproving the performance of the next generation electronic\nsystems. The three commonly used packaging technologies,\norganic interposer, silicon interposer, and EMIB are analyzed.\nB. Power Distribution Network Even though the signal integrity analysis of the die-to-die\nPower distribution network (PDN) of heterogenous integrated interconnect, for most applications, may not be complex, the\nsystems can be significantly different from the traditional jitter and power integrity require careful analysis to mitigate the\nelectronic systems as they include the interposer or EMIB impact of large current consumption and charge sharing among\npower and ground routings that connect to the PDN of the rest the many chiplets. It have been provided by Georgia Institute of Technology [108-\nconsists of a six-core graphics processor unit (GPU), two dual- 110], where they reported a differential Si complementary\ncore central processing unit (CPUs), two blocks of static metal-oxide semiconductor (CMOS) receiver IC (operating at 1\nFig. 1. SoC platforms for the A10, A11, and A12 APs.\nDownloaded\nfrom\nhttp://meridian.allenpress.com/jmep/article-pdf/16/2/45/2365359/imaps_780287.pdf\nby\nguest\non\n20\nJune\n2025\nLau: Recent Advances and Trends in Heterogeneous Integrations 47\nGbps)integratedwithalarge-areathin'}, {'source_filename': 'Recent Advances and Trends in Heterogeneous Integrations.txt', 'title': 'Recent Advances and Trends in Heterogeneous Integrations', 'chunk_id': 8, 'start_token': 2000, 'end_token': 2300, 'text': '5359/imaps_780287.pdf\nby\nguest\non\n20\nJune\n2025\nLau: Recent Advances and Trends in Heterogeneous Integrations 47\nGbps)integratedwithalarge-areathin-filmInGaAs/InPI-metal-\nsemiconductor-metal (MSM) photodetector (Fig. 2). Today,\nmost heterogeneous integrations focus on higher density, finer\npitch, and more complex system.\nA. Heterogeneous Integration Versus System-On-Chip\nWhy is the heterogeneous integration of such great interest?\nOne of the key reasons is because the end of Moore’s law is fast\napproaching and it is more and more difficult and costly to\nreduce the feature size (to do the scaling) to make SoCs.\nHeterogeneous integration contrasts with SoCs as follows. The\nheterogeneous integration uses packaging technology to in-\ntegrate dissimilar chips (either side-by-side or stack) or com-\nponents with different materials and functions, and from\ndifferent fablesshouses,foundries, wafer sizes, and feature sizes\n(as shown in Fig. 3) into a system or subsystem on different\n(e.g., organic, silicon, or redistribution layer [RDL]) substrates\nor stand alone, rather than integrating most of the functions into\na single chip and going for a finer feature size.\nB. Advantages of Heterogeneous Integration\nFig.'}, {'source_filename': 'Recent Advances and Trends in Heterogeneous Integrations.txt', 'title': 'Recent Advances and Trends in Heterogeneous Integrations', 'chunk_id': 9, 'start_token': 2250, 'end_token': 2550, 'text': ' organic, silicon, or redistribution layer [RDL]) substrates\nor stand alone, rather than integrating most of the functions into\na single chip and going for a finer feature size.\nB. Advantages of Heterogeneous Integration\nFig. 2. InGaAs/InP I-MSM integrated onto differential Si CMOS receiver IC.\nFor the next few years, we will see more of a higher level of\nheterogeneous integrations, whether it is for time-to-market,\nperformance, form factor, power consumption, signal integrity,\nor cost. Heterogeneous integration is going to take some of the\nFig. 3. The 3D HBM die stack with a size of\nFig. 5b shows the 3 SiPs in iPhone XS and XS Max as- 5.5 3 7.7 3.48 mm includes one base buffer die and four\nsembled by Foxconn. The rear SiP is a single-side PCB which DRAM core dice, which are interconnected with TSVs and fine-\nconsists of the baseband chipset, PMIC, etc. The large SiP on top pitch micro pillars with solder caps. Thesolder ball size is200 mmand\nthe ball pitch is.4 mm.\nHETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATIONS ON FAN-OUT\nREDISTRIBUTION LAYER SUBSTRATES\nRecently, to lower the package profile, enhance the perfor-\nmance, and lower the cost, the heterogeneous integration on\nRDLs by fan-out have been very popular, especially with the\nFOWLP technology. In general, this is for middle-end to high-\nend applications. Fig. 16. STATSChipPAC’s Fan-Out Flip Chip-Embedded Wafer Fig. 27; the technology is called fan-out wafer-level chip-on-\nLevel Ball Grid Array (eWLB) substrate (FOCoS). It is and the TSVs are not filled.\nDownloaded\nfrom\nhttp://meridian.allenpress.com/jmep/article-pdf/16/2/45/2365359/imaps_780287.pdf\nby\nguest\non\n20\nJune\n2025\n70 Journal of Microelectronics and Electronic Packaging, Vol. 16, No. 2, 2nd Qtr 2019\nmounted onthe serializerchip'}, {'source_filename': 'Recent Advances and Trends in Heterogeneous Integrations.txt', 'title': 'Recent Advances and Trends in Heterogeneous Integrations', 'chunk_id': 55, 'start_token': 13750, 'end_token': 14050, 'text': '780287.pdf\nby\nguest\non\n20\nJune\n2025\n70 Journal of Microelectronics and Electronic Packaging, Vol. 16, No. 2, 2nd Qtr 2019\nmounted onthe serializerchipwhile itisin awafer format. After\ndicing the 3D hybrid IC chipset, it is placed on the PCB (or\nsubstrate) ontopof thepolymerwaveguide. Special encapsulant\n(underfill) such as the transparent polymer may be needed to\nprotect the chipset. If it is needed, a heat slug or any conductive\nmaterial can be attached to the backside of the serializer chip by\na thermal interface material (TIM). Again, if it is needed, a heat\nspreader can be attached to the top side of the heat slug with a\nTIM. Similarly, the photodetector chip is solder-bumped flip\nchip on the transimpedance amplifier (TIA) chip, which is then\nsolder-bumped flip chip on the deserializer chip. The thermal\nmanagement and encapsulant techniques are the same as those\nof the VCSEL chipset.\nSUMMARY AND RECOMMENDATIONS\nSome important results and recommendations are as follows:\n1. Heterogeneous integration is defined as using packaging\ntechnology to integrate dissimilar chips, photonic devices,\nor components (either side-by-side, stack, or both) with\ndifferent materials and functions, and'}, {'source_filename': 'Recent Advances and Trends in Heterogeneous Integrations.txt', 'title': 'Recent Advances and Trends in Heterogeneous Integrations', 'chunk_id': 56, 'start_token': 14000, 'end_token': 14300, 'text': ' and recommendations are as follows:\n1. Heterogeneous integration is defined as using packaging\ntechnology to integrate dissimilar chips, photonic devices,\nor components (either side-by-side, stack, or both) with\ndifferent materials and functions, and from different fabless\nhouses foundries, wafer sizes, feature sizes and companies\ninto a system or subsystem on different substrates or stand\nalone.\n2. Heterogeneous integrations is classified as (1) heteroge-\nneous integrations on organic substrates, (2) heterogeneous\nintegrations on silicon substrates (TSV interposers), (3)\nFig. 48. IZM’s heterogeneous integration of MEMS on TSV interposer. heterogeneous integrations on silicon substrates (bridges),\n(4) heterogeneous integrations on fan-out RDL substrates,\nand (5) heterogeneous integrations on ceramic substrates.\nHETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION OF VERTICAL-CAVITY 3. Seventy-five percent of the heterogeneous integrations will\nSURFACE-EMITTING LASER be on organic substrates (actually most are SiPs). Twenty-\nfive percent of the heterogeneous integration will be on other\nA. The complexity to date. CS materials such as GaN and InP\nDiverse Accessible Heterogeneous Integration (DAHI) benefit greatly from leveraging novel silicon-enabled circuit\nprogram has developed heterogeneous integration or system architectures to enhance the performance of\nprocesses to intimately combine advanced compound advanced CS-based RF/mixed-signal circuits.\nsemiconductor (CS) devices and other emerging Given these trends, it is our view that the future of mm'}, {'source_filename': '15.1.txt', 'title': '15.1', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': ' heterogeneous integration or system architectures to enhance the performance of\nprocesses to intimately combine advanced compound advanced CS-based RF/mixed-signal circuits.\nsemiconductor (CS) devices and other emerging Given these trends, it is our view that the future of mm-\nmaterials and devices with high-density silicon CMOS wave electronics depends on heterogeneous integration of\ntechnology. The that will allow the advantages of multiple technology types\nDAHI program has facilitated access to this process to be optimally combined.\nthrough establishment of an integration foundry. In\naddition to establishing this foundry capability for chip-\nscale integration, the DAHI program has developed a\nnumber of other integration approaches.\nINTRODUCTION\nThe compound semiconductor (CS) electronics industry\nis well-positioned to address the analog challenges of future\nRF and mm-wave systems, due to the superior properties\nand diversity of CS materials. For example, the high electron\nmobility and peak velocity of InP-based material systems\nhave resulted in transistors with fmax above 1THz [1], as\nwell as ultra-high-speed mixed-signal circuits (see, for\nexample, [2]). The wide energy bandgap of GaN has enabled\nlarge voltage swings as well as high breakdown voltage RF\npower devices [3]. The excellent thermal conductivity of SiC\nFig. 1. This designs for processing.\napproach confers a number of important advantages for the\nestablishment of a diverse, accessible foundry technology.\nBy conducting the heterogeneous integration after standard\nback-end-of-line (BEOL) processing, no process changes are\nrequired for'}, {'source_filename': '15.1.txt', 'title': '15.1', 'chunk_id': 5, 'start_token': 1250, 'end_token': 1550, 'text': ' processing.\napproach confers a number of important advantages for the\nestablishment of a diverse, accessible foundry technology.\nBy conducting the heterogeneous integration after standard\nback-end-of-line (BEOL) processing, no process changes are\nrequired for the Si base or CS technologies being integrated.\nThis allows rapid introduction of new process technologies\nwithin the foundry. Other approaches, in which\nheterogeneous integration occurs prior to or during BEOL\nprocessing, require significant levels of process change,\nFig. 3.
