Scott, et al., Diverse Accessible Heterogeneous\nproperty, through the development of standardized Integration (DAHI) Foundry Establishment at Northrop\nintegrated circuit chiplets that can be rapidly reconfigured to Grumman Aerospace Systems (NGAS), Proc.'}, {'source_filename': '15.1.txt', 'title': '15.1', 'chunk_id': 11, 'start_token': 2750, 'end_token': 3050, 'text': ' Diverse Accessible Heterogeneous\nproperty, through the development of standardized Integration (DAHI) Foundry Establishment at Northrop\nintegrated circuit chiplets that can be rapidly reconfigured to Grumman Aerospace Systems (NGAS), Proc. IPRM,\nmeet emerging mm-wave integrated circuit needs. It is Santa Barbara, 2015.\n[13] Y.-C. Wu, et al., InP HBT/GaN HEMT/Si CMOS\nHeterogeneous Integrated Q-Band VCO-Amplifier\nChain, Proc. IPRM, Santa Barbara, 2015.\n[14] A. Carter, et al., Wafer-Scale InP/Si CMOS 3D\nIntegration Using Low-Temperature Oxide Bonding,\nCompound Semiconductor Week, 2015.\n[15] F. Herrault, et al., Metal Embedded Chip Assembly\nTechnology, GOMACTech, 2017.\nACRONYMS\nBEOL: Back end of line\nCMOS: Complementary metal oxide semiconductor\nCOSMOS: Compound Semiconductor Materials on\nSilicon\nCS: Compound semiconductor\nDAHI: Diverse Accessible Heterogeneous Integration\nDARPA: Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency\nDBI: Direct bond interconnect\nDoD: Department of Defense\nEDA: Electronic design automation\nFOM: Figure of merit\nHBT: Heterojunction bipolar transistor\nHEMT: High electron mobility'}, {'source_filename': '15.1.txt', 'title': '15.1', 'chunk_id': 12, 'start_token': 3000, 'end_token': 3094, 'text': ' Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency\nDBI: Direct bond interconnect\nDoD: Department of Defense\nEDA: Electronic design automation\nFOM: Figure of merit\nHBT: Heterojunction bipolar transistor\nHEMT: High electron mobility transistor\nMECA: Metal embedded chip assembly\nMEMS: Micro electromechanical systems\nPDK: Process design kit\nRF: Radio frequency\nTSV: Through silicon via\nVCO: Voltage-controlled oscillator'}, {'source_filename': '[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once _ TrendForce News.html', 'title': '[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': '# [News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once\n[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once\nThe semiconductor industry enters the era of integration. Various foundries are focusing on advanced packaging technologies, but the terminology surrounding advanced packaging can be daunting. This article aims to explain these terms in the simplest way possible.\nAccording to a report from\nTechNews (https://technews.tw/2024/03/18/semiconductor-advanced-packaging/)\n, currently, there are two main trends in advanced packaging: heterogeneous integration and chiplets.\nIn fact, the concept of “heterogeneous integration” has been developing for many years and is not exclusive to advanced packaging. It is not only used for the integration of heterogeneous chiplets but also for integrating other non-chip active/passive components into a single package, which is the technology commonly used in traditional Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test Services(OSATs).\nHeterogeneous Integration = Big Building Blocks? Advanced Packaging = Small Building Blocks?\nIn the simplest terms, “heterogeneous integration” can be likened to building with large building blocks, while “advanced packaging” is akin to assembling with small building blocks. Some manufacturers, like traditional Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test Services(OSATs), excel in stacking large blocks, such as logic circuits, radio frequency circuits, MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mech'}, {'source_filename': '[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once _ TrendForce News.html', 'title': '[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': '” is akin to assembling with small building blocks. Some manufacturers, like traditional Outsourced Semiconductor Assembly and Test Services(OSATs), excel in stacking large blocks, such as logic circuits, radio frequency circuits, MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems), or sensors, onto a IC substrate. The stacking of these different large blocks represents the concept of heterogeneous integration.\nOn the other hand, some blocks are too small to stack effectively, requiring assistance from advanced packaging, typically provided by semiconductor foundries.\nAdvanced packaging also encompasses 2.5D packaging and 3D packaging. Using the metaphor of building blocks, the former involves horizontally stacking small building blocks on a interposer, while the latter involves vertically stacking small building blocks with interconnection facilitated through Through-Silicon Vias (TSVs), which are ultra-small building blocks.\nIt’s important to emphasize that stacking blocks is a conceptual representation, and the distinction between large and small blocks is relative. The analogy above refers to heterogeneous integration in traditional packaging, and heterogeneous integration in advanced packaging follows a similar concept, but with even smaller building blocks.\nSoC / SiP / Heterogeneous Integration / Chiplet\nWith this concept in mind, let’s discuss the applications of heterogeneous integration in advanced packaging:\nAmong the various packaging types, SoC (System On Chip) involves integrating different chips such as processors and memory, with different functions, redesigned and fabricated using the “same process,” integrated onto a single chip, resulting in a final'}, {'source_filename': '[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once _ TrendForce News.html', 'title': '[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once', 'chunk_id': 2, 'start_token': 500, 'end_token': 800, 'text': ' in advanced packaging:\nAmong the various packaging types, SoC (System On Chip) involves integrating different chips such as processors and memory, with different functions, redesigned and fabricated using the “same process,” integrated onto a single chip, resulting in a final product with only one chip.\nOn the other hand, SiP (System in Package) involves connecting multiple chips with “different processes” through “heterogeneous integration” technology, integrated within the same packaging module. Therefore, the final product will be a system with many chips on it, resembling the stacking of different-sized building blocks mentioned earlier.\nTherefore, heterogeneous integration refers to integrating different and separately manufactured components (heterogeneous) into higher-level assemblies. These components include blocks of different sizes, such as MEMS devices, passive components, logic chips, and more.\nHowever, at a certain point, for the sake of process development, researchers found that separating components at the right time might facilitate miniaturization. Hence, chiplet was born.\nIs Chiplet the Fusion of Heterogeneous Integration and Advanced Processes?\nAs demands for ICs become increasingly complex, the size of SoC chips continues to grow. However, cramming too many components onto a limited substrate poses significant challenges, including heightened process complexity and reduced yield.\nHence, the concept of chiplets emerged, advocating for the segmentation of SoC functionalities, such as data storage, computation, signal processing, and data flow management, into smaller individual chips. These chiplets are then'}, {'source_filename': '[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once _ TrendForce News.html', 'title': '[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once', 'chunk_id': 3, 'start_token': 750, 'end_token': 1050, 'text': ' process complexity and reduced yield.\nHence, the concept of chiplets emerged, advocating for the segmentation of SoC functionalities, such as data storage, computation, signal processing, and data flow management, into smaller individual chips. These chiplets are then integrated through packaging to form a\xa0interconnected network.\nIt’s worth noting that Chiplets are essentially chips, whereas SiP refers to the packaging format. Chiplet architecture enable the reduction of individual chip sizes, simplify circuit design, overcome manufacturing difficulties and yield issues, and offer greater design flexibility.\nAdvanced Packaging Technologies in Foundries\nCurrently, advanced packaging can be broadly categorized into three main types: Wafer-Level Packaging (WLP), 2.5D Packaging, and 3D Packaging. Traditional packaging involves cutting wafers into chips before packaging, while advanced packaging entails packaging the silicon wafer before cutting, requiring subsequent stacking processes in fabs. Therefore, the technology is primarily the responsibility of fabs.\nTraditional packaging involves cutting wafers into chips before packaging. Advanced packaging, starting from wafer-level packaging, involves packaging silicon wafers before cutting, and subsequent stacking requires wafer fabrication processes.\nTherefore, this article will delve into advanced packaging technologies offered by the three major foundries, with a focus on 2.5D and 3D packaging.\nTo further explain using building blocks, the difference between 2.5D and 3DIC packaging lies in the “stacking method.”\nIn 2.5D packaging'}, {'source_filename': '[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once _ TrendForce News.html', 'title': '[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once', 'chunk_id': 4, 'start_token': 1000, 'end_token': 1300, 'text': ' with a focus on 2.5D and 3D packaging.\nTo further explain using building blocks, the difference between 2.5D and 3DIC packaging lies in the “stacking method.”\nIn 2.5D packaging, processors, memory, or other chips are stacked horizontally on a silicon interposer using a flip-chip method, with micro bumps connecting different chip’s electronic signals. Through silicon vias (TSVs) in the interposer link to the metal bumps below, then packaged onto the IC\xa0substrate, creating tighter interconnections between the chips and the substrate.\nIn a side view, although the chips are stacked, the essence remains horizontal packaging, with the chips positioned closer together and allowing for smaller chip sizes. Additionally, this is a form of “heterogeneous integration” technology.\n3D packaging involves stacking multiple chips (face down) together, directly using through-silicon vias to stack them vertically, linking the electronic signals of different chips above and below, achieving true vertical packaging. Currently, more and more CPUs, GPUs, and memories are starting to adopt 3D packaging technology.\nHybrid bonding is one of the die bonding techniques used in advanced chip packaging processes. One of the commercially available technologies in this domain is the “Cu-Cu hybrid bonding.”\nIn traditional wafer bonding processes, there are interfaces between copper and dielectric materials. With “Cu-Cu hybrid bonding,” metal contacts are embedded within the dielectric material.'}, {'source_filename': '[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once _ TrendForce News.html', 'title': '[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once', 'chunk_id': 5, 'start_token': 1250, 'end_token': 1550, 'text': ' commercially available technologies in this domain is the “Cu-Cu hybrid bonding.”\nIn traditional wafer bonding processes, there are interfaces between copper and dielectric materials. With “Cu-Cu hybrid bonding,” metal contacts are embedded within the dielectric material. Through a thermal treatment process, these two materials are bonded together, utilizing the atomic diffusion of copper metal in its solid-state to achieve the bond. This approach addresses challenges encountered in previous\nflip-chip\xa0bonding process.\nCompared to\xa0flip-chip bonding, hybrid bonding offers several advantages. It allows for achieving ultra-high I/O counts and longer interconnect lengths. By using dielectric material for bonding instead of bottom fillers, the cost of filling is eliminated.\nAdditionally, hybrid bonding results in minimal thickness compared to chip-on-wafer bonding. This is particularly beneficial for future developments in 3D packaging, where stacking multiple layers of chips is required, as hybrid bonding can significantly reduce the overall thickness.\nAdvanced Packaging Moves Towards the Era of Heterogeneous Integration\nAs the semiconductor industry enters the “post-Moore’s Law era,” the development focus of advanced packaging is gradually shifting from 2D planar structures to 3D stacking and from single-chip designs to multi-chip configurations. Therefore, “heterogeneous integration” will play a crucial role in future advanced packaging.\nCurrently, prominent companies such as TSMC, Samsung, and Intel are intensifying their research and development efforts and capacity expansions in this field'}, {'source_filename': '[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once _ TrendForce News.html', 'title': '[News] Understanding 3DIC, Heterogeneous Integration, SiP, and Chiplets at Once', 'chunk_id': 6, 'start_token': 1500, 'end_token': 1653, 'text': '-chip configurations. Therefore, “heterogeneous integration” will play a crucial role in future advanced packaging.\nCurrently, prominent companies such as TSMC, Samsung, and Intel are intensifying their research and development efforts and capacity expansions in this field, introducing their innovative packaging solutions.\nWith ongoing technological advancements and innovations, advanced packaging and heterogeneous integration will play increasingly vital roles in propelling the semiconductor industry towards greater heights, meeting the complex and diverse demands of future electronic devices.\nRead more\n(Photo credit: Intel)\nPlease note that this article cites information from\nTechNews (https://technews.tw/2024/03/18/semiconductor-advanced-packaging/)\n.\n(https://www.trendforce.com/landingPage/selected_topics_semiconductor)'}, {'source_filename': 'wp06-05a-en-white-paper-advance-packaging-preview.txt', 'title': 'wp06-05a-en-white-paper-advance-packaging-preview', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': 'ADVANCED PACKAGING AND HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION BY SENSOFAR | 1\nAdvanced packaging\nand heterogeneous integration\nOptical metrology solutions\nfor next-generation semiconductors\nADVANCED PACKAGING AND HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION BY SENSOFAR | 2\nINTRODUCTION 3\nMore Moore vs More than Moore 4\nApplications and markets 6\nIntegration strategies and challenges 7\nADVANCED PACKAGING 9\nThrough silicon vias 11\nMicrobumps 12\nWafer glue 13\nRedistribution layers 14\nFEOL and BEOL 15\nHETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION 16\nCPO Waveguides 18\nMicrolenses 19\nNew materials 20\nMEMS 21\nPCB applications 22\nCONCLUSIONS 23\nADVANCED PACKAGING AND HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION BY SENSOFAR | 9\nADVANCED\nPACKAGING\nAdvanced packaging techniques vary in complexity and\ndesign, each offering unique advantages for improving\nperformance, integration density, and energy efficiency.\n2.5D integration enhances connectivity by placing\nmultiple dies side by side on a silicon or organic\ninterposer, which serves as a high-density routing\nlayer. This approach reduces power consumption\nand increases bandwidth, making it ideal for High\nBandwidth Memory (HBM) integration with GPUs\nand AI'}, {'source_filename': 'wp06-05a-en-white-paper-advance-packaging-preview.txt', 'title': 'wp06-05a-en-white-paper-advance-packaging-preview', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': ' by side on a silicon or organic\ninterposer, which serves as a high-density routing\nlayer. This approach reduces power consumption\nand increases bandwidth, making it ideal for High\nBandwidth Memory (HBM) integration with GPUs\nand AI accelerators. One of the most famous 2.5D\nintegrations is Chip-on-Wafer-on-Substrate (CoWoS®).\nFor even greater integration, 3D packaging vertically\nstacks multiple dies, interconnecting them through\nThrough-Silicon Vias (TSV)—microscopic vertical\nchannels that allow direct electrical communication\nbetween layers. This architecture significantly reduces\ndata transfer distances, improving speed and efficiency\nwhile minimizing power loss. 3D integration is widely\nadopted in high-density memory solutions like 3D\nNAND flash and advanced processor architectures like\nIntel’s Foveros technology.\nADVANCED PACKAGING AND HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION BY SENSOFAR | 10\nMemory\nMicro bumps and hybrid bonding are crucial enablers Interposer\nof both 2.5D and 3D integration, two ways to create\nvery high-density wafer-to-wafer or die-to-wafer\ninterconnects.\nBeyond 2.5D and 3D integration, wafer-level and panel-\nlevel packaging have emerged as key strategies for\nimproving efficiency, yield, and scalability in advanced\nsemiconductor manufacturing.'}, {'source_filename': 'wp06-05a-en-white-paper-advance-packaging-preview.txt', 'title': 'wp06-05a-en-white-paper-advance-packaging-preview', 'chunk_id': 2, 'start_token': 500, 'end_token': 800, 'text': 'afer\ninterconnects.\nBeyond 2.5D and 3D integration, wafer-level and panel-\nlevel packaging have emerged as key strategies for\nimproving efficiency, yield, and scalability in advanced\nsemiconductor manufacturing. Wafer-level packaging\n(WLP) processes entire wafers before singulation, Substrate Logic\nenabling finer interconnect pitches and reducing\npackage size. Fan-Out Wafer-Level Packaging (FOWLP)\nenhances this by expanding interconnects beyond\nthe die footprint, improving thermal management\nand I/O density, making it ideal for mobile and high- High Bandwidth\nperformance applications. Fan-Out Panel-Level Logic memory (HBM)\nPackaging (FOPLP) further increases production\nefficiency by processing chips on larger rectangular\npanels, lowering costs and material waste, particularly\nin high-volume applications like automotive electronics.\nThese advanced packaging methods introduce\nsignificant metrology challenges, requiring precise\nmeasurements of TSV depths, microbump heights,\ninterconnect alignment, and bonding surface\nroughness. High-resolution 3D optical metrology plays\na critical role in ensuring process accuracy, optimizing\nperformance, and minimizing defects in next-\ngeneration semiconductor packaging.\nADVANCED PACKAGING AND HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION BY SENSOFAR | 11\n5X 10X Inte2rfer0omXetry 50X 100X\nCSI\nThrough\nSilicon V'}, {'source_filename': 'wp06-05a-en-white-paper-advance-packaging-preview.txt', 'title': 'wp06-05a-en-white-paper-advance-packaging-preview', 'chunk_id': 3, 'start_token': 750, 'end_token': 905, 'text': 'ANCED PACKAGING AND HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION BY SENSOFAR | 11\n5X 10X Inte2rfer0omXetry 50X 100X\nCSI\nThrough\nSilicon Vias\nMicrobumps Acquisition\nAnalysis\nHole\nTHROUGH SILICON VIAS\nOne key parameter to characterize these advanced\nsemiconductor architectures is the depth of the\ninterposer layer. This layer is designed to be as thin\nas possible to minimize the space it occupies. The\n17\nchallenge lies in accurately measuring through these\nsmall but deep holes, which can be measured using the\nS neox and Interferometry with low magnification.\nUnlock the complete\nWhite paper\nby signing up to'}, {'source_filename': 'Blog_ Heterogenous Integration Is Advancing Medical Devices.html', 'title': 'Heterogenous Integration Is Advancing Medical Devices', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': 'As medical treatments continue to evolve, innovation in medical devices plays a vital role, enabling increasingly complex, precise, and safer interventions. For example:\nEndoscopic devices provide high-resolution\nin vivo\nimaging,\nWearable sensors offer real-time monitoring of vital signs,\nImplantable devices engage with the human body at the cellular level.\nThese devices’ ongoing advancements in reliability, accuracy, and miniaturization help to meet the ever-growing demands of modern healthcare.\nHowever, successfully designing and manufacturing these devices presents significant challenges, especially for assembling complex sensors and microelectromechanical systems (MEMS) – a specific technology used to create miniaturized sensors and actuators by integrating mechanical components with electronic circuits on a single chip. Heterogeneous Integration (HI) has emerged as a transformative solution for these assemblies.\nHeterogeneous integration starts with the full wafer (left) containing many individual dies (right). Each die then requires packaging to create the electrical and mechanical interconnections needed to be functional.\nHeterogeneous Integration: A Revolutionary Approach to Microelectronics Assembly\nSimply stated, HI is the process of assembling diverse electronic and non-electronic components into a single, compact device. This methodology, around which an entire industry roadmap has been built, emerged from three key technological advancements:\nSemiconductor innovations\n: New materials and advanced microfabrication techniques have enabled creation of smaller, more efficient components with enhanced capabilities.\nGlobal supply chains\n: Improvements in manufacturing and supply chain systems have made high-performance'}, {'source_filename': 'Blog_ Heterogenous Integration Is Advancing Medical Devices.html', 'title': 'Heterogenous Integration Is Advancing Medical Devices', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': ' emerged from three key technological advancements:\nSemiconductor innovations\n: New materials and advanced microfabrication techniques have enabled creation of smaller, more efficient components with enhanced capabilities.\nGlobal supply chains\n: Improvements in manufacturing and supply chain systems have made high-performance components more accessible than ever before.\nAdvanced integration techniques\n: Wafer-level packaging, 3D integration, system-in-package (SiP), and other technologies have facilitated the creation of compact, high-performance devices.\nThese trends have paved the way for HI, which allows the integration of smaller, lighter, and more power-efficient components—such as sensors and MEMS—into devices that are computationally powerful but non-intrusive in size. Additionally, HI leads to more durable devices, as fewer external electrical and mechanical connections reduce the likelihood of failure.\nOvercoming Design and Manufacturing Challenges of HI Sensors and MEMS\nDespite its potential, HI presents unique challenges, particularly when integrating new components. While adding a sensor may enhance a device’s performance, it could also increase power consumption or complicate the assembly process. Moreover, to reach mass production, a device’s design must not only be optimized for functionality but also be scalable.\nFor a successful design, a thorough and structured development process is essential. Every aspect of the device—from design and component selection to manufacturing processes—should be considered holistically from the outset. This is where collaborating with the right Electronics Manufacturing Services (EMS) provider is crucial.\nA qualified EMS partner brings critical expertise in managing complex'}, {'source_filename': 'Blog_ Heterogenous Integration Is Advancing Medical Devices.html', 'title': 'Heterogenous Integration Is Advancing Medical Devices', 'chunk_id': 2, 'start_token': 500, 'end_token': 771, 'text': ' Every aspect of the device—from design and component selection to manufacturing processes—should be considered holistically from the outset. This is where collaborating with the right Electronics Manufacturing Services (EMS) provider is crucial.\nA qualified EMS partner brings critical expertise in managing complex integration projects. When evaluating EMS providers, it’s essential to look for credentials such as ISO 13485 certification, which ensures robust quality and risk management systems. Additionally, providers should have strong validation procedures, comprehensive operator training, and traceability for tools and parts.\nAt Promex, we combine industry-leading certifications with a proven process for transforming ideas into tangible products. We are ISO 9001:2016 and ISO 13485:2015 certified, and ITAR registered. Our engineers and designers leverage decades of experience and follow our Phase Gate Process, a structured approach that guides the design and manufacturing process from concept to production. This process defines manufacturing maturity at each stage, which enhances project predictability, reduces costs, and ensures that device method of manufacturing can be efficiently scaled for mass production.\nTo see the Phase Gate Process in action and learn how we have helped customers develop Lab-on-a-Chip (LoaC) devices – an impactful innovation in medical diagnostics –\nclick here (https://promex-ind.com/sensors-mems-expertise/)\nto download and explore our new white paper.'}, {'source_filename': 'Blog_ Heterogenous Integration Is Advancing Medical Devices.html', 'title': 'Heterogenous Integration Is Advancing Medical Devices', 'chunk_id': 3, 'start_token': 750, 'end_token': 771, 'text': 'promex-ind.com/sensors-mems-expertise/)\nto download and explore our new white paper.'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': 'WHITEPAPER FEBRUARY 2023\nHETEROGENEOUS\nINTEGRATION - CHIPLETS\nGLOBAL SEMICONDUCTOR ALLIANCE INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY INTEREST GROUP\nCONTENTS\n1. INTRODUCTION\n2. COMMERCIAL CHALLENGES\nBUSINESS\nTECHNICAL\n3. INTERFACE CHALLENGES\n4. D2D INTERFACE TYPES\nSERIAL VS. PARALLEL\nPROPRIETARY VS. OPEN D2D STANDARDS\nHOW TO SELECT THE “RIGHT” D2D INTERFACE?\n5. PACKAGING INNOVATIONS FOR CHIPLETS\n6. CHIPLET SECURITY CHALLENGES\nPROVING AND SECURING AUTHENTICITY\nSILICON-BASED COUNTERMEASURES\nSUPPLY CHAIN SECURITY & PROVISIONING\n7. RECOMMENDATIONS\n8. REFERENCES\n9. CONTRIBUTORS\n1. INTRODUCTION\nChiplets are a hot topic in the semiconductor industry, and to many represent a paradigm change for chip\ndesigners and chip consumers alike. Rather than a traditional single piece of monolithic silicon with many functions\nand features, a chiplet contains one or a very limited set of functions and features. Multiple chiplets are then\nassembled into a MCM (multi-chiplet module).\nChiplets can offer multiple advantages over monolithic silicon, including:\n• Chiplets can be designed in the optimum process node (performance, power, cost)'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': ' Multiple chiplets are then\nassembled into a MCM (multi-chiplet module).\nChiplets can offer multiple advantages over monolithic silicon, including:\n• Chiplets can be designed in the optimum process node (performance, power, cost) for the particular function\nand/or feature\n• Size and yield – as monolithic silicon reaches reticle limitations, chiplets are much easier and cheaper to\nmanufacture\n• The ability to manufacture with and integrate diverse and potentially incompatible semiconductor materials,\nsuch as GaN, SiC, Ga2O3, and others\n• Chiplets can be re-used over multiple products and projects\n• Enables choice: allows chipmakers/OEMs to select the right combination of chiplets for their needs, rather\nthan be forced to choose a potentially less-optimum monolithic chip\n• Lower NRE (non-recurring engineering) costs for chiplet designs, potentially enabling smaller volume\napplications\n• Lowers the entry barrier to designing and selling silicon\n• Allows semiconductor IP to be sold in silicon form\n• Quickens time to market\nHomogenous chiplet MCMs, or MCMs containing chiplets from only a single manufacturer, have been mass-market\ndeployed – an example would be the AMD Ryzen Zen 3 processors. Homogenous chiplets share many of the\nadvantages mentioned above – however, as they remain the domain of a single chip supplier, they don’t offer all\nthe advantages (and issues)'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 2, 'start_token': 500, 'end_token': 800, 'text': ' example would be the AMD Ryzen Zen 3 processors. Homogenous chiplets share many of the\nadvantages mentioned above – however, as they remain the domain of a single chip supplier, they don’t offer all\nthe advantages (and issues) that heterogenous chiplets do. A heterogenous chiplet MCM contains chiplets from\nmultiple manufacturers, often made in different facilities on different process nodes. Heterogenous chiplets might\ninclude a combination of CPU, GPU, NPU, FPGA, and/or special purpose chiplets. Chip consumers (OEMs, brands,\netc.…) can assemble the optimum selection of chiplets into a heterogenous chiplet MCM for their needs, and\nchiplet suppliers can manufacture their chiplets in the ideal location/node for price, performance, and area.\nWhile heterogenous chiplets seem to have multiple advantages over traditional monolithic silicon and even\nhomogenous chiplets, they still have not been mass-market deployed. This white paper will explore the\ncommercial, interface, packaging, and security issues heterogenous chiplets face, along with recommendations for\nthe industry’s successful deployment of heterogenous chiplets.\n2. COMMERCIAL CHALLENGES\nWith monolithic silicon and homogenous chiplets, there are clear lines of ownership and responsibility when\nbusiness and technical issues arise. However, how are these issues addressed with heterogenous chiplets?\nBusiness\nMonolithic silicon has always been ‘owned’ by a single party (the chip'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 3, 'start_token': 750, 'end_token': 1050, 'text': ' chiplets, there are clear lines of ownership and responsibility when\nbusiness and technical issues arise. However, how are these issues addressed with heterogenous chiplets?\nBusiness\nMonolithic silicon has always been ‘owned’ by a single party (the chipmaker). The same is true for homogenous\nchiplets. When a customer wishes to purchase a chip or monolithic MCM, they negotiate a contract with the\nchipmaker (or agent) and procure the silicon.\nHowever, what happens with a heterogenous chiplet MCM? As chiplets will likely be sold on the open market as\nraw silicon, rather than assembled in MCM form – what happens if an OEM specifies which chiplets are to be part\nof an MCM, and contracts the assembly and test of the MCM to a third party? Does the OEM own that design, or\ndoes the third party? Who warrants the design and fitness? Who owns the supply chain? Certainly, this can be\nsolved if a SoC (System on Chip) maker is taking ownership of the MCM design, sourcing chiplets from various\nmanufacturers, and sells the MCM as a finished product\nMany potential business challenges arise with heterogenous chiplets which likely must be addressed contractually\nprior to any MCM design start.\nTechnical\nBusiness issues aside, what happens if there are technical issues with heterogenous chiplet MCMs? With\nmonolithic silicon and homogenous chiplets, the chip'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 4, 'start_token': 1000, 'end_token': 1300, 'text': ' likely must be addressed contractually\nprior to any MCM design start.\nTechnical\nBusiness issues aside, what happens if there are technical issues with heterogenous chiplet MCMs? With\nmonolithic silicon and homogenous chiplets, the chip maker has always been the responsible party. What happens\nwith heterogenous MCMs?\nGiven that there will be multiple suppliers of chiplets within the MCM, who takes ownership in root cause analysis\nand fixes? And how does the market prevent echoes of the latter 1990s/early 2000s “WinTel” technical support\nboondoggle (CPU maker points finger at OS maker, who then points finger at computer maker, who in turn points\nfinger at CPU maker)?\n3. INTERFACE CHALLENGES\nDesign engineers are all familiar with the C2C (chip-to-chip) communications that are used between various\ncomponents with electronic systems today -- common examples include PCIe, LVDS, and CXL. Very similar to C2C\ncommunications, a D2D (die-to-die) interface is a functional block that provides the data interface between two\nsilicon dies (chiplets) within a package. The D2D creates a reliable link and is generally described as the PHY and\nthe controller. The D2D interface is a key enabler in the move from monolithic SoC to multi-die SoC in the same\npackage. There have been several recent developments with D'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 5, 'start_token': 1250, 'end_token': 1550, 'text': ' and is generally described as the PHY and\nthe controller. The D2D interface is a key enabler in the move from monolithic SoC to multi-die SoC in the same\npackage. There have been several recent developments with D2D communications that are used between\nchiplets.\nFigure 1: C2C versus D2D\nThe comparison between C2C and D2D is very similar. Where, in the case of C2C communications, the channel is\nusually over a PCB board. The PCB can have a variety of different materials and thus different channel\ncharacteristics. Similarly, for D2D communications, there are a variety of channels or substrates that can be used\nto communicate, generally an organic substrate (2D) or an interposer (2.5D). This will impact the particular D2D\ninterface IP since the channel will be different and will likely require modifications to the interface, for example,\nthe bump pitch or the signal loss.\nD2D C2C\nSubstrate PCB\nShort reach (mm) Long reach (cm)\nSmall low-power drivers High current drivers\nRaw/stream Protocol or packets\nTable 1: D2D vs C2C Comparison\nThese D2D communications can be serial or parallel, be source synchronous or clock forwarded, single-ended or\ndifferential. The Serial D2D interface is fundamentally a SerDes (serializer/deserializer), which includes the\nparallel'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 6, 'start_token': 1500, 'end_token': 1800, 'text': 'C Comparison\nThese D2D communications can be serial or parallel, be source synchronous or clock forwarded, single-ended or\ndifferential. The Serial D2D interface is fundamentally a SerDes (serializer/deserializer), which includes the\nparallel-to-serial (serial-to-parallel) conversion, impedance matching, CDR (clock data recovery), or clock\nforwarding. The greatest value of a serial interface is the minimization of the number of IOs required. Parallel\ninterfaces basically take a large number of IO pins (100’ to 1000’s) that drive a single-ended signal from die to die.\nThe data rates can be from a few gigabits to up to 40+ Gbps and the distance is generally up to 10mm.\n4. D2D INTERFACE TYPES\nD2D interfaces can be characterized in a variety of ways-- serial vs. parallel or channel/package type or throughput.\nIn this white paper, we will take a simple approach and discuss this in 2 ways a) Serial vs Parallel and b) Proprietary\nstandards and an Open standard.\nSerial vs. Parallel\n“Serial” D2D is essentially a SerDes, while “parallel” D2D is basically a point-to-point connection. While both can be\nused for D2D interfaces, the strengths, and costs of each have generally dictated which is used. Serial has been\npredominately used in high-end networking and optical chiplet'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 7, 'start_token': 1750, 'end_token': 2050, 'text': ' is basically a point-to-point connection. While both can be\nused for D2D interfaces, the strengths, and costs of each have generally dictated which is used. Serial has been\npredominately used in high-end networking and optical chiplet applications, while parallel has been more\ngenerally adopted. Both are characterized by the following below.\nSerial Parallel\nDie 1 Die 2 Die 1 Die 2\nData/Clock\nData\nTx Rx\nClock\nChip Chip\nFigure 2: Serial and Parallel Interfaces\nSerial Parallel\nHigh data/line rate Lower data/line rate\nLower IO count High IO count\nHigher power Lower Power\nHigher latency Lower latency\nOrganic substrate Organic or Interposer\nTable 2: Serial vs Parallel\nProprietary vs. Open D2D Standards\nSpecific to chiplets, the industry to date has utilized many proprietary interfaces as part of homogenous chiplet\nMCM deployments. The homogenous nature of these MCMs has allowed the use of proprietary D2D interfaces.\nTable 3 illustrates some, but not all, of the proprietary, interconnects proposed or used on homogenous chiplets.\nTable 3: Survey of Proprietary D2D\nWith the use of proprietary interfaces in homogenous chiplets, chip designers have design control over both sides\nof any chiplet link.\nHowever, as the market moves to heterogenous chiplets, the market must move to an open standards.'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 8, 'start_token': 2000, 'end_token': 2300, 'text': 'D\nWith the use of proprietary interfaces in homogenous chiplets, chip designers have design control over both sides\nof any chiplet link.\nHowever, as the market moves to heterogenous chiplets, the market must move to an open standards. These\nallow for different chiplet makers to build discrete chiplets which interoperate seamlessly. Several open standards\nhave been developed to usher in the age of heterogenous chiplets. Examples of some of the open standards\ndeveloped to date are shown in Table 4.\nTable 4: Survey of Open D2D Standards\nHow to Select the “Right” D2D Interface?\nWhen designing chiplets or multi-die SoCs, system designers are faced with many design choices and trade-offs.\nPerhaps the most difficult of these is in selecting the “right” D2D interface. But is there such a thing as the “right”\ninterface?\nFor chip designers and system architects, the first step in determining the chosen D2D interface may come from\nthe application or the functions of chiplets. This is where the priorities, trade-offs, and considerations are made.\nHere are some key trade-offs and considerations:\n• Bandwidth: The D2D interface must be able to support the required bandwidth of the application or\nsystem that it is talking to. This can be achieved by having fewer PHY lanes at a very high line rate or\nhaving a greater number of PHY lanes to increase throughput. Bandwidth requirements are generally\n'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 9, 'start_token': 2250, 'end_token': 2550, 'text': ' support the required bandwidth of the application or\nsystem that it is talking to. This can be achieved by having fewer PHY lanes at a very high line rate or\nhaving a greater number of PHY lanes to increase throughput. Bandwidth requirements are generally\ndetermined by the chiplet usage.\n• Beachfront: Refers to the throughput at the edge of a die, or the density of I/O at the edge. This can\npotentially determine the size of a chiplet.\n• Latency: This can have an impact depending upon architecture. In the case of partitioning a unified\narchitecture or memory architecture that requires uniformity, designers will want to prevent non-unified\ndelays or access times to data or memory.\n• Channel or Loss: The channel or loss can determine the kind of package used and how far or long the\nchannel can be. These criteria can also determine the robustness or SI/PI of the interface a designer\nmight select.\n• Power/Energy: Power is also an issue in chiplets, and a likely design goal is to achieve power targets on\npar with monolithic levels. D2D interfaces utilize short-reach and low-loss channels, but there is still a\nsignificant difference in the various implementations and number of lanes used.\n• Package Type: If a given package is already determined, it may also dictate which D2D interfaces are\navailable to the designers. Some D2D interfaces are more optimized for MCM-type packages and'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 10, 'start_token': 2500, 'end_token': 2800, 'text': ' various implementations and number of lanes used.\n• Package Type: If a given package is already determined, it may also dictate which D2D interfaces are\navailable to the designers. Some D2D interfaces are more optimized for MCM-type packages and will\nutilize C-bumps whereas interposer-based packages will utilize micro-bump architecture.\n• Cost: Continually a leading design factor that must be considered. This can include but is not limited to\nIP, implementation, package, and testing costs.\nHowever, one non-technical factor that needs to be considered is market acceptance. Just because a D2D\ninterface may be superior does not mean it is necessarily the right choice. It is imperative that system architects\nexamine what D2D interface open standards are present in their target markets, and make choices not only based\non technical reasons but also based on market reasons.\nMoore’s law has driven the semiconductor industry for decades and the chiplet era is clearly a way to preserve the\nessence of Moore’s Law. With that said, the D2D interface will become even more and more critical in the years to\ncome. It will determine how innovative the next generation of chiplets can be and truly democratize multi-die\nSoCs.\n5. PACKAGING INNOVATIONS FOR CHIPLETS\nWhat is the role of packaging as we move into the Chiplets era? The industry is moving toward desegregated SOC\nmodels that bring together'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 11, 'start_token': 2750, 'end_token': 3050, 'text': ' multi-die\nSoCs.\n5. PACKAGING INNOVATIONS FOR CHIPLETS\nWhat is the role of packaging as we move into the Chiplets era? The industry is moving toward desegregated SOC\nmodels that bring together heterogeneous chiplets components within a single package. As such, the packaging\ncommunity is responding by developing advanced packaging technology innovations to address urgent and\nincreasingly complex industry needs.\nFigure 3: Chiplets – Heterogeneous Integration Source: SEMICON Europa Nov 16, 2022\nFigure 3 illustrates the traditional monolithic System on Chip (SoC) die, desegregated by design into a modularized\nSoC (Chiplets) format, that is then integrated into a single package. Applications include artificial intelligence (AI)\nand machine learning (ML) network switches for high-performance servers within data centers and advanced PCs.\nThe packaging platform for chiplet integration includes large flip chip BGA (multichip module) on advanced build-\nup substrate, 2.5D (TSV) silicon interposer, high-density fanout RDl, silicon bridge, and 3D micro-bump stacking,\nand hybrid bond. Market applications, as well as packaging integration technologies, will continue to evolve for\nperformance and efficiency, from system and packaging architecture to co-design and co-design tools, assembly,\ntest, and manufacturing processes.\nThe electronics and semiconductor communities are highly innovative, and the packaging community is no\n'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 12, 'start_token': 3000, 'end_token': 3300, 'text': ' integration technologies, will continue to evolve for\nperformance and efficiency, from system and packaging architecture to co-design and co-design tools, assembly,\ntest, and manufacturing processes.\nThe electronics and semiconductor communities are highly innovative, and the packaging community is no\nexception. The fanout reconstitution process, materials, and equipment were originally developed for low-end\nWLCSP applications. Some years ago, packaging engineers used the materials and process equipment to integrate\ntwo die into a reconstituted format for flip chip assembly onto an organic substrate, shown in figure 4. This\ninnovative concept, the so-called high-density Fan Out RDL, has become an essential advanced packaging platform\nacross our industry for multiple die integration. Together with TSV silicon interposer and bridge technologies, Fan\nOut RDL innovations will form the advanced packaging technology base for the chiplet revolution as we move full\nsteam ahead.\nFigure 4. High Density Fanout RDL application Source SEMICON Europa Nov 16, 2022\nWhile the chiplet revolution may have already started with the concept of desegregated and “modularized”\nSoC, innovations in advanced packaging have expanded this original concept to reach many more market\napplications. The design and integration of multiple die through advanced packaging into a single package is\nenabling the business of bringing electronics products to the marketplace in the most effective and efficient\nways possible. An interesting article on this topic by Ed Sperling and'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 13, 'start_token': 3250, 'end_token': 3550, 'text': '. The design and integration of multiple die through advanced packaging into a single package is\nenabling the business of bringing electronics products to the marketplace in the most effective and efficient\nways possible. An interesting article on this topic by Ed Sperling and Karen Heyman may be found in reference\n[2]\n6. CHIPLET SECURITY CHALLENGES\nFor all the advantages chiplets offer, they have distinct drawbacks from a security point of view. Due to the nature\nof heterogenous MCMs, there is a particular vulnerability to malicious counterfeit systems snuck into the\nmanufacturing flow of any one of the chiplets being integrated. In addition to counterfeits, since chiplets involve\nan expanded number of supply-chain entities, each with their own design teams and EDA tool flows, there is an\nincreased opportunity for hardware trojans to be injected into one of the many chiplets, and therefore into the\naggregate MCM. Finally, another risk emerges from the new attack surfaces, in the form of the chiplet-to-chiplet\ninterfaces within the MCM, which are not present (or are much more difficult to electrically contact) in a\nmonolithic chip. In this situation, a reworked MCM could be susceptible to a man-in-the-middle attack (MITM),\nwhich can be used to spy, sabotage communications, and/or corrupt data, as well as steal personal information.\nProving and Securing Authenticity\nAuthenticity'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 14, 'start_token': 3500, 'end_token': 3800, 'text': 'worked MCM could be susceptible to a man-in-the-middle attack (MITM),\nwhich can be used to spy, sabotage communications, and/or corrupt data, as well as steal personal information.\nProving and Securing Authenticity\nAuthenticity is best achieved with cryptographic challenge/response authentication. This technique is not\ndissimilar to how online passwords are verified or how ATM withdrawals are authenticated with the account\nholder’s PIN number. In general: the verifier issues a “challenge” (e.g., a random number) to the prover, who then\ncalculates a cryptographic “response” involving the challenge and some pre-shared secret. In this way, the prover\ncan securely and reliability demonstrate to the verifier that “it knows a secret” which only authentic components\nshould know.\nWhile the challenge/response protocol aspect is straightforward enough, the challenges to doing it well are\nsignificant. Specifically:\n• How is secret data provisioned to the component (e.g., a chiplet) during manufacture, and how is this\nprovisioning process itself protected from theft or misuse?\n• How is secret authentication data stored and utilized within an MCM’s chiplets – both the prover and the\nverifier chiplets – such that it is not easily recovered by an adversary?\nSilicon-based Countermeasures\nThe first domain of authenticity resides with the silicon devices themselves. Specifically, how does the prover\nchiplet protect the secrets it’s been given?\nGenerally speaking,'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 15, 'start_token': 3750, 'end_token': 4050, 'text': ' that it is not easily recovered by an adversary?\nSilicon-based Countermeasures\nThe first domain of authenticity resides with the silicon devices themselves. Specifically, how does the prover\nchiplet protect the secrets it’s been given?\nGenerally speaking, public-key encryption techniques are the most straightforward approaches to measuring\nauthenticity. The major drawback to these techniques is the computational effort it requires to perform a\nchallenge/response exchange – symmetric encryption techniques are much lighter-weight computationally, but\nmore difficult to deploy.\nIn the most straightforward public-key authentication system, the “prover” chiplet contains a secret private key, as\nwell as the associated public key encapsulated within a signed certificate. During an authentication event, the\nverifying component (e.g., another chiplet containing the MCM’s principal Root-of-Trust security processor)\nobtains the certificate from the prover chip, verifies the signature on the certificate, then challenges the prover\ndevice to prove that it has the secret key associated with the verified public key. For example, the verifier might\ndeliver a 16-byte random number to the prover chip which takes that nonce as an input to a private-key signature\noperation. The prover chip then calculates and returns the signature value, which the verifier can then\nauthenticate – if the prover chip completes the calculation correctly, it must know the secret private-key value.\nOf course, while knowing the secret private-key value is an essential aspect of multifactor authentication'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 16, 'start_token': 4000, 'end_token': 4300, 'text': ' returns the signature value, which the verifier can then\nauthenticate – if the prover chip completes the calculation correctly, it must know the secret private-key value.\nOf course, while knowing the secret private-key value is an essential aspect of multifactor authentication, it\nshouldn’t be the only one – counterfeit chips can (and do) contain entirely valid private key values stolen from\nother authentic chips. At least two multifactor components are recommended for improved security, so not just\n“something the chip knows” (e.g., the chip knows the secret key) but perhaps also “something the chip has” like a\nhardware-accelerated engine for performing the calculations. A hardware-accelerated authentic chip might\ncomplete the signature calculation in 10mS while an inauthentic chip with a stolen key might complete the same\ncalculation in 50mS – a verifier could detect this as a second element of multifactor authentication.\nAs for how to best secure the secret data within a prover chip, the most critical aspect is to protect the calculations\ninvolving the data, not the data itself.\nFor example, one of the most tamper-resistant techniques for deriving secret key data is the use of a Physically\nUnclonable Function circuit (aka, a “PUF”). This is because, unlike NVM where the data exists in perpetuity, a true\nPUF circuit is a “mixed signal circuit” whose output value is inaccessible'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 17, 'start_token': 4250, 'end_token': 4550, 'text': ' Physically\nUnclonable Function circuit (aka, a “PUF”). This is because, unlike NVM where the data exists in perpetuity, a true\nPUF circuit is a “mixed signal circuit” whose output value is inaccessible when the chip is powered off. Attacks\nagainst PUFs must therefore be staged while the chip – and other chip-level countermeasures – are powered on.\nHowever, even with the most tamper-resistant PUF as the secret-key source, if the secret key value is\ncryptographically operated on without power-analysis countermeasures applied during that calculation, an\nadversary need only monitor power-supply consumption during execution of the challenge/response protocol to\nrecover the secret key with relatively low effort.\nSupply Chain Security & Provisioning\nEnsuring the integrity of a chiplets supply chain is in some ways more challenging than insuring the integrity of the\nchallenge/response algorithm, in that the protections are not as well known, nor as well standardized. Encryption\ntechnology does come into play, as do several other best practices. For example:\n• Every chiplet in a given product line should be provisioned with a device-unique ID, and that ID should be\nincluded as part of any authentication certificate. Ideally, verifiers should be able to confirm the validity of\nan ID value – for example, it can be compared against an online block/allow list.\n• During provisioning, the provisioning equipment should not only authenticate the chip ('}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 18, 'start_token': 4500, 'end_token': 4800, 'text': ' any authentication certificate. Ideally, verifiers should be able to confirm the validity of\nan ID value – for example, it can be compared against an online block/allow list.\n• During provisioning, the provisioning equipment should not only authenticate the chip (e.g., if a chiplet\nappears at final test asserting it corrected wafer-sorted, the provisioning equipment should verify the\nwafer sort provisioning contents before trusting the device), but the chip should authenticate the\nequipment (e.g., the provisioning equipment should prove it knows a netlist key before the chip unlocks\nitself for wafer-sort provisioning).\n• All certificate-signing operations performed during manufacture should of course be performed within\nthe security boundary of a tamper-resistant hardware security module. It is nearly impossible to\ndistinguish authentic devices from counterfeit devices if an adversary can steal a certificate signing key.\n• Auditing logs from various points of manufacture (wafer sort, final test, system attach, etc.) should be\ncentralized so that strict auditing checks can be performed. For example, every device provisioned during\nwafer sort should appear at final test once and only once – anything else would indicate supply-chain\ntheft (bad) or supply-chain injection (even worse).\n• Lastly, and primarily in the context of chiplets, a PKI-assured technique for adding certificate chain-of-\ntrust techniques to the authentication certificates should be considered. For example, chiplet vendors\nshould deliver not only silicon to'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 19, 'start_token': 4750, 'end_token': 5050, 'text': 'even worse).\n• Lastly, and primarily in the context of chiplets, a PKI-assured technique for adding certificate chain-of-\ntrust techniques to the authentication certificates should be considered. For example, chiplet vendors\nshould deliver not only silicon to MCM integrators, but they should also deliver trusted certificates\nassociated with their individual signing key used for their devices. The MCM integrator would then\nprovision that collection of trusted certificates into the main verifier of the MCM (i.e., the chiplet\ncontaining the principal Root-of-Trust) so that the authenticity of each chiplet in the system can be\nreliably confirmed.\n7. RECOMMENDATIONS\nThe value of heterogenous chiplets cannot be understated. The ability to create heterogenous MCMs featuring\nchiplets serving different functions, manufactured in the ideal method and with ideal materials, will usher in a new\nwave of semiconductor deployments and use cases.\nHowever, for heterogenous chiplets to achieve mass market success, the semiconductor industry is going to have\nto align on the key challenges discussed in this white paper.\n• The business and technical support owner of an MCM should be contractually established at the onset of\na program.\n• A very limited number of, if not a single, open D2D standard for chiplet interfaces must be agreed upon. A\nlarge number of competing D2D standards will only cause incompatibility and limit the effectiveness and\ndeployment of'}, {'source_filename': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4.txt', 'title': '2022-IPIG-Heterogenous-Integration-Chiplets-White-Paper-Final-v4', 'chunk_id': 20, 'start_token': 5000, 'end_token': 5230, 'text': '• A very limited number of, if not a single, open D2D standard for chiplet interfaces must be agreed upon. A\nlarge number of competing D2D standards will only cause incompatibility and limit the effectiveness and\ndeployment of heterogenous chiplets.\n• The packaging community needs to respond by developing advanced packaging technology innovations to\naddress urgent and increasingly complex industry needs for chiplets.\n• To assure chiplet authenticity, security provisions must be standardized and put into place during chiplet\ndesign and followed through in production.\n8. REFERENCES\n1. “Advanced Packaging Creativity Enabling Silicon Systems” presentation by Yin Chang, SEMICON Europa,\nNovember 16, 2022\n2. “The March Towards Chiplets”, Ed Sperling and Karen Heyman, Semiconductor Engineering December 15,\n2022. https://semiengineering.com/the-march-toward-chiplets/\n9. CONTRIBUTORS\nSource material for this white paper has been provided by:\nPaul Karazuba (Editor)\nFrankwell Lin\nWilliam Chen\nYoan Dupret\nScott Best\nVolker Politz\nKevin Yee, Tony Luk'}, {'source_filename': 'Chiplets and Heterogeneous Packaging Are Changing System Design and Analysis White Paper _ Cadence.html', 'title': 'Chiplets and Heterogeneous Packaging Are Changing System Design and Analysis', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': 'The transition to a chiplet-based architecture is a more subtle change if you consider the evolution of semiconductor packaging over nearly five decades (Figure 4), with heterogeneously designed integrated multi-chip products along for most of that time. EDA companies started to develop tools for advanced packaging in the early 1990s, around the time the ball grid array (BGA) provided a high-performance, small form-factor option to the mechanical lead frame package.\nMulti-chip modules (MCM) date back to the 1960s, which is still common for some applications. The term SiP began to replace the term MCM in the late 1990s as wafer-level testing became ubiquitous, and the KGD problem that plagued MCM design started to become less of an issue. This is also about the time that package designers started stacking die, which clearly shows that 3D stacking is not new to package designers. In the early-mid 2000s, RF modules started to move to the laminate BGA packages that are very common in today’s smartphones and watches. Moving to the current generation of advanced multi-chip packaging, silicon substrates now provide a high-density alternative to organic substrates with the introduction of through-silicon vias (TSVs). The earliest example of leveraging TSV-based silicon interposer technology was the re-partitioned Xilinx FPGA. Today, this technology is most commonly referred to as 2.5D-IC packaging'}, {'source_filename': 'Chiplets and Heterogeneous Packaging Are Changing System Design and Analysis White Paper _ Cadence.html', 'title': 'Chiplets and Heterogeneous Packaging Are Changing System Design and Analysis', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 433, 'text': ' through-silicon vias (TSVs). The earliest example of leveraging TSV-based silicon interposer technology was the re-partitioned Xilinx FPGA. Today, this technology is most commonly referred to as 2.5D-IC packaging.\nTen years ago, semiconductor foundries extended back-end design into silicon-based advanced packaging solutions. The foundry-based approach of high-density RDL also drove renewed interest in fan-out wafer-level packaging (FOWLP) and now, with support for multiple dies, interest is at an all-time high. Additionally, to further increase bandwidth, 3D-IC packaging, meaning wafer-on-wafer or chip-on-wafer stacking, has a new life.\nThe chiplet trend (Figure 4) shows that next-generation chiplet-based technologies are just a new way of partitioning logic that aligns nicely with advancements in package manufacturing technologies.'}, {'source_filename': 'Challenges and recent prospectives of 3D heterogeneous integration.txt', 'title': 'Challenges and recent prospectives of 3D heterogeneous integration', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': 'e-Prime - Advances in Electrical Engineering, Electronics and Energy 2 (2022) 100052\nContents lists available at ScienceDirect\ne-Prime - Advances in Electrical\nEngineering, Electronics and Energy\njournal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/prime\nChallenges and recent prospectives of 3D heterogeneous integration\nShuye Zhanga,b,*, Zhenfeng Lia, Hongzhi Zhoua, Rongqing Lia, Shaoan Wanga,\nKyung-Wook Paikc, Peng Hea,*\naState Key Laboratory of Advanced Welding and Joining, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China\nbChongqing Research Institute, Harbin Institute of Technology, Chongqing 401135, China\ncDepartment of materials science and engineering, KAIST, 291 Daehak-ro, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-338, Republic of Korea\nA R T I C L E I N F O A B S T R A C T\nKeywords: With the continuous reduction of chip feature size, the continuation of Moore’s Law becomes increasingly\n3D heterogeneous integration difficult and heterogeneous integration has become one of the important orientations of electronic technology.\nAdvanced packaging Focusing on the issues including system integration and interconnection in submicron to 10 μm orders of\nReliability\nmagnitude, 3D heterogeneous integration formed by multiple chip connections on package substrates'}, {'source_filename': 'Challenges and recent prospectives of 3D heterogeneous integration.txt', 'title': 'Challenges and recent prospectives of 3D heterogeneous integration', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': ' important orientations of electronic technology.\nAdvanced packaging Focusing on the issues including system integration and interconnection in submicron to 10 μm orders of\nReliability\nmagnitude, 3D heterogeneous integration formed by multiple chip connections on package substrates supported\nby active through-silicon vias breaks the scale barrier from micro-nano technology to system integration at the\nacceptable cost. 3D heterogeneous integration is gradually developing towards 3D stacking, multi-function\nintegration and hybrid heterogeneous integration, which makes integrated products have the advantages of\nhigh integration, low power consumption, miniaturization and high reliability. This paper summarizes the\ndevelopment of 3D heterogeneous integration, the current researches as well as the fields and prospects of\napplication.\n1. Introduction Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) and Semiconductor Equip-\nment and Materials International (SEMI). HIR defines heterogeneous\nAs Moore’s Law approaches its limits, the process of chip process integration as the integration of individually manufactured components\nminiaturization has been slowing down and the industry needs to create into higher-level components to enhance functionality and improve\nnew paradigms [1–5]. The industry is turning its attention to hetero- overall operational characteristics. HIR aims to maintain continuous\ngeneous integration. Heterogeneous integration uses packaging tech- creation of new economic values in the semiconductor industry. The devices available for heterogeneous inte- heterogeneous integration breaks the scale barrier from micro-nano\ngration include: bipolar devices in radio frequency/analog electronic technology to system integration at the acceptable cost [3,12].\nsystem, Complementary Metal Oxide Semiconductor (CMOS) devices in Semiconductor product packaging efficiency has gradually devel-\nlow-frequency analog-to-digital mixed signal system, oped from 10% to 90% or even 100%, and advanced packaging tech-\nMicro-Electro-Mechanical System (MEMS)/Nano-Electromechanical nologies such as flip chip, bump, wafer-level package, 2.5D package\nSystem (NEMS) devices, optoelectronics/electro-optical conversion de- (interposer), and 3D package have emerged in the way of heterogeneous\nvices, etc. [6–8]. Heterogeneous integration can be divided into two integration technology implementation, as shown in Fig. 1 [13–21].\ncategories: hybrid integration and mon'}, {'source_filename': 'Challenges and recent prospectives of 3D heterogeneous integration.txt', 'title': 'Challenges and recent prospectives of 3D heterogeneous integration', 'chunk_id': 3, 'start_token': 750, 'end_token': 1050, 'text': ' package have emerged in the way of heterogeneous\nvices, etc. [6–8]. The assembly process of SoIC can be either wafer- chip in the vertical direction can be achieved, unlike package bonding\non-wafer (WoW), chip-on-wafer (CoW), or chip-on-chip (CoC) hybrid\nand stacking technology using bumps, TSV enables the chip to increase\nbonding.\nIntel FOVEROS with the hybrid bonding technology, the pad pitch can go the density'}, {'source_filename': 'Challenges and recent prospectives of 3D heterogeneous integration.txt', 'title': 'Challenges and recent prospectives of 3D heterogeneous integration', 'chunk_id': 6, 'start_token': 1500, 'end_token': 1800, 'text': 'fer (CoW), or chip-on-chip (CoC) hybrid\nand stacking technology using bumps, TSV enables the chip to increase\nbonding.\nIntel FOVEROS with the hybrid bonding technology, the pad pitch can go the density of stacking in the three-dimensional direction while reducing\ndown to 10 µm. This is many times more than the one with 50-µm-pitch the form factor. Zhang et al. e - P r i m e - A d v a n c e s i n E l e c t r i c a l E n g i n e e r i n g, E l e c t r o n i c s a n d E n e rgy2(2022)100052\nFig. 3. (a) Chiplets are on the substrate of a heterogeneous integration package [1]. (b) 3D chiplet heterogeneous integration [2]. (c) Intel’s Ponte Vecchio OAM with\nchiplet. (d) Cost-performance scalability with chiplet design [79].\nFig. 4. Series of platforms for semiconductor advanced packaging.\n3\nS. Hwang, et al., The thermal dissipation characteristics of the novel system-in-\npractical applying technology research on new materials pro-\npackage technology (ICE-SiP) for mobile and 3D high-end packages, in: 2019\ncessing, brazing, soldering and micro-joining and reliability.\nIEEE 69th Electronic Components and Technology Conference (ECTC), 2019,\nHe is the duty dean of school of materials science and engi-\npp. 614–619, https://doi.org/10.1109/ECTC.2019.00098.\nneering at Harbin Institute of'}, {'source_filename': 'Challenges and recent prospectives of 3D heterogeneous integration.txt', 'title': 'Challenges and recent prospectives of 3D heterogeneous integration', 'chunk_id': 79, 'start_token': 19750, 'end_token': 20050, 'text': '2019,\nHe is the duty dean of school of materials science and engi-\npp. 614–619, https://doi.org/10.1109/ECTC.2019.00098.\nneering at Harbin Institute of Technology, China, also the duty\n[125] S.-K. Up to now, he has authorized 292\ntransient thermal model for 3D ICs with liquid cooling via enhanced heat transfer\npapers, 39 patent, 7 books, and 13 awards in nano-joining.\ncavity'}, {'source_filename': 'Challenges and recent prospectives of 3D heterogeneous integration.txt', 'title': 'Challenges and recent prospectives of 3D heterogeneous integration', 'chunk_id': 80, 'start_token': 20000, 'end_token': 20212, 'text': '. Up to now, he has authorized 292\ntransient thermal model for 3D ICs with liquid cooling via enhanced heat transfer\npapers, 39 patent, 7 books, and 13 awards in nano-joining.\ncavity geometries, in: 2010 16th International Workshop on Thermal\nNow his interest is in flexible metallic joining for displays ap-\nInvestigations of ICs and Systems (THERMINIC), 2010, pp. 1–6.\nplications.\n[127] D.R. Frear, L.N. Ramanathan, J.-. J.ang, N.L. Owens, Emerging reliability Phone number:+86 18,686,863,097\nchallenges in electronic packaging, in: 2008 IEEE International Reliability Physics\nAddress: Room 629, Harbin Institute of Technology, No. 92 Xidazhijie Street, Harbin\nSymposium, 2008, pp. 450–454.\nCity, China.\n14'}, {'source_filename': '14.Subramanian_lyer.txt', 'title': '14.Subramanian_lyer', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': 'What really is\nHeterogeneous Integration?\nSubramanian (”Subu”) S. Iyer\nemail s.s.iyer@ucla.edu\nUCLA Center for heterogeneous Integration and performance Scaling\nSamueli School of Engineering\nUniversity of California, Los Angeles, CA 90095\nchips.ucla.edu\n17th U.S.-Korea Forums on Nanotechnology\nApril 3-4 2023\nUCLA CHIPS\nA UCLA partnership to develop applications, architectures, design\nmethodologies, enablement, design, devices and core technologies and\nthe eco-system that create and leverage advanced packaging and\ndevelop our students & scholars to lead this effort in the real world\nSimplify hardware development through novel\narchitectures, integration methods,\ntechnologies, and devices.\n2\nTransistors are shrinking but die sizes are increasing!\n• 5X1010 Transistors\n• 7 nm node\n• 106 Transistors\n• 0.5 mm node\nMore cores, more cache, larger workloads\nBut mainly because monolithic chips\noutperform multi-die packaged chips\nThis is driven by slow package scaling\nSilicon and Package scaling\n25 160 (mm)\n(nm)\nAdvanced\n140\n20\nPackaging\n120\n100\n15\n80\n10\n60\n40\n5\nChiplet/dielet revolution 20\n0 0\n2011 2012 201'}, {'source_filename': '14.Subramanian_lyer.txt', 'title': '14.Subramanian_lyer', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': 'mm)\n(nm)\nAdvanced\n140\n20\nPackaging\n120\n100\n15\n80\n10\n60\n40\n5\nChiplet/dielet revolution 20\n0 0\n2011 2012 2013 2014 2015 2016 2017 2018 2019 2020 2021\nSince 2015, Packaging has taken off!\nWhy?\nAdvanced packaging borrowed\nimmensely from Silicon technology\nAdapted from: S.S. Iyer, MRS bulletin (2015)\nHeterogeneous Integration is Not New!\nWhat is different now is scale:\n• Finer pitch connections\n• Finer traces\n• Tighter inter- die spacings\nChip 1 Chip 2\nr\ne S Board e z • Simpler I/Os\nzl\nai\nr\nchannel 1\nila\nir\ne\ne s\nr e\ndr How do you make the package behave\nS e\nzl\nai\nr e B\nch\no\na\na\nn\nrd\nn el 2\nz ila\nir\ne\ne s like a monolithic solution?\nr e\nd\nThese dimensions must approach\nDie 1 Die 2\nmonolithic dimensions\n(NV\nIDI\nThe Chiplet/Dielet Golden Regime\nDie yielding\nconstraint\nOptimal pitch\nMechanical\nElectrical/logical\n'}, {'source_filename': '14.Subramanian_lyer.txt', 'title': '14.Subramanian_lyer', 'chunk_id': 2, 'start_token': 500, 'end_token': 800, 'text': 'd\nThese dimensions must approach\nDie 1 Die 2\nmonolithic dimensions\n(NV\nIDI\nThe Chiplet/Dielet Golden Regime\nDie yielding\nconstraint\nOptimal pitch\nMechanical\nElectrical/logical\n2 to 10 µm\nconstraints\nconstraints\nOptimal dielet\nSerDes-like\nsize\n1 to 100 mm2\nPackaging-like\nSoC-like\nDie handling\nconstraint\nCMOS\nwire-like\n50 nm 500 µm\nInterconnect pitch\nGate pitch BGA/LGA\nDielet/chiplet size (# of circuits) I/O complexity/power\nIP reuse Testing complexity\nIyer et al (2019)\nThese new paradigms are changing the way we put\ncomplex systems together\n• System is disaggregated into chiplets\n• Bare dielets are stocked\n– Requires a dielet marketplace\n• A few chiplets will need to be\ndesigned\n– Much more contained and will add to\nthe dielet marketplace\n• The interconnect fabric is designed\n7\nTime to market: less than 6 months\n• Dielets are then assembled at fine\npitch in an onshore secure facility\nNRE: ~millions of dollars\n1.\nS. S. Iyer, "Heterogeneous Integration for Performance and Scaling," in IEEE\nTransactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology, vol. 6, no. 7,\npp. '}, {'source_filename': '14.Subramanian_lyer.txt', 'title': '14.Subramanian_lyer', 'chunk_id': 3, 'start_token': 750, 'end_token': 1050, 'text': ' ~millions of dollars\n1.\nS. S. Iyer, "Heterogeneous Integration for Performance and Scaling," in IEEE\nTransactions on Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology, vol. 6, no. 7,\npp. 973-982, July 2016, doi: 10.1109/TCPMT.2015.2511626.\nChallenges of Heterogeneous Systems at Scale\nAssembly\nAssembly Enablement Applications\narchitectures\nTechnology\n• Signaling Protocols • Graph processors\n• Power delivery for short and log haul\n• Substrate • Memory appliances\n• Clocking\nTechnology • Heat dissipation • mm wave and THz\n• EDA applications\n• Connectors\n• Assembly: Fine\n• System • Medical applications\npitch die- • Passives\ndisaggregation and\nSubstrate\n• Test\nreintegration\nconnections\n• Dielet marketplace\n• Passivation\n• Security and\n• Assembling redundancy\nconnectors for • ESD and Test\npower and signal\n• Assembling\nThermal More in the Manufacturing Roadmap for Heterogenous\nIntegration and Packaging (UCLA, SEMI -sponsored by NIST)\ndissipation units\n(see the chips.ucla.edu webpage)\nUS - Korea Collaborations\n• Visiting Scholars from Korea:\n• Ji Won Shin (Samsung Packaging) spent a year in our Lab 2021-22\n• Support through SRC funding'}, {'source_filename': '14.Subramanian_lyer.txt', 'title': '14.Subramanian_lyer', 'chunk_id': 4, 'start_token': 1000, 'end_token': 1097, 'text': 'ipation units\n(see the chips.ucla.edu webpage)\nUS - Korea Collaborations\n• Visiting Scholars from Korea:\n• Ji Won Shin (Samsung Packaging) spent a year in our Lab 2021-22\n• Support through SRC funding (Samsung, SK Hynix)\n• Jump 1.0 Ascent Center\n• Jump 2.0 Chimes Center\n• Some personal history on US-Korea collaborations\n• We would welcome both industry and academic collaborations'}, {'source_filename': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI) vs System on Chip (SoC) – What’s the Difference_ - Life at Cadence - Cadence Blogs - Cadence Community.html', 'title': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI) vs System on Chip (SoC) - What’s the Difference?', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': 'Heterogeneous integration (HI) and SoC (system on chip) are two ways to design and build silicon chips. Heterogeneous integration aims to counter the growing expense and complexity of\xa0SoC design by taking a modular approach using advanced packaging technology.\nFor the past two decades, Cadence has supported the industry in transitioning to an SoC model and we remain dedicated to our customers as they push the boundaries of Moore’s Law, right down to 2nm and perhaps 1nm process nodes in pursuit of more transistors.\nNow, Heterogeneous integration\nprovides a cost-effective alternative that for many applications may prove to be more suitable than a monolithic SoC.\nLet’s look at the differences between these two design processes and the benefits and caveats from each side.\n### System on Chip (SoC)\nSystem on Chip (SoC)\nAn SoC is an integrated circuit (IC) combining every part of a compute system into a single silicon die. This can include one or more central processing units (CPUs) or processors (low-powered microcontrollers and/or application processors) each with several cores, alongside peripherals like a graphics processing unit (GPU), Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, or 4G/5G cellular modems, memory, and perhaps even storage.\nThe rise and scaling of SoCs have been one of the most innovative and exciting evolutions in semiconductor technology over the past two decades. In the past, designing a complex compute system meant designing a'}, {'source_filename': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI) vs System on Chip (SoC) – What’s the Difference_ - Life at Cadence - Cadence Blogs - Cadence Community.html', 'title': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI) vs System on Chip (SoC) - What’s the Difference?', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': ' cellular modems, memory, and perhaps even storage.\nThe rise and scaling of SoCs have been one of the most innovative and exciting evolutions in semiconductor technology over the past two decades. In the past, designing a complex compute system meant designing a system using discrete, off-the-shelf components.\nBecause all the components of an SoC are integrated together onto a single die, SoCs offer an unbeatable optimization of power, performance, and area (PPA). They require a relatively small amount of power to operate, perform compute functions very quickly, and take up far less physical space, meaning the end device can be smaller in form factor, too. SoCs now power billions of devices worldwide, from cars to laptops, smartphones to medical devices.\nHowever, building an advanced monolithic SoC, especially at the latest process nodes (7nm and below), has become increasingly expensive. Below 28nm, the cost per transistor has steadily increased since the industry embraced FinFET in the early 2010s—and the few foundries investing in the latest design nodes have all announced price increases.\nThen there are project and non-recurring engineering (NRE) costs to consider.
