Designing something at 3nm requires design teams of perhaps thousands of engineers working tens of thousands of hours and costing hundreds of millions of dollars, just for the functional verification step.\nIf you are looking to build an SoC for a mass-market smartphone, you may recoup that investment. But in smaller markets'}, {'source_filename': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI) vs System on Chip (SoC) – What’s the Difference_ - Life at Cadence - Cadence Blogs - Cadence Community.html', 'title': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI) vs System on Chip (SoC) - What’s the Difference?', 'chunk_id': 2, 'start_token': 500, 'end_token': 800, 'text': ' thousands of engineers working tens of thousands of hours and costing hundreds of millions of dollars, just for the functional verification step.\nIf you are looking to build an SoC for a mass-market smartphone, you may recoup that investment. But in smaller markets, for example, the defense/industrial spaces where only 1000 or so units are needed, it has become difficult to justify.\nFinally, consider the physical limitations. Today’s SoCs are reaching reticle limits: the manufacturing equipment simply can’t handle the growing die sizes without employing expensive stitching technology. Larger chips also have a naturally far higher risk of defects, resulting in lower yield per wafer.\n### Heterogeneous Integration\nHeterogeneous Integration\nProviding an alternative to advanced monolithic SoCs, heterogeneous integration has become a very attractive option for\xa0complex yet cost-sensitive designs.\nHeterogeneous integration refers to the use of advanced packaging technologies to combine smaller, discrete chiplets—physically realized and tested (hardened) pieces of IP designed to each perform a particular logical function—into one system in package (SiP).\nThink of an SoC as monolithic, heterogeneous integration as modular—integrating different chiplets from different foundries. As each chiplet is manufactured separately and designed to be well below the reticle limit, physically producing the chip is a far easier process. Chiplets may also be designed on any process node; heterogeneous integration could potentially combine 28nm chiplets alongside 2nm chiplets,'}, {'source_filename': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI) vs System on Chip (SoC) – What’s the Difference_ - Life at Cadence - Cadence Blogs - Cadence Community.html', 'title': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI) vs System on Chip (SoC) - What’s the Difference?', 'chunk_id': 3, 'start_token': 750, 'end_token': 1050, 'text': ' separately and designed to be well below the reticle limit, physically producing the chip is a far easier process. Chiplets may also be designed on any process node; heterogeneous integration could potentially combine 28nm chiplets alongside 2nm chiplets, for example.\nWhile heterogeneous integration is still in its early stages, the integration of different chips and discrete components side by side on a common substrate (ceramic, silicon/glass or organic) is not a new concept. Technologies such as multichip module (MCM) and SiP have been around for decades, each used in a variety of applications.\nHeterogeneous i\nntegration\ntakes SiP design a significant step forward by combining it with cutting-edge packaging and interconnect technologies such as 2.5D/3D-IC, fan-out wafer level packaging (FOWLP), silicon and glass interposers, and embedded bridges.\nIt also enables larger, more powerful chips for applications such as HPC and the server market. In these cases, large amounts of memory can be integrated directly into the package itself. This simply isn’t possible on an SoC.\nHowever,\nheterogeneous\xa0i\nntegration\nhas its caveats. Design does not get easier with heterogeneous integration, it gets harder. Moving from a single monolithic SoC to a system-level architecture re-introduces considerations that SoCs effectively counteracted, such as thermal, electrical, and mechanical stresses. It’s vital that the correct'}, {'source_filename': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI) vs System on Chip (SoC) – What’s the Difference_ - Life at Cadence - Cadence Blogs - Cadence Community.html', 'title': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI) vs System on Chip (SoC) - What’s the Difference?', 'chunk_id': 4, 'start_token': 1000, 'end_token': 1300, 'text': ' easier with heterogeneous integration, it gets harder. Moving from a single monolithic SoC to a system-level architecture re-introduces considerations that SoCs effectively counteracted, such as thermal, electrical, and mechanical stresses. It’s vital that the correct tools, methodologies, and team collaboration approaches are put in place before attempting to design a heterogeneous integration chip.\nHeterogeneous integration also means longer signal paths, more I/Os, and a larger form factor—the enemy of efficient PPA. The form factor of a heterogeneous integration-designed chip will naturally be bigger in the X and Y planes and even Z if 3D stacking is implemented, which it inevitably will be.\nFurthermore, the way chiplets are packaged in heterogeneous integration is vital to the success of the chip. This means far greater attention needs to be paid to the packaging technology at the design stage, be it 2.5D, 3D-IC, or some other packaging technology.\n### System on Chip or Heterogeneous Integration—Which Is Right for My Project?\nSystem on Chip or Heterogeneous Integration—Which Is Right for My Project?\nThe answer to this question depends largely on the application, your budget and the number of products containing your chip that you expect to ship. As more use cases emerge requiring powerful artificial intelligence (AI) and autonomous systems employing high-performance computing (HPC) at the edge, SoC technology is likely to reach the physical limits of its capability. Heterogeneous integration provides a natural'}, {'source_filename': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI) vs System on Chip (SoC) – What’s the Difference_ - Life at Cadence - Cadence Blogs - Cadence Community.html', 'title': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI) vs System on Chip (SoC) - What’s the Difference?', 'chunk_id': 5, 'start_token': 1250, 'end_token': 1381, 'text': ' ship. As more use cases emerge requiring powerful artificial intelligence (AI) and autonomous systems employing high-performance computing (HPC) at the edge, SoC technology is likely to reach the physical limits of its capability. Heterogeneous integration provides a natural next step.\nYet there will always be more applications in which no compromise in PPA is acceptable, in which case an SoC remains the best option.\nIf you’re unsure which path to take in your next chip design, talk to us. With more than 25 years of advanced packaging experience, we enable our customers to generate higher bandwidth, lower power consumption, and reduce area without traditional process scaling.'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': 'The terms “chiplet” and “heterogeneous integration” fill news pages, conference papers, and marketing presentations, and for the most part engineers understand what they’re reading. But speakers sometimes stumble during a presentation trying to figure out whether a particular die qualifies as a chiplet, and heterogeneous integration comes in different guises for different people. Both terms lack well-accepted definitions that can help navigate the nuances that now bedevil the terms.\nFor some industry experts, the presence of a die-to-die interface qualifies a piece of silicon as a chiplet. There is much more disagreement when it comes to heterogeneous integration, and when pushing and pulling on different criteria during a discussion, one person’s definition may shift. Today, this discussion could feel pedantic, but in the long term, clear and consistent definitions could spur growth in this nascent architecture.\nWhat’s a chiplet?\nChiplets are a popular topic today, thanks to the evolution of heterogeneous integration (more on this later). The disaggregation of an SoC into discrete components is significant on one level. But integrating multiple dies into a single package is far from new. Multi-chip modules (MCMs) have done this for years, mostly for non-mainstream applications that receive little attention.\nIn an MCM, the constituent components are typically chips that would otherwise be sold separately in packaged form. Absent a custom implementation, nothing new was required of the components within the MCM package.\nSo are chiplets merely a'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': ' receive little attention.\nIn an MCM, the constituent components are typically chips that would otherwise be sold separately in packaged form. Absent a custom implementation, nothing new was required of the components within the MCM package.\nSo are chiplets merely a new name for what we’ve been doing for years? Despite some disagreements on defining details, no one views chiplets as simply a new name for an old thing. The key feature identifying chiplets for most (but not all) is a dedicated way to connect one die directly to another. “It would need some level of die-to-die interface within it,” said Michael Posner, vice president of product management for high-performance computing IP solutions at\nSynopsys (https://semiengineering.com/entities/synopsys-inc/)\n.\nSome suggest that only silicon with a standardized die-to-die interface should qualify. “For us, the only point to say a chiplet is a chiplet is if we have a standardized interface between the chips,” said Andy Heinig, group leader, advanced system integration, department head of efficient electronics at\nFraunhofer IIS EAS (https://semiengineering.com/entities/fraunhofer-iis-eas/)\n. Otherwise, the assembly is just an MCM.\nAlthough a new interface isn’t strictly necessary, as existing MCMs prove, chiplet integration can improve performance and efficiency in ways that a chip in an MCM cannot. “A traditional chip is designed to be put'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 2, 'start_token': 500, 'end_token': 800, 'text': ' is just an MCM.\nAlthough a new interface isn’t strictly necessary, as existing MCMs prove, chiplet integration can improve performance and efficiency in ways that a chip in an MCM cannot. “A traditional chip is designed to be put in a package on its own, and then has to be able to drive a printed circuit board (PCB) line on its own,” said Marc Swinnen, director of product marketing at\nAnsys (https://semiengineering.com/entities/ansys/)\n. “So it has big drivers on the I/Os to drive these outside lines. A chiplet, on the other hand, is intended for use in conjunction with other chiplets on an interposer. Hence, its interface is going to be much higher speed and lower power, and it can drive only a few millimeters of wire. That chiplet can never be put in a regular package because it doesn’t have the drive strength to drive a normal wire.”\nThe whole chiplet idea originates from economic concerns about the cost of advanced nodes, as well as the inability to produce die larger than reticle size. “Chiplets result in improved overall yield and costs since they are assembled using smaller, individual known-good die and a fabrication technology suitable for each die — as opposed to integrating all functionality at a large die level with the most technologically advanced fabrication node necessary to achieve the most demanding function within the device,” said Dave Fromm, COO of\nPromex (https://'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 3, 'start_token': 750, 'end_token': 1050, 'text': ' technology suitable for each die — as opposed to integrating all functionality at a large die level with the most technologically advanced fabrication node necessary to achieve the most demanding function within the device,” said Dave Fromm, COO of\nPromex (https://semiengineering.com/entities/promex-industries/)\n.\nMany companies today employ proprietary schemes to move data more efficiently from die to die. That qualifies such dies as chiplets for many people. For those that want standardization,\nUCIe (https://semiengineering.com/knowledge_centers/communications-io/on-chip-communications/universal-chiplet-interconnect-express-ucie/)\nand\nBunch of Wires (https://semiengineering.com/knowledge_centers/communications-io/on-chip-communications/bunch-of-wires-bow/)\n(BoW) add that imprimatur, and uptake has begun.\nThe issue of standards is important mostly for the broad notion of chiplets being available in an open market in the way IP is today. Interoperability becomes critical to ensure that any chiplets purchased from different sources will play well together. And for some, this is really what a chiplet is about, and it’s why they feel a standardized interface is necessary to qualify as a chiplet.\nVariations on the theme\nA slight relaxation of this approach comes from Arm, which focuses more on disaggregation than the interface. “A chiplet is an unpackaged silicon die designed to be combined and packaged with other chiplets'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 4, 'start_token': 1000, 'end_token': 1300, 'text': ' a chiplet.\nVariations on the theme\nA slight relaxation of this approach comes from Arm, which focuses more on disaggregation than the interface. “A chiplet is an unpackaged silicon die designed to be combined and packaged with other chiplets and operate as part of a system on chip (SoC),” said Mark Knight, director of architecture product management at\nArm (https://semiengineering.com/entities/arm/)\n. “Chiplets create larger, more complex systems that can be packaged and sold as a single component, with each chiplet being optimized for a specific function or task. This eliminates the need to build one large monolithic die, which can present cost-efficiency challenges and cause yield-related economic issues.”\nThe chiplet-market notion complicates things, and Synopsys makes a distinction based on that. “Because ‘chiplet’ was also used to describe this open chiplet marketplace, we started using the term ‘multi-die’ because it goes across all the markets and use cases,” said Posner. Given this definition, only a die designed for that open market would be a chiplet. But he still sees having a die-to-die interface as necessary.\nAnother view focuses on the role the die plays in the package rather than the interfaces. If it’s independent, then it’s not a chiplet. “Chiplets are modular,” said Pratyush Kamal, director, central engineering solutions at\nSiemens EDA (https://semiengineering.com/entities'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 5, 'start_token': 1250, 'end_token': 1550, 'text': ' package rather than the interfaces. If it’s independent, then it’s not a chiplet. “Chiplets are modular,” said Pratyush Kamal, director, central engineering solutions at\nSiemens EDA (https://semiengineering.com/entities/mentor-a-siemens-business/)\n. “Different chiplets designed to perform specific functions are combined together on an interposer within a single package.”\nBut specific functions are not necessarily standalone functions. “It requires the die to be packaged with other functions,” said Synopsys’ Posner. “It doesn’t necessarily mean it’s small. We are seeing reticle-size dies being packaged together.”\nBy this definition, a set of chiplets must be regarded together and cannot be considered individually to be complete functions. “When you look at boot, debug, test, the clock, or power management, you have to look at the multiple entities together,” said Kamal. “You can’t look at them in isolation.”\nAnd internal latency must be nominal. “Die-to-die latency has to be a small portion of your total latency,” he said. “Only then you could say that it’s tightly coupled with another entity.”\nAlthough packaging would seem to be an important part of the chiplet notion, even that isn’t viewed as being universally required. “The industry is leaning toward putting the essential constraint that a chiplet has to be co-packaged,” said Kamal. “I don’t necessarily agree with that, because you could have'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 6, 'start_token': 1500, 'end_token': 1800, 'text': ' notion, even that isn’t viewed as being universally required. “The industry is leaning toward putting the essential constraint that a chiplet has to be co-packaged,” said Kamal. “I don’t necessarily agree with that, because you could have a system where it may be economical to have the two chiplets directly mounted on the board.”\nWhat’s a chip?\nRelated to this discussion is what we should call that little piece of silicon. Is it a die or a chip? With one die per package, that was never a question. Now multiple components are being packaged together, is the “chip” the die or the packaged unit? Many people are assigning “die” to the single piece of silicon, while “chip” is a packaged unit, regardless of the contents.\n“Normally, a chip is something tested and packaged, ready to be shipped and assembled into devices,” said Pax Wang, director for advanced packages at\nUMC (https://semiengineering.com/entities/umc/)\n. “However, a chiplet refers to different functional chips (or dies) tested and packaged into the same chip.”\nFig. 1: Chip vs. chiplet. The left illustrates a chip where ‘chip’ is defined by some as a packaged product ready to solder to a PCB. The right shows a collection of components, some of which are chiplets, being integrated into the same package. Source: UMC\nThat distinction isn’t driven by technical considerations, but by convenience — we'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 7, 'start_token': 1750, 'end_token': 2050, 'text': ' packaged product ready to solder to a PCB. The right shows a collection of components, some of which are chiplets, being integrated into the same package. Source: UMC\nThat distinction isn’t driven by technical considerations, but by convenience — we need terms for these things. The challenge is when not everyone gets the memo on the names. It also conflicts with the older multi-chip module terminology, where “chip” refers to a die, so even here clarity is tough to achieve.\nn this basis, some people prefer the term “dielet” to “chiplet,” because what we call chiplets don’t look like smaller versions of a packaged circuit. “When developing wafer stacking services for customers, I believe ‘dielet’ could be better than ‘chiplet’ in some circumstances,” said Wang. But “chiplet” sounds better than “dielet,” so for the time being, any of us who aren’t fans of “chiplet” are stuck with it.\nAnalog and optical complicate things\nThings get more complicated when an analog die is included in the package. The standardized interfaces are digital and apply to any control signals or digital data being converted to or from analog. Analog signals obviously would not make use of any such interfaces. If the analog signals drive or are driven solely by external signals, then it has no need for any special internal interface. If analog is driving another chiplet, however, that connection would need to be custom-designed and custom-'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 8, 'start_token': 2000, 'end_token': 2300, 'text': ' of any such interfaces. If the analog signals drive or are driven solely by external signals, then it has no need for any special internal interface. If analog is driving another chiplet, however, that connection would need to be custom-designed and custom-verified.\nSo if the litmus test for a chiplet is the existence of the standardized interface, then an analog die would qualify as a chiplet only if it had those digital signals. If those signals provide control rather than data, it’s unlikely it would require the bandwidth of a standardized interface. Whether it still made sense to employ that interface or something simpler would be a decision for the architects.\nBut this is where things get a little funky. If one accepts the standardized interface as sacrosanct, then some analog chips may not qualify as chiplets. If any die-to-die interface qualifies without requiring a standard, then more analog chips would qualify.\nPhotonic chips have the same potential issue, although it’s probably too early to worry about it. A purely photonic chip will have no electrical interfaces. So can it still be considered a photonic chiplet?\nThis is where these definitions run into a general test. If some criterion — such as a standardized interface — must be satisfied to qualify as a chiplet, with some dies having it and some not, does it make a difference? If some hypothetical analog chip had no digital signals and therefore didn’t qualify as a chiplet, does that mean it can’t be integrated into'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 9, 'start_token': 2250, 'end_token': 2550, 'text': ' to qualify as a chiplet, with some dies having it and some not, does it make a difference? If some hypothetical analog chip had no digital signals and therefore didn’t qualify as a chiplet, does that mean it can’t be integrated into a package? Of course not. So that’s where debating these definitions can help communication, but living rigorously by them may not be as important.\nTable 1: Summary of chiplet definitions from our interviews. The most common one requires a die-to-die interface. Other definitions are more scattered. Source: Semiconductor Engineering\nHeterogeneous integration is messier\nHeterogeneous integration is also a new concept. “For 30 or 40, years, we’ve gone toward monolithic design, and there were huge advantages,” said Swinnen. “If you have multiple cores and put them all on one SoC, they would be faster, smaller, and cheaper, and they would have lower power. That still exists. The only reason you might not be able to do that is that the chip gets too big.”\nAnother factor is simply the cost of advanced nodes and the desire to avoid that cost if it doesn’t add value. “Certain chips (e.g. memory, CPUs, GPUs) aren’t valued if they don’t ‘keep up,’ and those are the ones that migrate to the leading edge,” said David Park, vice president of marketing at\nTignis (https://semiengineering.com/entities/tignis/)\n'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 10, 'start_token': 2500, 'end_token': 2800, 'text': ', GPUs) aren’t valued if they don’t ‘keep up,’ and those are the ones that migrate to the leading edge,” said David Park, vice president of marketing at\nTignis (https://semiengineering.com/entities/tignis/)\n. “But other chips don’t need to migrate to a smaller process node.”\nVerification and processing considerations also play a role. 2.5D integration gets much of the attention these days, but 3D can compound integration issues. “Integration challenges emerge when you try to connect different 3D components,” noted Benjamin Vincent, worldwide senior manager of semiconductor process and integration at\nLam Research (https://semiengineering.com/entities/lam-research/)\n, in a blog post. Those challenges can motivate the “heterogeneous” distinction.\nWhereas varying definitions of “chiplet” are close, there’s more diversity in how engineers view heterogeneous integration. They all account for multiple things in a package, but there are different “lines” for defining it as heterogeneous. “I don’t see there’s a very clear definition for this,” noted Wang. Those lines escalate as follows:\nHaving multiple items in the package is enough.\nIt can simply be two of the same die. “Heterogeneous integration is typically dies with different functions being packaged together,” said Posner. “But part of that could also be compute scaling. [Having four of the same die] would still be heterogeneous integration.”\nMultiple dies must be different.\n“It can'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 11, 'start_token': 2750, 'end_token': 3050, 'text': 'eterogeneous integration is typically dies with different functions being packaged together,” said Posner. “But part of that could also be compute scaling. [Having four of the same die] would still be heterogeneous integration.”\nMultiple dies must be different.\n“It can be easier to define the opposite of heterogeneous integration first,” said Knight. “A system composed of several identical CPU chiplets is not heterogeneous. A system composed of different chiplet types is a heterogeneous system.” Promex hews to a similar definition: “We define heterogeneous integration as a methodology for assembling diverse components — electronic and non-electronic — into a single compact device,” said Fromm.\nDifferent dies must be independently designed, as opposed to one coordinated project.\nThose dies may or may not come from the same company.\nPackages must contain a mix of process nodes.\n“My personal definition is, if two chiplets come from the same node, that’s homogeneous integration,” said Wang. “If we connect DRAM to logic, it definitely leads to wafers that come from different nodes, so this is heterogeneous integration.”\nPackages must contain a mix of advanced and mature nodes.\n“We can integrate an old technology node for high voltage for actuators or sensors together with 5nm processing elements,” said Heinig. “This is what we understand as heterogeneous integration in Europe. If Intel or AMD is combining 7nm with 12nm, it’s not heterogeneous for us because they don’t face all the same problems.”\nPackages must integrate different materials'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 12, 'start_token': 3000, 'end_token': 3300, 'text': ' Heinig. “This is what we understand as heterogeneous integration in Europe. If Intel or AMD is combining 7nm with 12nm, it’s not heterogeneous for us because they don’t face all the same problems.”\nPackages must integrate different materials.\n“DARPA focuses on the material,” said Kamal. “Even something as fundamental as a silicon interposer with a plastic substrate is heterogeneous integration.”\nEither mixed nodes or mixed materials will qualify.\n“Early on, many participants thought of heterogeneous integration as different silicon nodes used in a multi-chip or chiplet solution,” said Melissa Grupen-Shemansky, CTO and vice president of technology communities at\nSEMI (https://semiengineering.com/entities/semi/)\n. “Some technical professionals still see it as the integration of chips from different nodes. But it can also include chips of different materials, like silicon and germanium, and compound semiconductors like GaN and InP.”\nSome of those considerations are relatively brittle. For instance, given the requirement for different nodes, then if a package contains a 22nm die and a 12nm die, it’s heterogeneous. If at some point in the future the 22nm die is upgraded to 12nm, now it’s no longer heterogeneous by that definition.\nHow does the definition help?\nSo what is the “heterogeneous” distinction supposed to convey? In this example, nothing really changes in the assembly process. It’s just that one die'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 13, 'start_token': 3250, 'end_token': 3550, 'text': 'nm, now it’s no longer heterogeneous by that definition.\nHow does the definition help?\nSo what is the “heterogeneous” distinction supposed to convey? In this example, nothing really changes in the assembly process. It’s just that one die was updated. It’s not clear that changing from heterogeneous to homogeneous provides any substantive clarity.\n“Even if they share the same node, if they have totally different functions, totally different design concepts, and totally separate roadmaps, I would call it heterogeneous integration,” Wang said.\nHere again, analog complicates things. “Analog doesn’t use advanced nodes,” Swinnen said. “There are so many parasitic effects that come out of the woodwork at 5nm that it becomes difficult to meet spec.”\nOne view of the heterogeneous distinction is the understanding that the whole package must be simulated together to ensure a product that will perform and yield as desired.\n“If you have 100 V on one chiplet, you have to make sure it doesn’t couple into your 5nm subsystem,” said Heinig. “That’s a multi-physical problem. A second problem that we see is that, if you look at BoW and UCIe, the interface voltage is around 0.7 or 0.8\xa0V. This works very well for 7nm, 10nm, or 22nm, but if you go to 65 or 90nm, your supply voltage is 3\xa0V or 5\xa0V.'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 14, 'start_token': 3500, 'end_token': 3800, 'text': '7 or 0.8\xa0V. This works very well for 7nm, 10nm, or 22nm, but if you go to 65 or 90nm, your supply voltage is 3\xa0V or 5\xa0V. Then, the 0.8\xa0V on the interface doesn’t work because you are not reaching the threshold voltage of the mature technology.”\nThe necessary simulation grows with additional materials, because multi-physics simulation will confirm thermal performance, reliability, noise, signal quality, and any other considerations for a robust product. “You could have a subsystem spanning multiple technologies,” said Kamal. “You need to extract and simulate that as a whole, and that’s why we need this emphasis on heterogeneity.”\nRounding out the discussion, IEEE publishes a heterogeneous integration roadmap, and it aligns with the most lenient definition: “Heterogeneous integration refers to the integration of separately manufactured components into a higher-level assembly that, in the aggregate, provides enhanced functionality and improved operating characteristics.”\nTable 2: Summary of heterogeneous-integration definitions from SE interviews. Responses are widely spread among the criteria. Source: Semiconductor Engineering\nSo who cares?\nOne can argue subtleties of definitions, but will that effort have a payoff? Generally, a definition is useful if it yields information that helps decision-making. For chiplets, the value of the definition depends on the context. A company doing its own SoC disintegration can use its own nomenclature.\nBut'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 15, 'start_token': 3750, 'end_token': 4050, 'text': ' a payoff? Generally, a definition is useful if it yields information that helps decision-making. For chiplets, the value of the definition depends on the context. A company doing its own SoC disintegration can use its own nomenclature.\nBut if a chiplet market emerges, interoperability hinges on a purchased product qualifying as a chiplet. Of course, if someone sells a “chiplet” on the open market — one that doesn’t have a die-to-die interface but otherwise works — is that really a problem? As long as the die is well documented, probably not.\nHeterogeneous integration may be another matter. Today, everyone is developing their own flow for advanced packaging, and those flows differ from each other specifically because these companies are trying to differentiate their processes. Moreover, many projects proceed individually rather than benefiting from a recipe.\nBut if those recipes start to separate into two distinct tiers — one for homogeneous and one for heterogeneous — then the designation will determine the recipe. Ideally, all companies engaged in packaging would agree on the same definition so that planners shopping around can make apples-to-apples comparisons rather than having to wade through varying definitions.\nClearly the chip industry is not there yet, so for now the discussion has most value as an opportunity to think about what the most appropriate definitions are. With luck, such cogitations will start to align as practical implications become more evident.\nRelated Reading\nAdvanced Packaging Fundamentals for Semiconductor Engineers (https://semiengineering.com/ebook-'}, {'source_filename': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration_.html', 'title': 'What Exactly Are Chiplets And Heterogeneous Integration?', 'chunk_id': 16, 'start_token': 4000, 'end_token': 4217, 'text': ' value as an opportunity to think about what the most appropriate definitions are. With luck, such cogitations will start to align as practical implications become more evident.\nRelated Reading\nAdvanced Packaging Fundamentals for Semiconductor Engineers (https://semiengineering.com/ebook-advanced-packaging-fundamentals-for-semiconductor-engineers-2025/)\nNew SE eBook examines the next phase of semiconductor design, testing, and manufacturing.\nNavigating Increased Complexity In Advanced Packaging (https://semiengineering.com/precision-under-pressure-navigating-increased-complexity-in-advanced-packaging/)\nVariability is a growing challenge; achieving higher yields requires even tighter control and precision.\nSignal Integrity Plays Increasingly Critical Role In Chiplet Design (https://semiengineering.com/signal-integrity-plays-increasingly-critical-role-in-chiplet-design/)\nChiplet design engineers have complex new considerations compared to PCB concepts.\nWhat Exactly Is Multi-Physics? (https://semiengineering.com/what-exactly-is-multi-physics/)\nThe chip industry’s new buzzword comes with lots of implications and some vague definitions.'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': 'electronics\nArticle\nChiplet Heterogeneous Integration\nTechnology—Status and Challenges\nTao Li 1, Jie Hou 2, Jinli Yan 1,*, Rulin Liu 1, Hui Yang 1 and Zhigang Sun 1\n1 Computer College, National University of Defense Technology, Changsha 410073, China;\ntaoli@nudt.edu.cn (T.L.); rl_liu@nudt.edu.cn (R.L.); yanghui@nudt.edu.cn (H.Y.);\nsunzhigang@nudt.edu.cn (Z.S.)\n2 Naval Research Academy, Beijing 100000, China; hlwang@nudt.edu.cn\n* Correspondence: yanjinli10@nudt.edu.cn\n(cid:1)(cid:2)(cid:3)(cid:1)(cid:4)(cid:5)(cid:6)(cid:7)(cid:8)(cid:1)\n(cid:1)(cid:2)(cid:3)(cid:4)(cid:5)(cid:6)(cid:7)\nReceived: 19 March 2020; Accepted: 14 April 2020; Published: 20 April 2020\nAbstract: As a heterogeneous integration technology, the chiplet-based design technology integrates\nmultiple heterogeneous dies of diverse functional circuit blocks into a single chip by using\nadvanced packaging technology, which is a promising way to tackle the failure of Moore’s law'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': '0\nAbstract: As a heterogeneous integration technology, the chiplet-based design technology integrates\nmultiple heterogeneous dies of diverse functional circuit blocks into a single chip by using\nadvanced packaging technology, which is a promising way to tackle the failure of Moore’s law and\nDennard scaling. Currently, as process nodes move forward, dramatically rising cost, design cycle,\nand complexity are driving industry to focus on the chiplets. Chiplets allows IC designers to merge\ndies fabricated at different process nodes and reuse them in different projects, which helps to reduce\nthe cost during design and improve yield. In this review, we look back at the industry’s efforts over\nthe past decade and summary the concepts and techniques associated with chiplets. In the end,\na discussion and conclusion will be given to forecast the future of chiplets.\nKeywords: chip integration; chip package; heterogeneous; IP integration; EDA\n1. Introduction\nAfter decades of rapid development, large-scale integrated circuits have become the backbone\nof information technologies. In the past, the integrated circuits (IC) fabricating process continues\nto develop along with Moore’s Law, 7 nm-process has now entered the production stage, 5 nm and\n3 nm process is also steadily advancing. Every breakthrough in the process node leads to a better\nperformance and lower power consumption [1]. However, as the complexity of the IC manufacturing\nprocess increases dramatically, the cost of tape-out rises greatly. For example, the design cost is'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 2, 'start_token': 500, 'end_token': 800, 'text': ' advancing. Every breakthrough in the process node leads to a better\nperformance and lower power consumption [1]. However, as the complexity of the IC manufacturing\nprocess increases dramatically, the cost of tape-out rises greatly. For example, the design cost is 300M\nfor building a new graphics processing unit (GPU), central processing unit (CPU) or system on chip\n(SoC) [2], which brings great challenges for the design of the chips in multiple fields.\nThe slowdown and stagnation of Moore’s law [3] and Dennard scaling [4] exacerbate this problem.\nTo be specific, Moore’s Law shows signs of slowdown since 2000. The gap between predictions of\nMoore’s Law and the actual performance of chips grew by 15 times by 2008. Dennard scaling began to\nslowdownsignificantlyin2007andnearlylapsedin2012. Theimprovementofperformanceandpower\nconsumption in the chip becomes less cost-effective with the upgrade of the IC manufacturing process.\nIndustry and academia generally believe that the Post Moore’s Era [5] will come soon, which means\nlonger-term research will focus on the More Moore technologies and efforts should be made in every\naspect of semiconductor industry along with scaling process such as design, device architectures,\npackage process, even new devices beyond-CMOS to sustain power, performance, area and cost (PPAC)\nscaling [6].\nAs efforts towards integration technology under the'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 3, 'start_token': 750, 'end_token': 1050, 'text': '\naspect of semiconductor industry along with scaling process such as design, device architectures,\npackage process, even new devices beyond-CMOS to sustain power, performance, area and cost (PPAC)\nscaling [6].\nAs efforts towards integration technology under the More Moore framework, chiplet-based\ndesign technology has gained widespread attention for solving the above problems from three aspects.\nFirstly, chiplets try to integrate multiple modular chips (the main form of modular chips is die.) into a\nElectronics2020,9,670;doi:10.3390/electronics9040670 www.mdpi.com/journal/electronics\nElectronics2020,9,670 2of12\nsingle package for developing dedicated heterogeneous chips via internal interconnection technology.\nThis solution solves the problems of scale, development costs and period efficiently. Secondly, with\n2.5D, 3D and other advanced packaging technologies, chiplets achieves high-performance multi-chip\non-chip interconnection, which improves the integration of the chip system and the optimizations\nof performance and power consumption. Finally, modular integration not only accelerates the\ndevelopment speed effectively, but also reduces the development cost and the threshold. Therefore, the\nresearch and development of chips focus on algorithms and core technologies, improving the overall\ninnovation level and capabilities greatly.\nCompared to the traditional monolithic integration method, chiplets have advantages and\npotentials in many aspects. However, there are many challenges for promoting'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 4, 'start_token': 1000, 'end_token': 1300, 'text': ' of chips focus on algorithms and core technologies, improving the overall\ninnovation level and capabilities greatly.\nCompared to the traditional monolithic integration method, chiplets have advantages and\npotentials in many aspects. However, there are many challenges for promoting the maturity and\nwidespread application of chiplets. Firstly, the unified interfaces and standards are essential for\nheterogeneous integrated systems. The design of interconnect interfaces and standards for diverse\nheterogeneous chips face difficulties in balancing performance and flexibility in terms of technology\nand competitions for market domination. Secondly, the core packaging technologies of chiplets face\nchallenges in performance, power consumption, cost, and so forth. Thirdly, a critical issue to be solved\nfor chiplets is whether the electronics design automation (EDA) toolchains and ecological system that\nsupports the design and implementation of chips is complete and sustainable.\nCurrently, chiplets have been successfully applied in industry, especially companies with high-end\ntechnologies and research capabilities. HBM memory [7] is a typical representation of early successful\napplication. Since then, in the FPGA-related fields, Intel company introduced the Agilex field\nprogrammable gate array (FPGA) productions based on chiplet technology. These productions use\n3D packaging technology to achieve heterogeneous chip integration. In the field of high-performance\nCPU chips, AMD introduced Zen 2 architecture [8] that separates IO components and processor cores\ninto multiple small chips (using 7 nm'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 5, 'start_token': 1250, 'end_token': 1550, 'text': ' These productions use\n3D packaging technology to achieve heterogeneous chip integration. In the field of high-performance\nCPU chips, AMD introduced Zen 2 architecture [8] that separates IO components and processor cores\ninto multiple small chips (using 7 nm, 14 nm and other process) for further on-demand integration [9].\nIn the field of networking, Intel (formerly Barefoot)’s Tofino2 chip, with 12.8 T switching capability,\nis implemented via chiplets. It integrates the switching logic chip with a high-speed SerDes chip.\nRecently, in academia, University of California, Georgia Institute of Technology, and European research\ninstitutions [10,11] begin to research the chiplet-related problems, including the interconnect interfaces,\npackaging, and applications.\nThese above researches mainly focus on independent products or local technologies. Differently,\nthe CHIPS plan (Generic Heterogeneous Integration and IP Reuse Strategy [12]) launched by DARPA\n(US Department of Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency) in 2017 attempts to push chiplets to\nthe level of strategic unification and ecological construction. In DARPA’s planning, chiplets involves\ndies with different function from different companies, different process node, different semiconductor\nmaterials, different signal types (i.e., wave, electron, photon, even microelectromechanical system).\nThus, chiplet technology is designed to support a huge technical roadmap in new ecology and\napplication'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 6, 'start_token': 1500, 'end_token': 1800, 'text': ', different process node, different semiconductor\nmaterials, different signal types (i.e., wave, electron, photon, even microelectromechanical system).\nThus, chiplet technology is designed to support a huge technical roadmap in new ecology and\napplication system.\nDespite the widespread attention of the chiplet technology, the review article about chiplets is\nscarce and urgently needed. In this paper, we analyze the existing research and applications about\nchiplets in detail and propose future development trends. We wish that our efforts could provide\nreferences for researchers engaged in the research and design of the next-generation chips.\n2. Overview of Chiplets\nTraditionally, there are two ways for IC designers to develop their next generation IC productions.\nThe mainstream method is directly transporting old design into smaller process node to obtain higher\ndevice frequency for better PPA. The second way is to incorporate more functional blocks under same\nprocess node to reduce the cost in mask and EDA tools [13].\nHowever, with the evolution of chip manufacturing process, the overall chip design cost\nhas increased significantly due to the more complicated process and more difficult design and\nimplementation. According to the survey from the International Business Strategy Corporation\nElectronics2020,9,670 3of12\n(IBS), the increase of design cost for each generation technology has exceeded 50% after 22 nm\nprocess, including EDA, design verification, IP core, tape-out, and so forth. For instance, the total'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 7, 'start_token': 1750, 'end_token': 2050, 'text': '3of12\n(IBS), the increase of design cost for each generation technology has exceeded 50% after 22 nm\nprocess, including EDA, design verification, IP core, tape-out, and so forth. For instance, the total\ndesign cost of 7 nm process is about 300 million dollars, and that of 3 nm process is expected\nto increase 5 times up to 1.5 billion dollars [2], as depicted in Figure 1. Thus, the difficulties for\nimplementing a high-performance chip upgrade based on process improvement are increasing and\nthe price-performance ratio is increasing. Furthermore, due to the technical limitations in yield (such\nas the mask size of lithography machine), the existing monolithic integration becomes unsustainable\nwith new process for upgrading and expanding the functions and performance.\nFigure 1. Chip Design and Manufacturing Cost under Different Process Nodes: Data Source from\nIBS [2].\nIn such a scenario, chiplets provide a feasible way for future chip design. Multi-Chip Modules\n(MCM) technology [14], which appeared in the 1980s, has already embodied the concept of\nchiplets. MCM technology connects multiple chips on a substrate or other medium for meeting the\nperformance and functional requirements of complex system chips. MCM could reduce the overhead\nof board-level interconnection and the complexity of board-level system design, which saves the cost\nof building a system greatly. Recently, Intel, AMD,'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 8, 'start_token': 2000, 'end_token': 2300, 'text': ' meeting the\nperformance and functional requirements of complex system chips. MCM could reduce the overhead\nof board-level interconnection and the complexity of board-level system design, which saves the cost\nof building a system greatly. Recently, Intel, AMD, and other companies have developed a series of\nhigh-performance chip products based on MCM technology. However, MCM mainly focuses on the\nunderlying packaging technology. And it does not consider the high-level problems on heterogeneous\nintegration of chip, including multi-level interconnection standards, interfaces, tools, and ecology.\nIn 2017, DARPA planned the “General Heterogeneous Integration and IP Reuse Strategy [12]”\n(CHIPS) in the “Electronic Revival Plan”. This project attempts to utilize the industrial and academic\nforces to solve the above problems. The participants consist of the system integration vendors\n(Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, Boeing, Intel, Micron, etc.), EDA vendors (Cadence, Synopsys,\netc.) and research institutions (University of Michigan, Georgia Institute of Technology and North\nCarolina State University, etc.). This project emphasizes on developing a new technology framework\nthat incorporates chip dies with different functions, then matches and combines them onto the\ninterposer. It not only integrates dies into on-chip systems at a lower cost, but also enhances the\noverall flexibility and reduces the design time of next-generation products.\nThe Open Compute Project (OCP) promoted by'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 9, 'start_token': 2250, 'end_token': 2550, 'text': ' combines them onto the\ninterposer. It not only integrates dies into on-chip systems at a lower cost, but also enhances the\noverall flexibility and reduces the design time of next-generation products.\nThe Open Compute Project (OCP) promoted by Facebook and other companies also actively\nlaunched the Open Domain-Specific Architecture (ODSA) [15] research at the end of 2018. It tries to\ndevelop a complete architecture interface stack and to create a chiplet open market. By defining open\nstandardized interfaces, the die integrated in Chiplet chips could interoperate to support the flexible\ncombination from different vendors for building more flexible chip systems.\nTo achieve these above goals, the research and development of software toolchains and the\ntypical applications will be the important problems to be addressed for further development of\nchiplets. To be specific, the software toolchains includes the full-stack feasible interconnection interface\nElectronics2020,9,670 4of12\nspecifications and standards at the physical layer, link layer, and network layer, advanced chip\npackaging technologies, yield-oriented EDA, and so forth.\n3. Advantages of Chiplets\nCompared to the traditional printed circuit board (PCB) board integration and monolithic ASIC\nintegration, the advantages of chiplets mainly reflected in technology, development cost, and business.\nTechnology. Chiplets improve the optimization potentials on performance and power\nconsumptionbyrecombiningmultiplesmallch'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 10, 'start_token': 2500, 'end_token': 2800, 'text': 'B) board integration and monolithic ASIC\nintegration, the advantages of chiplets mainly reflected in technology, development cost, and business.\nTechnology. Chiplets improve the optimization potentials on performance and power\nconsumptionbyrecombiningmultiplesmallchips. Thus, itsupportsthedomain-specificcustomization\nand mitigating the effect from the slowdown of Moore’s law for developing diverse chips. For instance,\nhigh-speed SerDes has higher requirements on power consumption for the network chips with\nhigh-density and high-speed interfaces. The network chips based on chiplet technology seperate\nthe high-speed SerDes IO module from the core logic, providing more layout options for the\npower consumption. This is also an important reason why chiplet technology is applied in Intel’s\nprogrammableswitchingchipTofino2. Besides, thememoryaccessbandwidthisusuallyaperformance\nbottleneck for high-performance CPUs and AI chips. Chiplets improve the signal transmission quality\nand bandwidth and mitigates the "storage wall" issue by combining the processor core and memory\nchips with 3D stacking technology. This is the key point that AMD and Intel focus and adopt chiplets\nin the early days.\nDevelopment Cost. Chiplets generally integrate multiple small dies into a large monolithic chip\nwith advanced packaging processes. The features of low cost and high yield in small chips that occupy\nrelatively low area could reduce the overall cost effectively [16]. In addition to the chip manufacturing\n'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 11, 'start_token': 2750, 'end_token': 3050, 'text': ' integrate multiple small dies into a large monolithic chip\nwith advanced packaging processes. The features of low cost and high yield in small chips that occupy\nrelatively low area could reduce the overall cost effectively [16]. In addition to the chip manufacturing\ncost, the research and development cost gradually takes up a great proportion of the overall chip cost.\nBy combining the known good dies (KGD) directly, the research and development cycle is greatly\nreduced and the relative investment is saved. The development and manufacturing cost of AMD’s\n32 core EPYC CPU is reduced by 40% with chiplets [17]. Besides, the large-scale high-performance\nchips, especially commercial chips, usually become mature products and come into market after\nmultiple silicon verifications in traditional monolithic integration. This solution generally results\nin great pressure on the research and development cost. On the contrary, the chips developed with\nchiplets usually select the widely used and mature chip dies to integrate, reducing the development\nrisk of chips. Thus, the number of re-spins and packaging is decreased and the cost is saved effectively.\nBusiness. Chiplets could effectively improve the speed of chip development and reduce the\nrelated cost and barriers. Thus, scientific research and commercial institutions pay much attention to\nthe core algorithms and technologies, which promotes technological innovation effectively. Besides, the\ncontinuous evolution and improvement of the chiplet ecosystem will accelerate the generation of new\nindustries. The business model of chiplets may'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 12, 'start_token': 3000, 'end_token': 3300, 'text': ' commercial institutions pay much attention to\nthe core algorithms and technologies, which promotes technological innovation effectively. Besides, the\ncontinuous evolution and improvement of the chiplet ecosystem will accelerate the generation of new\nindustries. The business model of chiplets may generate three types of business roles, including chiplet\nsuppliers that supply chiplet modular chips, chiplet integrators that integrate chiplet modular chips to\nform a complete system, and EDA softwares that provide the tool chains and design automation\nservices. At present, Intel, Micron and other corporations begin to play important roles in the\nindustry chain while startups (like zGlue [18]) focus on opening up the missing links in the chiplet\nindustry chain.\nTable 1 shows the comparisons between chiplet technology and traditional technologies. To be\nspecific, chiplets are very close to a monolithic ASIC chip in terms of performance, power consumption\nand integration. With respect to the cost and design cycle, there is only a small gap between chiplets\nand traditional PCB technology. In summary, the chiplet makes a good tradeoff between monolithic\nASIC and PCB technology and has great development potential.\nElectronics2020,9,670 5of12\nTable 1. Comparisons between Chiplet Technology and Traditional Chip Integration Technologies.\nIntegration Technology Chiplet Monolithic ASIC PCB\nPerformance Higher, on-demand selection Highest Low\nPower Consumption Lower, on-demand selection Lowest High\nIntegration Higher Highest Low\n'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 13, 'start_token': 3250, 'end_token': 3550, 'text': '\nTable 1. Comparisons between Chiplet Technology and Traditional Chip Integration Technologies.\nIntegration Technology Chiplet Monolithic ASIC PCB\nPerformance Higher, on-demand selection Highest Low\nPower Consumption Lower, on-demand selection Lowest High\nIntegration Higher Highest Low\nCost($) [18] Thousands-millions Millions Hundreds-thousands\nDesign Cycle [18] Months 12+ Months Weeks\n4. Challenges of Chiplets\nAlthough chiplets have many advantages as mentioned above, there are still many challenges\nfor further development, including interconnect interfaces and protocols, packaging technology and\nquality control.\n4.1. Interconnect Interfaces and Protocols\nThe interconnect interfaces and protocols among chiplets is very critical for the development\nof chiplets. The design of interfaces and protocols must consider the requirements of matching the\nmanufacturing process and packaging technology, system integration, and expansion. Besides, the\nrelated performance indexes of chiplets in different fields are also crucial, such as the transmission\nbandwidth per unit area, power consumption per bit. Generally, the above-mentioned factors are\ncontradictory, which brings greater challenges for the design of interconnected interfaces and protocols.\nThe interconnect interfaces used by chiplet technology in the physical layer could be divided into\nthe following categories.\n4.1.1. Serial Interfaces\nFrom the perspective of application transmission distance, serial interfaces consist of long\nreach/medium reach/very short reach SerDes (LR/MR/VSR SerDes), extremely short distance'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 14, 'start_token': 3500, 'end_token': 3800, 'text': '\nthe following categories.\n4.1.1. Serial Interfaces\nFrom the perspective of application transmission distance, serial interfaces consist of long\nreach/medium reach/very short reach SerDes (LR/MR/VSR SerDes), extremely short distance (XSR)\nSerDes and ultra-short distance (USR) SerDes. Figure 2 shows that the application scenarios in which\nthese interfaces are applied.\nLR Serdes XSR Serdes\nChip Chip\nBackplane/Passive Copper Cable Optics Chip\nMR Serdes Chip-to-Optical Engine\nChip Chip\nUSR Serdes\nChip-to-Chip/Middle Plane\nVSR Serdes\nChip Optics Optics Chip 2.5D Chip-to-Optical\n3D Stacking\nChip-to-Module Engine\n(a) (b)\nFigure2. ClassificationandApplicationofTypicalSerialInterfaces;(a)ClassificationofSerialInterfaces,\n(b) Application of Serial Interfaces.\nLR/MR/VSR SerDes are generally applied for the inter-chip and chip-to-module connections\nbased on PCB boards. They are widely used to implement communication interfaces such as PCI-E,\nEthernet, and RapidIO. The main features of these interfaces are reliable, long transmission distance,\nlow cost, and easy integration. However, since these interfaces have no advantages in power\nconsumption, area and delay, it is difficult to support the construction of high-performance chips that\nhas high requirements on these indexes above.\nElectronics202'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 15, 'start_token': 3750, 'end_token': 4050, 'text': ',\nlow cost, and easy integration. However, since these interfaces have no advantages in power\nconsumption, area and delay, it is difficult to support the construction of high-performance chips that\nhas high requirements on these indexes above.\nElectronics2020,9,670 6of12\nXSR SerDes provide related SerDes standards for the interconnection of Die-to-Die(D2D) and\nDie-to-Optical Engine (D2OE). The design of XSR SerDes mainly focuses on the interconnection of\nD2OE. It is developed based on traditional SerDes structure that integrates the clock data recovery\ncircuit (CDR) and has strict requirements on the cost of insertion loss. To achieve a lower bit error rate,\ncomplex forward error correction (FEC) mechanisms are necessary to be integrated. However, it will\nintroduce significant delay and power consumption. Especially when the bandwidth reaches 112 G or\nhigher, the overhead caused by signal reflection would introduce extra delay and power consumption.\nMoreover, in order to provide good signal integrity, high-performance chip process and packaging\nsubstrate materials are demanded to support large-scale integration. XSR is suitable to be deployed\nbetween optical devices and bare chips with end-to-end FEC.\nCompared to XSR, USR SerDes mainly focuses on implementing high-speed interconnect\ncommunication of Die-to-Die at ultra-short distance (10 mm level) via 2.5D/3D'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 16, 'start_token': 4000, 'end_token': 4300, 'text': ' chips with end-to-end FEC.\nCompared to XSR, USR SerDes mainly focuses on implementing high-speed interconnect\ncommunication of Die-to-Die at ultra-short distance (10 mm level) via 2.5D/3D packaging technology.\nSince the communication distance is short, USR provides better performance and power consumption\nratio and better scalability with advanced coding, multi-bit transmission, and other technologies.\nFor example, Kandou’s Glasswing 112 G USR SerDes using CNRZ-5 encoding could achieve 0.72 pJ/bit\npower consumption, and 224 G SerDes could achieve 0.8 pJ/bit [19]. Since the implementation of USR\ninterface generally involves patented technologies (such as encoding methods), its interoperability\ncompatibility faces greater challenges. Besides, the requirements of USR on the transmission distance\nimpede the integration of large-scale chiplets.\nAccording to the 56 G SerDes interface specification defined by Reference [20], a comprehensive\ncomparison of multiple interfaces is presented in terms of transmission, application, and other aspects,\nas shown in Table 2.\nTable 2. Comparisons among different types of OIF 56 G SerDes.\nSerDesType MR/LRSerDes VSRSerDes XSRSerDes USRSerDes\nDie-to-Die\nApplicationFields Inter-chip Chip-to-Module and Die-to-Die\nDie-to-OpticalEngine\nSubstrate'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 17, 'start_token': 4250, 'end_token': 4550, 'text': 'SerDesType MR/LRSerDes VSRSerDes XSRSerDes USRSerDes\nDie-to-Die\nApplicationFields Inter-chip Chip-to-Module and Die-to-Die\nDie-to-OpticalEngine\nSubstrate\nPCB PCB\nTransmissionMedium Substrate or\n(1–2connectors) (1connector)\nSiliconInterposer\nCodingScheme PAM4,ENRZ PAM4 PAM,NRZ NRZ(CNRZ-5)\n1E-41E-6 1E-6\nBirErrorrate 1E-101E-15 1E-101E-15\n(1E-101E-15withRS-FEC) (1E-101E-15withRS-FEC)\n125mm/25mm\nTransmissionDistance 500–1000mm 50mm 10mm\n(mainPCB/modularPCB)\nPowerConsumptionperbit - - 5pJ/bit 3pJ/bit\n4.1.2. Parallel Interfaces\nAt present, the generic parallel interfaces for the interconnections of chiplet dies are Intel’s\nAIB/MDIO [21], TSMC’s LIPINCON, OCP’s BoW [22], and so forth. HBM interface also belongs to\nthis type of interface and is dedicated to the interconnections among high-bandwidth storages.\nIntel’s AIB (Advanced Interface Bus), a'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 18, 'start_token': 4500, 'end_token': 4800, 'text': 'INCON, OCP’s BoW [22], and so forth. HBM interface also belongs to\nthis type of interface and is dedicated to the interconnections among high-bandwidth storages.\nIntel’s AIB (Advanced Interface Bus), a parallel interconnection standard in the physical layer,\nis similar to the DDR DRAM Interface. In DARPA’s CHIPS project, Intel provides free AIB interface\nlicenses to related vendors for supporting the broad chiplet ecosystem. As an upgraded version of AIB,\nMIDO provides higher transmission efficiency, and the response speed and bandwidth density are over\ntwo times than that of AIB. AIB and MDIO technologies are mainly suitable for 2.5D and 3D packaging\ntechnologies with short communication distance and low loss, such as EMIB [23], Foveros [24].\nLIPINCON [25] is a high-performance interconnect interface proposed by TSMC for chiplets.\nBy using advanced silicon-based interconnect packaging technologies (such as InFO and CoWoS) and\ntiming compensation technology, LIPINCON could reduce power consumption and area overhead\nElectronics2020,9,670 7of12\nwithout PLL/DLL. The LIPINCON interface contains two types of PHY: PHYC and PHYM. PHYC is\nused for SoC die while PHYM is for memory and transceiver dies.\nThe BoW interface proposed by the OCP ODSA group'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 19, 'start_token': 4750, 'end_token': 5050, 'text': ' The LIPINCON interface contains two types of PHY: PHYC and PHYM. PHYC is\nused for SoC die while PHYM is for memory and transceiver dies.\nThe BoW interface proposed by the OCP ODSA group focuses on solving the organic\nsubstrate-based parallel interconnection problems. There are three types of BoW, namely BoW-Base,\nBoW-Fast, and BoW-Turbo. BoW-Base is designed for the transmission distances below 10 mm and\nuses unterminated unidirectional interfaces. The data transmission rate of each line could reach\nup to 4 Gbps. With respect to BoW-Fast, it uses terminated interfaces and the cable length is up to\n50 mm. The transmission rate per line is 16 Gbps. Compared to BoW-Fast, Bow-Turbo uses two\nwires to support bidirectional 16 Gbps transmission bandwidth. Moreover, BoW supports backward\ncompatibility and has fewer restrictions on chip process and packaging technology. It does not rely on\nadvanced silicon-based interconnection packaging technology and has a wide range of applications.\nThese parallel interfaces of chiplets in the physical layer are compared in terms of encapsulation,\ntransmission rate and bandwidth density, as described in Table 3.\nThe above mentioned advanced electrical signal interface in the physical layer has achieved a\nlow power consumption on per-bit data transmission. Whereas, as the bandwidth requirements of\nhigh-performance network'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 20, 'start_token': 5000, 'end_token': 5300, 'text': ',\ntransmission rate and bandwidth density, as described in Table 3.\nThe above mentioned advanced electrical signal interface in the physical layer has achieved a\nlow power consumption on per-bit data transmission. Whereas, as the bandwidth requirements of\nhigh-performance network and computing grow dramatically, the increase in power consumption\ncaused by data transmission is still a critical challenge for chip development. Mark Wade et al.\nproposed to use the optoelectronics hybrid technology to solve IO bottlenecks, which provides new\nideas for the development of interconnect technologies and standards with high-performance and low\npower consumption.\nAll the above interface standards are designed based on the specific interconnection requirements\nand the optimal chiplet interconnection solution is related to specific applications. Although parallel\ninterfaces provide low power consumption, low latency, and high bandwidth, it requires more routing\nresources. On the contrary, serial interfaces require fewer routing resources, but it brings more power\nconsumption and delay. Therefore, chiplet designers must select one or more interfaces in the physical\nlayer for achieving the goal of system optimization according to the actual application requirements,\nconstraints and die features.\nTable 3. Comparisons among parallel interfaces of chiplets at the physical layer [22].\nParallelInterface AIBGen1 MDIOGen1 LIPINCON BoW-Turbo(3slices)\nDataRate(GT/s) 2 5.4 8 16\nShortlineBandwidthDensity(Gbps/mm) '}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 21, 'start_token': 5250, 'end_token': 5550, 'text': 'Interface AIBGen1 MDIOGen1 LIPINCON BoW-Turbo(3slices)\nDataRate(GT/s) 2 5.4 8 16\nShortlineBandwidthDensity(Gbps/mm) 504 1600 536 1280\nPHYPower(pJ/bit) 0.85 0.5 0.5 0.5(7nm)\nPackage EMIB EMIB/ODI CoWoS MCP\nArealBWDensity(GBps/mm2) 150 198 198 148\nTypicalApplications Stratix10FPGA - VLSIPresentation GFSample\n4.1.3. Other Interfaces\nIn principle, traditional interface standards (such as Ethernet MAC, PCIe, etc.) could be used\nfor the transmission of chiplets at the link layer by adapting to the underlying physical layer (PHY)\n. Tilelink interface protocol receives widespread attention for its openness and open-source mode.\nIt attempts to decouple the implementation of the on-chip network and cache controller from the cache\nconsistency protocol. Any cache consistency protocol that follows the Tilelink transaction structure\ncould be used in conjunction with any physical layer network and cache controller. The CCIX [26]\ninterface standard is designed specifically for the inter-chip accelerator structure. It supports the cache\nconsistency by extending functions in the transaction layer and protocol layer over the standard PCIe\ndata link layer. CCIX also'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 22, 'start_token': 5500, 'end_token': 5800, 'text': ' controller. The CCIX [26]\ninterface standard is designed specifically for the inter-chip accelerator structure. It supports the cache\nconsistency by extending functions in the transaction layer and protocol layer over the standard PCIe\ndata link layer. CCIX also supports flexible topologies and is mainly used for communication between\nthe main CPU and accelerators.\nIn order to improve the scalability of synchronous communication mechanisms (such as Tilelink,\nCCIX), an active participant of the OSDA project, namely Netronome, designed the ISF interface\nprotocol. ISF is a lightweight message protocol that supports asynchronous storage access and consists\nElectronics2020,9,670 8of12\nof transport layer, network layer, and link layer. ISF was originally used for the interconnections of\non-chip components on the NFP network stream processor of Netronome and It would support the\ninterconnection of Die-to-Die of chiplets.\nDifferent from the industry that closely researches chiplet-related interconnect standards\nand specifications, the academia mainly focuses on the design and optimizations of the NOC\narchitecture and algorithms between dies in the network layer. Besides, much attention is paid\nto the implementation of high-performance on-chip network communication based on the active\nintermediary layer [16,27,28].\n4.2. Packaging Technology\nThe physical implementation of die interconnection in chiplets depends on the completion of\nthe chip packaging process. The performance, cost'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 23, 'start_token': 5750, 'end_token': 6050, 'text': ' network communication based on the active\nintermediary layer [16,27,28].\n4.2. Packaging Technology\nThe physical implementation of die interconnection in chiplets depends on the completion of\nthe chip packaging process. The performance, cost, and maturity of multi-chip packaging technology\naffect the application of chiplets greatly. As shown in Figure 3, the packaging technologies that support\ninterconnections of chiplets could be divided into three types according to the difference of connection\nmedia and processes. They are substrate-based packaging technologies, silicon interposers-based\npackaging technologies and RDL-based (Redistribution Layer) fan-out packaging technology.\nDie 1 Die 2\nDie 1 Die 2\nInterposer\nSubstrate\nPackSaugbes tSrautbestrate\n(a) (b)\nPlastic\nDie 1 Die 2 R P D ad L s s Die Materials\nSilicon Bridge\nSubstrate\nSolder ball\n(c) (d)\nFigure 3. Description of Chiplet Packaging Technologies; (a) Substrate-based Packaging, (b) Silicon\nInterposer-based Packaging, (c) Silicon Bridge-based Packaging, (d) RDL-based Packaging.\nAt present, organic substrates are widely used due to the cost and other factors. Similar\nto the traditional PCB, the organic substrate materials complete the wiring connections with the\netching process, which does not depend on the silicon process used in the'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 24, 'start_token': 6000, 'end_token': 6300, 'text': 'At present, organic substrates are widely used due to the cost and other factors. Similar\nto the traditional PCB, the organic substrate materials complete the wiring connections with the\netching process, which does not depend on the silicon process used in the semiconductor equipment.\nMultiple dies could be connected with high density on the substrate through wire bonding or flip-chip\ntechnology. Since the substrate-based packaging method does not rely on the chip foundry process,\nthe related materials and production cost is low. With this method, the packaging size could be up\nto 110 mm × 110 mm (Land Grid Array Packaging, LGA) and it is widely used in large-scale chiplet\nsystems [29,30]. However, the density of IO pins is low with wiring bonding and flip-chip and most\npins of chip are occupied by the power supply. As a result, the pins used for data transmission are more\nscarce and the external bandwidth of the full chip is limited. Moreover, the crosstalk effect impedes\nthe improvement of the transmission capability of a single pin. Furthermore, these above problems\nwould also limit the transmission bandwidth of Die-to-Die connections and affect the development of\nchiplets with higher performance.\nThe 2.5D/3D packaging technologies are mainly in the form of silicon interposer-based packaging\ntechnology. The interconnection and communication between dies are implemented by placing an\nextra silicon layer between the substrate and die. The connection between'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 25, 'start_token': 6250, 'end_token': 6550, 'text': 'The 2.5D/3D packaging technologies are mainly in the form of silicon interposer-based packaging\ntechnology. The interconnection and communication between dies are implemented by placing an\nextra silicon layer between the substrate and die. The connection between die and substrate is achieved\nwith through-silicon vias (TSVs) and Micro-Bumps. Since micro-bumps and TSVs have smaller bump\npitch and trace distance, silicon interposer-based packaging technology provides a higher IO density\nand lower transmission delay and power consumption. However, compared to the organic substrate,\nElectronics2020,9,670 9of12\nthe implementation of silicon interposer provided by the chip foundry brings higher cost in materials\nand process. To solve this problem, Silicon Bridge technology attempts to combine the substrate-based\nand silicon interposer-based technology. It integrates small thin layers on the substrate for inter-die\ninterconnection (less than 75 um [22]), which makes a good balance between performance and cost.\nGenerally, there are two types of silicon interposers: one is a passive interposer that only contains the\nconnection circuits, and the other is an active interposer that not only contains the connection circuits\nbut also integrates the logic circuits. Although the implementation cost of active interposers is more\nexpensive, it could provide more flexible and extensible solutions than passive interposers [31,32].\nTherefore, academia focuses more on active interposers'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 26, 'start_token': 6500, 'end_token': 6800, 'text': ' also integrates the logic circuits. Although the implementation cost of active interposers is more\nexpensive, it could provide more flexible and extensible solutions than passive interposers [31,32].\nTherefore, academia focuses more on active interposers.\nThe substrate-less fan-out packaging technology based on the redistribution layer (RDL) deposits\nmetal and dielectric layers on the surface of the wafer. It forms a redistribution layer to carry the\ncorresponding metal wiring pattern and rearranges the IO ports of chips on the loose area outside\nthe die. In fan-out packaging technology, RDL could enhance the signal quality by shortening the\ncircuit length and improve the chiplet integration by reducing the area of chip. Moreover, fan-out\npackaging, as a substrate-less packaging method with lower vertical height, provides additional\nvertical space for more components to be stacked upward. Compared to the silicon interposer-based\npackaging technology, the cost of fan-out packaging is relatively low. But the wiring resources of\nfan-out packaging are limited by the RDL wiring level. Since the TSMC’s InFo (Integrated fan-out\npackaging) technology is applied in the A10 processor of Apple’s iPhone 7 successfully, fan-out\ntechnology has received extensive attention from packaging and testing factories and chip foundries.\nAt present, there are more than 10 fan-out packaging technologies in the market launched, providing\nabundant selections for chiplet integration.\nThe'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 27, 'start_token': 6750, 'end_token': 7050, 'text': ' successfully, fan-out\ntechnology has received extensive attention from packaging and testing factories and chip foundries.\nAt present, there are more than 10 fan-out packaging technologies in the market launched, providing\nabundant selections for chiplet integration.\nThe comparisons of packaging technologies available for chiplet integration are shown in Table 4.\nWith the continuous development of chiplet technology and the evolution of the related packaging\ntechnologies, chiplet technology would gain better support by overcoming the problems about\nperformance, power consumption, cost, and implementability.\nTable 4. Comparisons of Chiplet Packaging Technologies.\nPackagingTechnology Substrate-Based SiliconInterposer-Based RDL-BasedFan-Out\nIntegrationDensity Low High Middle\nTransmissionPerformance Low High High\nRoutingResources Highest Higher Middle\nHeatDispersion Middle Middle High\nCost Low High Middle\n3DExtensibility Low Middle High\nPackagingTest\nProvider ChipPackagingTestFactory ChipFoundry Factory/Foundry/Integrated\nDeviceManufacturer\n4.3. Quality Control Technology\nThe dies integrated by chiplets are generally silicon-certified products. It could guarantee the\nvalidity of its design and physical implementation, but the yield problem would still occur during the\nfiltering and packaging process. As for chiplets, the failure is costly because a problem in a single die\nwould cause the monolithic chip failed. Therefore, perfect and comprehensive testing is particularly\nimportant for the quality control of chiplets. Compared with monolithic integration, the'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 28, 'start_token': 7000, 'end_token': 7300, 'text': ' As for chiplets, the failure is costly because a problem in a single die\nwould cause the monolithic chip failed. Therefore, perfect and comprehensive testing is particularly\nimportant for the quality control of chiplets.
