Compared with monolithic integration, the difficulty of\nchip testing is much higher because chiplet packages multiple dies together. Since the pins of chiplets\nare limited, it only guarantees the connection requirements of some pins and some dies during the test.\nThus, it brings new challenges for chiplet comprehensive testing.\nThe EDA software in chiplets is an important solution to solve this problem. During the chip\ndesign and manufacturing process, 30%–40% of the cost is brought from software tools. Chiplets\nrequire EDA tools to provide comprehensive support in terms of architecture exploration, chip design,\nElectronics2020,9,670 10of12\nphysical and packaging implementation. Therefore, with the intelligent and optimized assistance in\neach process, human participation is minimized and extra problems are avoided.\nCurrently, many research institutions and companies in academia and industry have started\nlots of productive work. Jinwoo Kim et al. [11]. from Georgia Institute of Technology introduce the\nEDA process of 2.5D-based chiplet packaging. It covers and automates the entire design phase of\nthe architecture, circuit, and packaging. Besides, the availability of EDA process is validated by a\nROCKET-64 CPU with NOC configured. Moreover, traditional integrated'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 29, 'start_token': 7250, 'end_token': 7550, 'text': ' chiplet packaging. It covers and automates the entire design phase of\nthe architecture, circuit, and packaging. Besides, the availability of EDA process is validated by a\nROCKET-64 CPU with NOC configured. Moreover, traditional integrated circuit EDA companies,\nsuch as Cadence, Synopsys, and Mentor, are developing related tools to support chiplet integration.\n5. Applications and Development Trends of Chiplets\nAlthoughthestandardizationofchipletshasjustbegun, ithasdemonstrateditsuniqueadvantages\nin many fields, ranging from high-performance CPU, FPGA, network chips to low-end chips for\nBluetooth, Internet of Things (IoT) and wearable devices.\nIn terms of high-performance CPU, AMD’s Zen 2 architecture [30] builds chiplets by combining\nmultiple processor core dies (7 nm process), IO dies (14 nm process) and memory dies in different\nprocess nodes. Thus, the improvement of computing performance from the high-end process could be\nachieved at a lower cost.\nIntel’s Stratix 10 high-performance FPGA was developed with chiplet technology in the early\ndays. It integrates FPGA die and SerDes IO die with AIB interface based on EMIB silicon bridge\npackaging technology (2.5D). Stratix 10 integrates the dies in 6 process nodes from 3 chip foundries,\neffectively proving the interoperability of chiplet technology among different foundries. Intel’s Agilex'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 30, 'start_token': 7500, 'end_token': 7800, 'text': ' silicon bridge\npackaging technology (2.5D). Stratix 10 integrates the dies in 6 process nodes from 3 chip foundries,\neffectively proving the interoperability of chiplet technology among different foundries. Intel’s Agilex\nFPGAs use advanced 3D packaging technology for the integration of 10 nm FPGA core and 112 G\nSerDes. It demonstrates that the feasibility of chiplet technology for building high-tech process and\nchips with high IO performance.\nzGlue company focuses on the development and standardization of low-end and mid-range\nchiplets. The chiplets that developed and manufactured by zGlue, such as Bluetooth, Internet of\nThings and WiFi, are developed based on nearly 100 dies from more than 30 companies including ADI,\nDialog, Macronix and Vishay. Moreover, zGlue builds a set of basic chiplet EDA toolchains, making it\npossible to achieve die composition and reuse quickly.\nOverall, since chiplets could provide customizability and optimizability in multiple dimensions\n(such as the yield and cost), it would be widely applied in more fields [33,34]. With the rapid rise of\nopen-source ecology and agile development [35] in the chip field, chiplet approach would become the\nmainstream technology for chip development in the future. Therefore, it is worthwhile for scientific\ninstitutions and industry to research the relevant technical challenges and issues deeply.\n6.'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 31, 'start_token': 7750, 'end_token': 8050, 'text': ' development [35] in the chip field, chiplet approach would become the\nmainstream technology for chip development in the future. Therefore, it is worthwhile for scientific\ninstitutions and industry to research the relevant technical challenges and issues deeply.\n6. Conclusions\nBy summary, this paper introduces the concept of chiplet technology and its developments.\nAs a possible solution to break the slowdown of Moore’s law, chiplet heterogeneous integration\ntechnology has received widespread attention in recent years. Efforts made by industry on reducing\nmanufacturing cost and improving yield during the last decade have been proven to be effective.\nIn order to make better use of chiplet technology, more research should be carried out on related\ntechnologies, such as interconnection and packaging technologies, to simplify systematic integration\ncomplexity with higher performance, lower power consumption, and more area-efficient. What is more,\nfrom the perspective of IC designers, chiplets design is based on new architecture that heterogenous\nre-usable IP dies integrated in a systematic way into a monothetic chip, thus design methodology\nand the corresponding EDA tool development are also urgent research directions that need urgent\nattentions.\nAuthor Contributions: Conceptualization, T.L. and J.H.; Methodology, T.L. and J.Y.; Investigation, R.L. and H.Y.;\nWriting—original draft preparation, T.L., J.H. and J.Y.; Writing—review and editing, R.L., H.Y. and Z.S.;'}, {'source_filename': 'electronics-09-00670-v2.txt', 'title': 'electronics-09-00670-v2', 'chunk_id': 32, 'start_token': 8000, 'end_token': 8300, 'text': 'ology, T.L. and J.Y.; Investigation, R.L. and H.Y.;\nWriting—original draft preparation, T.L., J.H. and J.Y.; Writing—review and editing, R.L., H.Y. and Z.S.; Funding\nacquisition, T.L. and Z.S. A new golden age for computer architecture: Domain-specific\nhardware/software co-design, enhanced security, open instruction sets, and agile chip development.\nIn Proceedings of the 2018 ACM/IEEE 45th Annual International Symposium on Computer Architecture\n(ISCA), Los Angeles, CA, USA, 1–6 June 2018. This article is an open access\narticle distributed under the terms and conditions of the Creative Commons Attribution\n(CC BY) license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).'}, {'source_filename': 'Heterogeneous_Integration_for_Performance_and_Scaling.txt', 'title': 'Heterogeneous_Integration_for_Performance_and_Scaling', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': 'IEEE TRANSACTIONS ON COMPONENTS, PACKAGING AND MANUFACTURING TECHNOLOGY, VOL. 6, NO. 7, JULY 2016 973\nHeterogeneous Integration for\nPerformance and Scaling\nSubramanian S. Iyer, Fellow, IEEE\n(Invited Paper)\nAbstract—Moore’s law has so far relied on the aggressive tighter integration of devices. Bipolar technology was fast but\nscaling of CMOS silicon minimum features of over 1000× power hungry, and this in turn led to the development of\nfor over four decades, and recently, on the adoption of\nelaborate packaging techniques for these chips to allow for\ninnovative features, such as Cu interconnects, low-k dielectrics\nheat dissipation [4]. Downloaded on July 03,2025 at 19:49:32 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.'}, {'source_filename': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI) _ ASE.html', 'title': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI)', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': "## Heterogeneous Integration Roadmap (HIR)\nHeterogeneous Integration Roadmap (HIR)\nEmerging technologies—such as AI, 5G, edge, cloud, and data center, autonomous vehicles and wearable technology— hold great promise for improving the lives of individuals across the globe. The HIR maps out the future of system-level integration and the potential advanced packaging solutions necessary for implementing the emerging technologies.\nASE's Dr. William (Bill) Chen is leading the HIR initiative alongside Dr. Bill Bottoms of 3MTS (IEEE EPS), Tom Salmon (SEMI), S. Iyer (IEEE EDS), A. Helmy (IEEE Photonics), and Ravi Mahajan (ASME EPPD). The Heterogeneous Integration Roadmap is the result of dedicated collaboration and enormous effort by ASE contributors including Rich Rice, CP Hung, John Hunt and Mark Gerber, as well as the many contributors from across the electronics community. The 2021 edition of the Heterogeneous Integration Roadmap includes 24 chapters and can be downloaded by visiting\nhttps://eps.ieee.org/technology/heterogeneous-integration-roadmap/2021-edition.html. (https://eps.ieee.org/technology/heterogeneous-integration-roadmap/2021-edition.html)\n## Solution Platform\nSolution Platform\nThe ASE heterogeneous integration team possess many years of industry-leading experience in packaging and SiP technology development. ASE SiP technology enables ultra-compact, high-cap"}, {'source_filename': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI) _ ASE.html', 'title': 'Heterogeneous Integration (HI)', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 417, 'text': 'ogeneous-integration-roadmap/2021-edition.html)\n## Solution Platform\nSolution Platform\nThe ASE heterogeneous integration team possess many years of industry-leading experience in packaging and SiP technology development. ASE SiP technology enables ultra-compact, high-capacity, low-power module solutions with controller and sensor integration to meet the application needs of AI, IoT and mobile device miniaturization. The company has also developed various business models to actively promote the SiP eco-system. ASE’s innovative SiP and MEMS solutions leverage upon established IC assembly capabilities including copper wiring, flip chip packaging, wafer level packaging, fan-out wafer level packaging, 2.5D/3D IC and embedded chip packaging to address ongoing trends for the mobile device, IoT, high-performance computing, and IoV (Internet of Vehicles) markets.'}, {'source_filename': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI) - AnySilicon.html', 'title': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI)', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': 'Heterogeneous integration\noffers a solution by allowing different materials and components to work together seamlessly, driving innovation in various applications. This approach not only enhances system performance but also enables the development of sophisticated electronic devices that are smaller, faster, and more efficient.\nUnderstanding heterogeneous integration requires a grasp of its key concepts, technologies, and applications. From monolithic 3D integration to chiplet designs, each aspect plays a pivotal role in modern electronics, especially in sectors like medical devices and wearable technology. As industries push the boundaries of performance, innovations such as quantum integrated photonics and specialized transistor technologies emerge as game-changers.\nIn this article, we will explore the multifaceted world of heterogeneous integration, examining its key concepts, latest technological advances, applications, challenges, and future opportunities. By delving into this complex field, you will gain insights into how heterogeneous integration is shaping the next generation of electronic solutions and reshaping the landscape of gadgetry.\n## Key Concepts in Heterogeneous Integration\nKey Concepts in Heterogeneous Integration\nHeterogeneous integration is transforming the electronics industry. This method combines different components onto a single chip. It offers a way to enhance performance and functionality in devices. Let’s dive into what this means and why it matters.\n### Definition of Heterogeneous Integration\nDefinition of Heterogeneous Integration\nHeterogeneous integration refers to combining different semiconductor technologies. These can be in a single chip or closely connected chips. This approach uses various processes like wafer bonding and hybrid'}, {'source_filename': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI) - AnySilicon.html', 'title': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI)', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': '.\n### Definition of Heterogeneous Integration\nDefinition of Heterogeneous Integration\nHeterogeneous integration refers to combining different semiconductor technologies. These can be in a single chip or closely connected chips. This approach uses various processes like wafer bonding and hybrid bonding. Instead of monolithic integration, which uses one type of material, it merges multiple technologies. Components like silicon wafers, silicon chips, and silicon vias play a key role. Think of it as building a puzzle using different technology pieces.\n### Importance in Modern Electronics\nImportance in Modern Electronics\nHeterogeneous integration is crucial for the semiconductor industry today. It helps create more compact and efficient devices. Here are some reasons why it is important:\nEnhanced Performance\n: It boosts the speed and efficiency of electronic devices.\nVersatility\n: It offers flexibility in design, allowing for more innovative products.\nEnergy Efficiency\n: It reduces the thermal budget, saving energy.\nAdvanced Applications\n: Key for areas like autonomous vehicles and sophisticated mobile devices.\nDesign teams rely on this integration to meet modern demands. Heterogeneous Integration Competence Centers help provide guidelines and standard practices. Staying aligned with industry standards is vital for success.\nHere is a simple table to summarize the key elements:\nBy effectively integrating these elements, the semiconductor and electronics industries can push boundaries, creating smarter and more efficient devices.\n## Major Technologies in Heterogeneous Integration\nMajor Technologies in Heterogeneous Integration\nHeterogeneous integration is a vital part of the semiconductor industry'}, {'source_filename': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI) - AnySilicon.html', 'title': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI)', 'chunk_id': 2, 'start_token': 500, 'end_token': 800, 'text': ' effectively integrating these elements, the semiconductor and electronics industries can push boundaries, creating smarter and more efficient devices.\n## Major Technologies in Heterogeneous Integration\nMajor Technologies in Heterogeneous Integration\nHeterogeneous integration is a vital part of the semiconductor industry. It involves combining different materials and components on a single chip. This results in improved performance and functionality. By using techniques like wire bonding and hybrid bonding, manufacturers create advanced silicon wafers and silicon chips. Such integration is key for many industries, including electronics and autonomous vehicles.\n### Monolithic 3D Integration\nMonolithic 3D Integration\nMonolithic 3D integration stacks layers directly on a silicon wafer. This approach optimizes chip communication and increases density. It reduces the thermal budget by using alignment markers to ensure perfect layer line-ups. This method helps in creating a compact design that’s energy efficient. Monolithic integration has become an industry standard due to its impressive benefits.\n### Network-on-Chip (NoC) Architectures\nNetwork-on-Chip (NoC) Architectures\nNetwork-on-Chip (NoC) architectures address chip communication issues. They connect various chip components efficiently. NoCs replace traditional wire connections with a network grid. This allows for better data flow on the silicon vias. A design team can improve performance by designing networks that help with fast information exchange. This is particularly crucial for complex systems in fields like AI and autonomous vehicles.\n### Heterogeneous Waveguide Systems\nHeterogeneous Waveguide'}, {'source_filename': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI) - AnySilicon.html', 'title': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI)', 'chunk_id': 3, 'start_token': 750, 'end_token': 1050, 'text': ' on the silicon vias. A design team can improve performance by designing networks that help with fast information exchange. This is particularly crucial for complex systems in fields like AI and autonomous vehicles.\n### Heterogeneous Waveguide Systems\nHeterogeneous Waveguide Systems\nHeterogeneous waveguide systems use different materials to guide light on a single chip. This helps in faster data transfer and lower power use. Waveguides are essential in the central region of chips where speed is crucial. These systems often guide light through a cavity region, reducing interference. They enhance the capabilities of chips, making them suitable for high-performance tasks. These systems are becoming more important as data demands increase.\nTogether, these technologies define the future of the semiconductor and electronics industry. They support advanced designs, improve functionality, and meet industry standards. As integration evolves, we see more innovations like those from the Heterogeneous Integration Competence Center, which continues to push the boundaries of what’s possible.\n## Applications of Heterogeneous Integration\nApplications of Heterogeneous Integration\nHeterogeneous integration is transforming the semiconductor industry. It combines different chip components into a single chip, enhancing performance and reducing costs. This method is crucial for chip communication and developing complex silicon chip systems. Industries like electronics and autonomous vehicles are adopting this technology to improve efficiency and capability.\n### Integration of GaN and III-V Semiconductors\nIntegration of GaN and III-V Semiconductors\nGaN (Gallium Nitride) (https'}, {'source_filename': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI) - AnySilicon.html', 'title': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI)', 'chunk_id': 4, 'start_token': 1000, 'end_token': 1300, 'text': ' electronics and autonomous vehicles are adopting this technology to improve efficiency and capability.\n### Integration of GaN and III-V Semiconductors\nIntegration of GaN and III-V Semiconductors\nGaN (Gallium Nitride) (https://anysilicon.com/gallium-nitride-gan-overview/)\nand III-V semiconductors are vital in electronics, offering high performance and efficiency. By integrating these materials with silicon wafers, design teams can craft devices with superior thermal management and lower power consumption. This integration, utilizing silicon vias and hybrid bonding, is setting a new industry standard for high-performance electronics.\n### ChipletDesigns for Medical Devices\nChiplet (https://anysilicon.com/the-ultimate-guide-to-chiplets/)\nDesigns for Medical Devices\nMedical devices benefit greatly from advanced chiplet designs. These designs allow for customization and flexibility by combining various small silicon chips into one cohesive unit. The Heterogeneous Integration Competence Center is at the forefront of this innovation. Chiplets in medical devices contribute to faster data processing and improved patient monitoring systems.\n### Wearable Technology Innovations\nWearable Technology Innovations\nWearable technology is seeing a boom thanks to heterogeneous integration. New designs incorporate post-processed CMOS chips and wafer bonding for enhanced functionality. This enables devices like smartwatches and fitness trackers to operate with greater efficiency and longer battery life. With wearables, the compact and innovative design offers users limitless possibilities'}, {'source_filename': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI) - AnySilicon.html', 'title': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI)', 'chunk_id': 5, 'start_token': 1250, 'end_token': 1550, 'text': ' New designs incorporate post-processed CMOS chips and wafer bonding for enhanced functionality. This enables devices like smartwatches and fitness trackers to operate with greater efficiency and longer battery life. With wearables, the compact and innovative design offers users limitless possibilities.\n## Recent Advances in Heterogeneous Integration\nRecent Advances in Heterogeneous Integration\nHeterogeneous integration is making waves in the semiconductor industry. This technology combines different types of chip components onto a single chip. By doing this, it enhances chip communication and performance. Engineers are utilizing silicon vias and wafer bonding to achieve these goals. The electronics industry is buzzing with new ways to reduce the thermal budget, making chips more efficient.\n### Quantum Integrated Photonics\nQuantum Integrated Photonics\nQuantum integrated photonics is a game-changer in the field of heterogeneous integration. It allows light-based information processing. This is crucial for developing autonomous vehicles and fast computing systems. Unlike traditional wire bonding, these technologies rely on silicon wafers and hybrid bonding methods. These advancements are helping design teams create more powerful quantum systems.\n### Complementary FETs (CFET) Technology\nComplementary FETs (CFET) Technology\nComplementary FETs (CFET) technology is creating smaller and more efficient transistors. CFETs offer a route to improve power consumption. The use of CFETs in silicon chips is becoming an industry standard. They help maintain effective chip communication by ensuring precise alignment markers. This innovation is'}, {'source_filename': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI) - AnySilicon.html', 'title': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI)', 'chunk_id': 6, 'start_token': 1500, 'end_token': 1800, 'text': ' creating smaller and more efficient transistors. CFETs offer a route to improve power consumption. The use of CFETs in silicon chips is becoming an industry standard. They help maintain effective chip communication by ensuring precise alignment markers. This innovation is paving the way for more compact and efficient electronic devices.\n### Ferroelectric FETs (FeFET) Developments\nFerroelectric FETs (FeFET) Developments\nFerroelectric FETs (FeFET) developments are taking center stage in heterogeneous designs. FeFETs use materials that have ferroelectric properties. These components provide better speed and lesser power consumption. The central and active regions of these chips benefit from improved performance. Post-processed CMOS chips are also seeing enhancements with FeFET technology. The advancements help in achieving better energy efficiency and data retention.\nIn summary, these advances in heterogeneous integration are revolutionizing the semiconductor sector. From quantum photonics to CFETs and FeFETs, the industry is seeing a technological transformation. These innovations assure a brighter future for electronics and open up endless possibilities for devices and systems.\n## Challenges in Heterogeneous Integration\nChallenges in Heterogeneous Integration\nHeterogeneous integration is crucial in the semiconductor industry. It involves combining different technologies into a single chip. However, integrating different components can be challenging. These challenges must be addressed to meet the industry’s growing demands.\n### Technical Limitations\nTechnical Limitations\nOne major hurdle'}, {'source_filename': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI) - AnySilicon.html', 'title': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI)', 'chunk_id': 7, 'start_token': 1750, 'end_token': 2050, 'text': ' crucial in the semiconductor industry. It involves combining different technologies into a single chip. However, integrating different components can be challenging. These challenges must be addressed to meet the industry’s growing demands.\n### Technical Limitations\nTechnical Limitations\nOne major hurdle is technical limitations. Integrating various chip components into a single system is complex. This process needs precise alignment markers to ensure all parts fit together perfectly. Additionally, the thermal budget must be carefully managed. Overheating can cause damage to delicate components. Design teams must consider these factors when creating heterogeneous designs.\n### Material Compatibility Issues\nMaterial Compatibility Issues\nMaterial compatibility is another significant issue. Different materials may not always work well together. For example, silicon wafers must be compatible with other chip materials. Incompatible materials can affect chip communication and performance. Ensuring material compatibility is essential for successful integration.\n### Cost and Scalability Concerns\nCost and Scalability Concerns\nCost and scalability are also critical concerns. Developing heterogeneous integration solutions can be expensive. The need for advanced technologies, like hybrid bonding and silicon vias, can drive up costs. Moreover, scaling these solutions for mass production is not always easy. The challenge is to balance cost-effectiveness with performance. The industry needs to find ways to make these technologies accessible and affordable for wider use.\nAddressing these challenges is key to advancing the semiconductor and electronics industries. By overcoming them, we can better meet the demands of modern technology, including applications like autonomous vehicles and advanced computing.\n##'}, {'source_filename': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI) - AnySilicon.html', 'title': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI)', 'chunk_id': 8, 'start_token': 2000, 'end_token': 2300, 'text': ' ways to make these technologies accessible and affordable for wider use.\nAddressing these challenges is key to advancing the semiconductor and electronics industries. By overcoming them, we can better meet the demands of modern technology, including applications like autonomous vehicles and advanced computing.\n## Future Directions and Opportunities\nFuture Directions and Opportunities\nHeterogeneous integration is reshaping the semiconductor industry. It brings together different chip components on a single chip. This enables better performance and functionality. The shift opens up exciting opportunities in various sectors. Key areas include edge computing, deep learning applications, and electronic design automation.\n### Impact on Edge Computing\nImpact on Edge Computing\nEdge computing benefits greatly from heterogeneous integration. By combining various silicon wafers, data processing becomes faster at the edge. This reduces the need to send data back to central servers. It improves speed and efficiency in devices like autonomous vehicles. Reduced thermal budget in heterogeneous designs means that devices can handle more complex tasks on-site.\n### Influence on Deep Learning Applications\nInfluence on Deep Learning Applications\nDeep learning applications thrive on powerful computation. Heterogeneous integration helps create silicon chips with enhanced capabilities. This leads to more advanced neural networks. By improving chip communication and using hybrid bonding techniques, systems can run complex algorithms quickly. Such innovations are crucial for developing intelligent applications.\n### Electronic Design Automation Enhancements\nElectronic Design Automation Enhancements\nElectronic Design Automation (EDA) tools are vital for creating complex chip designs. With the rise of heterogeneous integration, EDA tools need upgrades. They must'}, {'source_filename': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI) - AnySilicon.html', 'title': 'Understanding Heterogeneous Integration (HI)', 'chunk_id': 9, 'start_token': 2250, 'end_token': 2419, 'text': ' are crucial for developing intelligent applications.\n### Electronic Design Automation Enhancements\nElectronic Design Automation Enhancements\nElectronic Design Automation (EDA) tools are vital for creating complex chip designs. With the rise of heterogeneous integration, EDA tools need upgrades. They must support new methods like wafer bonding and align alignment markers accurately. Design teams require sophisticated software that accommodates these advancements. This boosts productivity and ensures industry standards are met.\n## Future Directions and Opportunities\nFuture Directions and Opportunities\nThe future of heterogeneous integration holds immense potential. It promises to revolutionize how electronics are developed and used. As the semiconductor industry adapts, the focus will be on creating more efficient and integrated systems. Continuous research and innovation are necessary. Organizations like the Heterogeneous Integration Competence Center are at the forefront. They develop cutting-edge solutions and set benchmarks for future designs.'}, {'source_filename': 'Heterogeneous_Integration_Technologies_for_Artificial_Intelligence_Applications.txt', 'title': 'Heterogeneous_Integration_Technologies_for_Artificial_Intelligence_Applications', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': 'IEEE Journal on Exploratory Solid-State Computational Devices and Circuits\nReceived2August2024;revised2October2024;accepted15October2024.\nDateofpublication23October2024;dateofcurrentversion11November2024.\nDigitalObjectIdentifier10.1109/JXCDC.2024.3484958\nHeterogeneous Integration Technologies for\nArtificial Intelligence Applications\nMADISON MANLEY (Graduate Student Member, IEEE), ASHITA VICTOR,\nHYUNGGYU PARK, ANKIT KAUL, MOHANALINGAM KATHAPERUMAL (Member, IEEE),\nand MUHANNAD S. BAKIR\n(Invited Paper)\nSchoolofElectricalandComputerEngineering,GeorgiaInstituteofTechnology,Atlanta,GA30332USA\nCORRESPONDINGAUTHOR:M.MANLEY(madison.manley@gatech.edu)\nThisworkwassupportedinpartbytheCenterforHeterogeneousIntegrationofMicroelectronicSystems(CHIMES),oneoftheseven\ncentersintheJointUniversityMicroelectronicsProgram2.0(JUMP2.0),aSemiconductorResearchCorporation(SRC)Programsponsored\nbytheDefenseAdvancedResearchProjectsAgency(DARPA).\nABSTRACT The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence (AI) has been enabled by semiconductor-\nbased electronics. However, the conventional methods of transistor scaling are not enough to meet the\nexponential demand for computing power driven by AI'}, {'source_filename': 'Heterogeneous_Integration_Technologies_for_Artificial_Intelligence_Applications.txt', 'title': 'Heterogeneous_Integration_Technologies_for_Artificial_Intelligence_Applications', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': 'ProjectsAgency(DARPA).\nABSTRACT The rapid advancement of artificial intelligence (AI) has been enabled by semiconductor-\nbased electronics. However, the conventional methods of transistor scaling are not enough to meet the\nexponential demand for computing power driven by AI. This has led to a technological shift toward\nsystem-level scaling approaches, such as heterogeneous integration (HI). HI is becoming increasingly\nimplemented in many AI accelerator products due to its potential to enhance overall system performance\nwhile also reducing electrical interconnect delays and energy consumption, which are critical for supporting\ndata-intensive AI workloads. In this review, we introduce current and emerging HI technologies and their\npotential for high-performance systems. We then survey recent industrial and research progress in 3-D HI\ntechnologies that enable high bandwidth systems and finally present the emergence of glass core packaging\nfor high-performance AI chip packages.\nINDEX TERMS Artificial intelligence (AI), glass packaging, heterogeneous integration (HI), packaging.\nI. INTRODUCTION ways, allowing more memory or logic to be integrated within\nThe introduction of ChatGPT in 2022 sparked an exponential a single package [11], [12], [13]. Furthermore, reducing the\ngrowth in artificial intelligence (AI) and high-performance die sizes and performing known good die (KGD) testing\ncomputing (HPC) applications, making AI increasingly more before packaging can enable a higher level of control in die\nimportant to daily life. Large AI models excel at handling performance, resulting in yield'}, {'source_filename': 'Heterogeneous_Integration_Technologies_for_Artificial_Intelligence_Applications.txt', 'title': 'Heterogeneous_Integration_Technologies_for_Artificial_Intelligence_Applications', 'chunk_id': 2, 'start_token': 500, 'end_token': 800, 'text': ' performing known good die (KGD) testing\ncomputing (HPC) applications, making AI increasingly more before packaging can enable a higher level of control in die\nimportant to daily life. Today, semiconductor companies such as\nper-sec, or TOPS) and energy efficiency (TOPS/W) to meet Nvidia, Intel, and AMD have lever'}, {'source_filename': 'Heterogeneous_Integration_Technologies_for_Artificial_Intelligence_Applications.txt', 'title': 'Heterogeneous_Integration_Technologies_for_Artificial_Intelligence_Applications', 'chunk_id': 3, 'start_token': 750, 'end_token': 1050, 'text': ' to achieve high system throughput (tera-operations- designs [17], [18]. In conventional WLP, the KGDs are canincreasesignificantly.Inaddition,thermaldissipationand\nVOLUME10,2024 91\nIEEE Journal on Exploratory Solid-State Computational Devices and Circuits\nIn 2024, Cerebras introduced WSE-3, a wafer-scale AI\naccelerator that is twice as fast as WSE-2, and designed\nto train models 10× larger than GPT-4 and Claude [78].\nInterestingly, Cerebras uses traditional device scaling and\nwafer-scale integration to go beyond Moore’s law. With\nTSMC’s 5-nm technology, four trillion transistors are fabri-\ncated on a single wafer with a chip size that is appropriately\n57× larger compared with a GPU. However, the compute\nand memory components are decoupled to enable memory\ncapacity scaling, so a single WSE-3 system is capable of\nstoring and training models with 24 trillion parameters much\nmore efficiently than a cluster made up of 10000 GPUs.\nCompared with Cerebras, other semiconductor compa-\nFIG'}, {'source_filename': 'Heterogeneous_Integration_Technologies_for_Artificial_Intelligence_Applications.txt', 'title': 'Heterogeneous_Integration_Technologies_for_Artificial_Intelligence_Applications', 'chunk_id': 11, 'start_token': 2750, 'end_token': 3050, 'text': ' so a single WSE-3 system is capable of\nstoring and training models with 24 trillion parameters much\nmore efficiently than a cluster made up of 10000 GPUs.\nCompared with Cerebras, other semiconductor compa-\nFIGURE3. SANCHEZ, A. BOURENNANE, M. BREIL, P. AUSTIN, M. BRUNET,\nJ-P LAUR\nMIXED DESIGN\nLAAS/CNRS, FRANCE\nMIXDES 2007 UNIVERSITY OF TOULOUSE, FRANCE\nCiechocinek, POLAND\nKEYWORDS: Power integration, Functional integration, 3D\n21 – 23 June 2007\nheterogeneous integration, Microfabrication techniques\nABSTRACT: This paper presents a brief overview of the monolithic integration in the field of power electronics.\nEmphasis is mainly put on the functional integration concept. The role that this mode of integration, according to its\nclassical definition, played to enable the monolithic integration of the power device with auxiliary elements (mainly\nprotections and supply) for the realization of new functions dedicated for medium power applications is highlighted. At\nthat end, some of the recent realizations are described in order to showcase some of the potentialities of this mode of\nintegration. Furthermore, to extend further the classical integration towards a 3D “heterogeneous” functional\nintegration, an example that highlights the improvements that should be achieved at the device’s level as well as at the\n'}, {'source_filename': 'Evolution_of_the_Classical_Functional_Integration_Towards_a_3D_Heterogeneous_Functional_Integration.txt', 'title': 'Evolution_of_the_Classical_Functional_Integration_Towards_a_3D_Heterogeneous_Functional_Integration', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': ' of this mode of\nintegration. Furthermore, to extend further the classical integration towards a 3D “heterogeneous” functional\nintegration, an example that highlights the improvements that should be achieved at the device’s level as well as at the\ndevice’s environment level, for the development of new power integrated functions for ac applications, is discussed.\nThe last part deals with the technology process evolution for the realization of the active devices as well as the passive\nelements. In this part, a flexible technological process and its importance in the development of more complex\nfunctions, implemented in 3D within the silicon die volume and at the surface, is described in more detail.\nINTRODUCTION than signal processing circuits. To cut down on\nfabrication costs, the basic structure and technology used\nDuring the last years, an impressive progress has occurred to obtain the integrated function have to be that of the\nin the field of power integrated circuits. This is, on one power device and additional elements can be integrated\nhand, due to the joint evolution of CMOS, BICMOS by adding a minimum number of technological steps. To highlight\nthis point, we rely on examples that are recently reported\nin literature. (a)\nPARTITIONING OF THE POWER\nINTEGRATED FUNCTIONS\nIn the field of power systems, the integration might\ninvolve both low voltage/power circuits and high\nvoltage/power circuits. To achieve such integration, one\ncan use technologies that permit to deal with both, low\nand high voltage/power circuits rather than technologies\nthat can deal with only one circuit type. The big\nadvantage of utilizing a technology that makes possible\nthe integration of both circuit types is the cost but this is\nachieved at the price of a poorer performance of the (b)\nintegrated function. In applications, where fairly\ncomplex signal processing functions and lower power\nhandling capability are required or where the thermal path\nis such that it can keep the device cool regardless in the\ngenerated power, the BCD process is used as illustrated in\nfigure 3 [6]. In applications where devices are mainly\nhandling power with limited signal processing, the power\nprocess alone will often work. An example, as reported in\nliterature, is given in figure 4 that shows simple function Figure.5. This last solution seems to be well suited to considered as unusual working conditions like high dI/dt\ndevelop new integrated functions for medium power and high'}, {'source_filename': 'Evolution_of_the_Classical_Functional_Integration_Towards_a_3D_Heterogeneous_Functional_Integration.txt', 'title': 'Evolution_of_the_Classical_Functional_Integration_Towards_a_3D_Heterogeneous_Functional_Integration', 'chunk_id': 14, 'start_token': 3500, 'end_token': 3800, 'text': ' of the power device are not affected by the operating modes. Extreme operating modes can be\nintegration. This last solution seems to be well suited to considered as unusual working conditions like high dI/dt\ndevelop new integrated functions for medium power and high dV/dt gradients, high dissipated energy and so\napplications and voltage blocking capability in the range on. Power devices are usually used in a wide panel of\n600-1200 V. applications. In many of them, they need to be protected\nfrom external fault conditions and over temperature\nconditions are usually implemented in hybrid way with\nthe power device. The natural evolution is thus to\nintegrate in monolithic way the protection circuits into the\nIGBT in order to increase power system reliability.\n- Overvoltage protection: The overvoltage protection\ncircuits allow to clamp the voltage spikes at an\nacceptable level. Voltage overshoots take place when\nthe device is switched off. An interesting comparison\nstudy is carried out on the different integrated solutions\n(a)\nin [13]. The various integrated techniques encountered\ntend to provide an effective voltage clamping while\nmaking it possible to benefit from thermal cooling\ncapabilities of power switch. There are basically two\nlarge families that can be integrated with the power\nswitch. The first one operates a feedback on power\nswitch gate terminal while the other solution provides a\nparallel path to dissipate energy. In both cases,\nhowever, thermal issues are important to consider since'}, {'source_filename': 'Evolution_of_the_Classical_Functional_Integration_Towards_a_3D_Heterogeneous_Functional_Integration.txt', 'title': 'Evolution_of_the_Classical_Functional_Integration_Towards_a_3D_Heterogeneous_Functional_Integration', 'chunk_id': 15, 'start_token': 3750, 'end_token': 4050, 'text': ' families that can be integrated with the power\nswitch. The first one operates a feedback on power\nswitch gate terminal while the other solution provides a\nparallel path to dissipate energy. In both cases,\nhowever, thermal issues are important to consider since\nvoltage protection circuits may have to dissipate a large\namount of heat. For the purpose of illustration, the over\nvoltage protection circuit given in figure 7 consists of a\nseries of poly-crystalline silicon zener diodes and a bulk\n(b)\navalanche diode. Restrictions apply.\nMicrofabrication techniques for the\nrealization of passive elements\nNumerous works carried out in the field of Microsystems\nhave permitted the development of MEMS devices that\nare mainly used to ensure detection or actuating\nfunctions. The fabrication of these MEMS is based on the\nmicro-fabrication techniques.\nIn the field of mobile electronics, the miniaturisation is\nalso of great importance. Nowadays, the converters size is (a)\nmainly determined by the sizes of the constituting passive\nelements (inductors, capacitors, transformers).\nConsequently, for the above mentionned reasons, the\nfuture micro-converters generations impose the integration\nof passive elements on the silicon wafer or on an insulated\nsubstrate. An integrated device permits miniaturisation and\na collective fabrication that leads to cost reduction as well\nas to reliability improvements due mainly to the reduction\nin the number of interconnections. Many works enabled\nthe realisation of 3D capacitors on silicon as well as\ninductors. This galvanic\n(a'}, {'source_filename': 'Evolution_of_the_Classical_Functional_Integration_Towards_a_3D_Heterogeneous_Functional_Integration.txt', 'title': 'Evolution_of_the_Classical_Functional_Integration_Towards_a_3D_Heterogeneous_Functional_Integration', 'chunk_id': 32, 'start_token': 8000, 'end_token': 8300, 'text': 'For miniaturisation reasons, the integration of the gate\ndriver of power insulated gate transistors of type\nMOSFET or IGBT requires the development of\nintegrable galvanic insulating functions. This galvanic\n(a)\ninsulation can be achieved by a micro-transformer using\neither a piezoelectric coupling or a magnetic coupling.\nThe integration in silicon of piezoelectric micro-\ntransformers to realize insulation between low voltage and\nhigh voltage parts is based on the exploitation of\nmechanical vibrations of multi-layer membrane structures,\na process that is usually employed for the realization of\n(b)\nactuators. The mechanism of power transmission in a\npiezoelectric transformer consists in transforming the Figure 19. (a) a micro-inductor micrographe\nelectric power into mechanical vibrations (primary FeNi magnetic core [34]\narmatures) then to recover these mechanical vibrations in (b) micrographe of a cross section of\nmicro-inductor FeNi magnetic core [34]\nthe form of electric energy in the second armatures [32, 33].\n31\nAuthorized licensed use limited to: Howard University. Restrictions apply.\nSilicon capacitors:\nTo reach a high capacitance per unit surface, a\ncompromise on the dielectric thickness is necessary: the\nminimum dielectric thickness is set by the dielectric\nbreakdown voltage. However, the increase of the\nsurface of the face-to-face electrodes is a purely\ngeometric parameter that can be easily adjusted. For\nsilicon structures, by creating, through etching, trenches\nor pores in the wafer, the developed surface is increased\nwhile the apparent surface is maintained at its\nminimum. The fabrication of three dimensional\ncapacitors using silicon etching has been demonstrated\nfor decoupling functions. A multi-layer ONO (Oxide-\nNitride-silicon Oxide) is deposited by LPCVD (Low\nPressure Chemical Vapor Deposition) in the high form\nfactor pores (figure 20). A doped polysilicon is used as Figure. 21: a micro photograph of a capillary wick structure\na top face electrode. The obtained results are promising made in silicon technology [40]\nsince the capacitance per unit surface is about\n40 nF/mm2 [35]. CONCLUSION AND TRENDS\nIn this paper, a description of some of the realizations\nachieved using the classical functional integration\nconcept permits to highlight the gradual increase of the\nnumber of functions integrated monolithically. The'}, {'source_filename': 'Evolution_of_the_Classical_Functional_Integration_Towards_a_3D_Heterogeneous_Functional_Integration.txt', 'title': 'Evolution_of_the_Classical_Functional_Integration_Towards_a_3D_Heterogeneous_Functional_Integration', 'chunk_id': 34, 'start_token': 8500, 'end_token': 8800, 'text': ' [35]. CONCLUSION AND TRENDS\nIn this paper, a description of some of the realizations\nachieved using the classical functional integration\nconcept permits to highlight the gradual increase of the\nnumber of functions integrated monolithically. The type\nof integration mode (mixing low power circuits with\npower processing devices) depends more upon the\nOxide-nitride\ncomplexity of the signal processing functions as well as\non the amount of power processed by the power\nelements. Meanwhile, impressive breakthroughs were\nachieved in the technology for the realisation of passive\nelements on silicon wafers. Today, the passive elements\nintegration permitted the reduction of low DC power\nconverters size, however, their use could be extended in\nfuture for the realisation of new functionalities such as\ncommunicating switches as described previously.\nAs illustrated in figures 22 and 23, a long term objective\ncould be the integration of the switching function\nFigure 20. 3D capacitors, deep silicon etching high K\n(Integrated Power Switch IPS), i.e the power\ndeposition in trenches [36]\nsemiconductor device, control functions, protection\nMicro-Cooling: functions, isolation, storage elements and may be even the\nIn the last ten years, due to the microelectronic cooling mechanism. Vasic et al.”A new MOSFET &IGBT gate drive heat transfer in MCMs”, IEEE MCMC’95\ninsulated by a piezoelectric transformer”, Power Conference, 1995, pp. 189-194.\nElectronics Specialist Conference, PESC 2001, pp. [40]Y. Avenas, M. Ivanova, N.Popova, C. Schaeffer,\n1479-1484 vol. 3. A. Bricard, “Thermal analysis of thermal spreaders\nused in power electronics cooling”, 37th annual\nIndustry Applications Society Meeting and\nConference, Pittsburgh, USA, Oct. 13-17, 2002.\n34\nAuthorized licensed use limited to: Howard University. Downloaded on June 20,2025 at 18:52:59 UTC from IEEE X'}, {'source_filename': 'Evolution_of_the_Classical_Functional_Integration_Towards_a_3D_Heterogeneous_Functional_Integration.txt', 'title': 'Evolution_of_the_Classical_Functional_Integration_Towards_a_3D_Heterogeneous_Functional_Integration', 'chunk_id': 46, 'start_token': 11500, 'end_token': 11556, 'text': ' and\nConference, Pittsburgh, USA, Oct. 13-17, 2002.\n34\nAuthorized licensed use limited to: Howard University. Downloaded on June 20,2025 at 18:52:59 UTC from IEEE Xplore. Restrictions apply.'}, {'source_filename': 'What Is 3D-IC_ _ 3D-IC Technology Benefits and Overview _ Cadence.html', 'title': '3D-IC Technology', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': '## Benefits of 3D-IC\nBenefits of 3D-IC\nSoCs today can pack increased functionality onto a single silicon die. SoCs typically include a processor, digital logic, memory, analog components, and\nembedded software (/en_US/home/tools/embedded-software.html)\n. Some SoCs have hundreds of millions of gates and are pushing gigahertz speeds. The biggest concern with SoC design today is the rising development costs. Long development cycles also add to the costs.\nThe packaging options that have evolved over the years include package-in-package (PiP)—where several smaller systems in package (SiPs) may be mounted in a larger SiP—and package-on-package (PoP)—where one SiP may be mounted on top of another SiP. Both PiP and PoP assemblies may be categorized as 3D-ICs, but neither offers the performance, power, density, and form factor of true 3D-ICs using TSVs.\nUntil recently, silicon die has been attached to the SiP substrate using conventional wire bond and/or flip-chip technologies. Today, a silicon interposer substrate (either passive or active) may be added to provide much finer die-to-die interconnections, thereby increasing performance and reducing power consumption. A\nsilicon interposer (https://resources.pcb.cadence.com/blog/how-interposers-are-designed-and-used-in-chip-packaging)\nalso includes TSVs, which'}, {'source_filename': 'What Is 3D-IC_ _ 3D-IC Technology Benefits and Overview _ Cadence.html', 'title': '3D-IC Technology', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': ' interconnections, thereby increasing performance and reducing power consumption. A\nsilicon interposer (https://resources.pcb.cadence.com/blog/how-interposers-are-designed-and-used-in-chip-packaging)\nalso includes TSVs, which connect the upper metal layers to additional backside metal layers. Sometimes this technique is referred to as 2.5D stacking.\n3D-ICs with TSVs are expected to broadly impact networking, graphics, mobile communications, and computing, especially for applications that require small, ultra-light, low-power devices. Specific application areas include multi-core CPUs, GPUs, packet buffers/routers, smartphones, tablets, netbooks, cameras, DVD players, and set-top boxes.\n3D-ICs with TSVs represent a significant new trend in the semiconductor industry. With the die-stacking option from different process nodes, the system components, including analog and RF, are no longer restricted to a single process node. Some significant advantages of 3D-ICs with TSVs, compared to traditional SoCs, can be summarized as follows:\nLower costs are possible because not all functionalities, such as analog and memory, need to move to advanced process nodes.\nIt’s easier to meet high interconnect speeds and bandwidth requirements, reaching 100Gbps for advanced memory technologies.\n3D-ICs allow miniaturization, saving board space and end product space. They’re ideal for extremely compact mobile devices.\n3D-ICs'}, {'source_filename': 'What Is 3D-IC_ _ 3D-IC Technology Benefits and Overview _ Cadence.html', 'title': '3D-IC Technology', 'chunk_id': 2, 'start_token': 500, 'end_token': 800, 'text': ' high interconnect speeds and bandwidth requirements, reaching 100Gbps for advanced memory technologies.\n3D-ICs allow miniaturization, saving board space and end product space. They’re ideal for extremely compact mobile devices.\n3D-ICs can reduce power because significant drivers are no longer needed. A 3D stack can use small I/O drivers with lower power. Further, reduced resistance-inductance-capacitance (RLC) helps minimize power.\nInterconnect between packages is reduced, allowing faster performance and a better power profile.\nTime to market can be faster, thanks to modularity, the potential for “die reuse,” and the ability to leave analog/RF at higher process nodes.\nEmerging technologies such as\nphotonics (/en_US/home/solutions/photonics.html)\nor MEMS can be integrated into 3D stacks.\nCompared to a wire-bonded SiP, TSVs offer reduced RLC parasitics, better performance, more power savings, and a denser implementation. Compared to a silicon interposer approach, a vertical 3D die stack provides a higher level of integration, a smaller form factor, and a faster design cycle. But a 3D stack raises additional challenges, including thermal, timing, and power management concerns.\nWhile there are no major showstoppers from a design or process point of view, much still needs to be done to bring 3D-ICs into volume production by mainstream users.'}, {'source_filename': 'What Is 3D-IC_ _ 3D-IC Technology Benefits and Overview _ Cadence.html', 'title': '3D-IC Technology', 'chunk_id': 3, 'start_token': 750, 'end_token': 914, 'text': ' challenges, including thermal, timing, and power management concerns.\nWhile there are no major showstoppers from a design or process point of view, much still needs to be done to bring 3D-ICs into volume production by mainstream users. New capabilities are required in system-level exploration,\n3D floorplanning (/en_US/home/tools/digital-design-and-signoff/soc-implementation-and-floorplanning.html)\n, implementation, extraction/analysis, testing, and IC/package co-design. A 3D-IC silicon realization flow will support unified design intent, abstraction, and convergence with physical and manufacturing data for optimal, timely, and cost-effective design. A well-defined ecosystem, including foundries, IP providers, EDA vendors, and OSATs, must emerge with design kits and reference flows.'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 0, 'start_token': 0, 'end_token': 300, 'text': 'AFRL-RY-WP-TR-2017-0168\nHETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY\nDr. Burhan Bayraktaroglu\nDevices for Sensing Branch\nAerospace Components & Subsystems Division\nAUGUST 2017\nFinal Report\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\nSee additional restrictions described on inside pages\nSTINFO COPY\nAIR FORCE RESEARCH LABORATORY\nSENSORS DIRECTORATE\nWRIGHT-PATTERSON AIR FORCE BASE, OH 45433-7320\nAIR FORCE MATERIEL COMMAND\nUNITED STATES AIR FORCE\nNOTICE AND SIGNATURE PAGE\nUsing Government drawings, specifications, or other data included in this document for any\npurpose other than Government procurement does not in any way obligate the U.S. Government.\nThe fact that the Government formulated or supplied the drawings, specifications, or other data\ndoes not license the holder or any other person or corporation; or convey any rights or permission to\nmanufacture, use, or sell any patented invention that may relate to them.\nThis report was cleared for public release by the USAF 88th Air Base Wing (88 ABW) Public\nAffairs Office (PAO) and is available to the general public, including foreign nationals. Copies may\nbe obtained from the Defense Technical Information Center (DTIC) (http://www.dtic.mil).\nAFRL-RY-WP-TR-2017'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 1, 'start_token': 250, 'end_token': 550, 'text': 'PAO) and is available to the general public, including foreign nationals. Copies may\nbe obtained from the Defense Technical Information Center (DTIC) (http://www.dtic.mil).\nAFRL-RY-WP-TR-2017-0168 HAS BEEN REVIEWED AND IS APPROVED FOR\nPUBLICATION IN ACCORDANCE WITH ASSIGNED DISTRIBUTION STATEMENT.\n*//Signature//\nBURHAN BAYRAKTAROGLU, Program Manager\nDevices for Sensing Branch\nAerospace Component & Subsystems Division\n*//Signature//\nROSS W. DETTMER\nChief\nDevices for Sensing Branch\nAerospace Component & Subsystems Division\nThis report is published in the interest of scientific and technical information exchange, and its\npublication does not constitute the Government’s approval or disapproval of its ideas or findings.\n*Disseminated copies will show “//Signature//” stamped or typed above the signature blocks.\nForm Approved\nREPORT DOCUMENTATION PAGE\nOMB No. 0704-0188\nThe public reporting burden for this collection of information is estimated to average 1 hour per response, including the time for reviewing instructions, searching existing data sources, gathering and\nmaintaining the data needed, and completing and reviewing the collection of information. ABSTRACT\nThis report provides'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 4, 'start_token': 1000, 'end_token': 1300, 'text': ' public release; distribution is unlimited.\n13. SUPPLEMENTARY NOTES\nPAO case number 88ABW-2017-2617; Clearance Date 24 May 2017. Report contains color.\n14. ABSTRACT\nThis report provides a review of the heterogeneous integration of different types of devices and materials for the purpose\nof increasing the functional density and therefore the performance of electro-optic systems for sensor applications. A\ncategorization of heterogeneous integration methods is provided to provide a structure for this review. The history and\nthe current status of integration technologies in each category are examined and product examples are provided. Finally,\npotential applications are discussed where heterogeneous integration may provide an advantage for electronic system\napplications.\n15. SUBJECT TERMS\nheterogeneous integration, electro-optic systems, sensor applications\n16. SECURITY CLASSIFICATION OF: 17. LIMITATION 18. NUMBER\n19a. NAME OF RESPONSIBLE PERSON (Monitor)\nOF ABSTRACT: OF PAGES\na. REPORT b. ABSTRACT c. THIS PAGE Burhan Bayraktaroglu\nSAR 108\nUnclassified Unclassified Unclassified\n19b. Summary of Heterogeneous Integration Techniques..................................................... 64\nvii\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\n1. EXECUTIVE SUMMARY\nThis report provides a review of the heterogeneous integration of different types of devices and\nmaterials for the purpose of increasing the functional density and the performance of electro-\noptic systems for sensor applications.\nThe importance of heterogeneous integration can be best understood if we first consider the\nelectronic systems alone. The impressive integration levels achieved on a single chip for\nelectronic systems is continuing thanks to ever shrinking transistor feature sizes and the\nimprovements in the interconnect technology. Almost all electronic applications (digital,\nmemory, analog, power etc.) benefit from this improvement in integration density and transistor\nimprovements. However, the design, fabrication and testing maturity are not the same for all\napplications and therefore different technology nodes are employed for each application at a\ngiven time. Integrating several functions on the same chip requires the use of the lowest\n'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 21, 'start_token': 5250, 'end_token': 5550, 'text': 'vements. However, the design, fabrication and testing maturity are not the same for all\napplications and therefore different technology nodes are employed for each application at a\ngiven time. Integrating several functions on the same chip requires the use of the lowest\ncommon technology node rather than the best available one for each. This is where\nheterogeneous integration first comes in. Rather than using the lowest common technology node\nfor all applications, heterogeneous integration allows the use of the best available technology\nnode for each application to maintain maximum performance. Multiple application components\ncan be separately fabricated and then heterogeneously integrated with each other on a common\nplatform to preserve the compactness while increasing the performance and the functionality\ndensity.\nThe functional density of electronic systems can be further enhanced by integrating different\ntypes of devices made from advanced materials. Such devices can provide functions that cannot\nbe obtained from the baseline technology (mostly refers to Si complementary metal-oxide-\nsemiconductor (CMOS)). These added functions can be imaging, optical signal processing,\nmechanical functions (i.e. inertial) or high frequency amplification. Co-integration of such\nfunctions increases the functional density even further.\nWe will examine the concept of heterogeneous integration first and determine how this\ntechnology may help in product development. To provide a structure for this review, we will\ncategorize heterogeneous integration techniques. This is a fast moving technology area driven by\ncommercial products and the technology front is rapidly changing. Categories are kept'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 22, 'start_token': 5500, 'end_token': 5800, 'text': ' this\ntechnology may help in product development. To provide a structure for this review, we will\ncategorize heterogeneous integration techniques. This is a fast moving technology area driven by\ncommercial products and the technology front is rapidly changing. Categories are kept at a high\nlevel to avoid obsolescence because of this rapid change. The history and the current status of\nintegration technologies in each category are examined and product examples are provided.\nFinally, we discuss potential applications where heterogeneous integration may provide an\nadvantage for electronic system applications.\nThis report provides only a technology review. It is not intended for identifying existing\nproblems and proposing new solutions. Different readers may benefit from this document by\nconcentrating on different sections. Those interested in understanding the purpose of\nheterogeneous integration, will benefit from sections 2-6. Categorization of heterogeneous\nintegration technologies can be found in sections 7-8. Those interested in the technical details of\nvarious integration methods can find them in sections 9-12. If you are only interested in the\nexamples of heterogeneous integration, they are in section 13. Summary and recommendations\nare in section 14.\n1\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\n2. INTRODUCTION\nBy word definition, “heterogeneous integration” (HI) refers to the integration of dissimilar\ncomponents on a common platform. The term is extensively used in very diverse applications to\nencompass efforts to make previously separate functions to'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 23, 'start_token': 5750, 'end_token': 6050, 'text': ' INTRODUCTION\nBy word definition, “heterogeneous integration” (HI) refers to the integration of dissimilar\ncomponents on a common platform. The term is extensively used in very diverse applications to\nencompass efforts to make previously separate functions to operate together by an intimate\nfusion of components. It can mean a seamless integration of previously incompatible software,\ndatabase, drugs or machine parts. The particular definition that is of significance for us is the\nintegration of dissimilar sensor components onto a common substrate to make new compact\ncomponents that provide enhanced characteristics. The concept of heterogeneous integration that\nis most closely aligned to our needs is the one used by the Institute of Electrical and Electronics\nEngineers (IEEE) as formulized in the International Technology Roadmap for Semiconductors\n(ITRS 2.0) documents. We will focus our assessment by concentrating on this specific version.\nHowever, a broader definition will also be used in connection with some types of HI.\nIEEE defines Heterogeneous Integration to be “the integration of separately manufactured\ncomponents into a higher-level assembly that, in the aggregate, provides enhanced functionality\nand improved operating characteristics”. In this definition, components should be taken to mean\nany unit whether individual die, micro-electromechanical systems (MEMS) device, passive\ncomponent and assembled package or sub-system that are integrated into a single package. The\noperating characteristics should also be taken in its broadest meaning including characteristics\nsuch as system level performance and'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 24, 'start_token': 6000, 'end_token': 6300, 'text': 'romechanical systems (MEMS) device, passive\ncomponent and assembled package or sub-system that are integrated into a single package. The\noperating characteristics should also be taken in its broadest meaning including characteristics\nsuch as system level performance and cost of ownership [1].\nHeterogeneous integration includes a variety of technology components ranging from transistors\nand their fabrication methods to testing and packaging. Therefore, the heterogeneous integration\nroadmap activities are sponsored by more than a single IEEE chapter. Although the lead IEEE\nChapter is the IEEE Components, Packaging and Manufacturing Technology Society (CPMT),\nother IEEE Societies such as IEEE Electron Devices Society (EDS) and IEEE Photonics Society\nare involved as well as the Semiconductor Equipment and Materials International (SEMI)\norganization. The intention is to expand the roadmap collaboration to other IEEE Technical\nSocieties that share interest in the Heterogeneous Technology Roadmap as well as to\norganizations outside IEEE that share this common vision for the roadmap [2].\nWhen we think about the evolution of electronic systems, we often form opinions by relying\nsolely on the progress of “digital technologies” whose evolution is often governed by the so-\ncalled “Moore’s Law”. This empirical rule predicts that the number of transistors of an (digital)\nelectronic system will double every 2 years. The transistor geometries are expected to continue\nshrinking to accommodate ever higher density circuits following the well-known technology\nroadmaps such as the'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 25, 'start_token': 6250, 'end_token': 6550, 'text': ' that the number of transistors of an (digital)\nelectronic system will double every 2 years. The transistor geometries are expected to continue\nshrinking to accommodate ever higher density circuits following the well-known technology\nroadmaps such as the ITRS. These roadmaps help synchronize technology development efforts\nby providing guidance to research communities and funding agencies. So far, the use of such\nroadmaps have been very successful in improving resource efficiencies and in providing\npredictions in the capabilities of future systems.\nUntil recently, compliance with the Moore’s Law used to require simple geometrical shrinkage\n(geometric scaling) of transistor feature sizes so that more transistors can be accommodated on a\ngiven chip size. Smaller transistor gate lengths also improved the device performance by\nreducing the transit time for electrons between electrodes. This trend has continued unbroken\n2\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\nuntil about 2004, when it became apparent that simply cramming more transistors in an area is\nnot the best way to improve (digital) system performance. Delays due to metal interconnects and\nthe formation of thermal hot spots have severely limited the performance gains obtained by\ndensity improvements [3]. Currently, the circuit density is still increasing but the operation\nfrequency is saturating, as shown in Figure 1.\nFigure 1: Number of Transistors per Chip has doubled every 2 Years and this Trend is\ncontinuing'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 26, 'start_token': 6500, 'end_token': 6800, 'text': '3]. Currently, the circuit density is still increasing but the operation\nfrequency is saturating, as shown in Figure 1.\nFigure 1: Number of Transistors per Chip has doubled every 2 Years and this Trend is\ncontinuing as the Transistor Feature Sizes Shrink\nBut the speed of chips saturated since 2004 due to high temperature limits [Source: Intel].\nOther scaling approaches are being investigated to increase the chip performance without\nnecessarily reducing transistor size, in which case the circuit density increase will saturate. This\nnew reality can be seen by comparing the device feature size reduction expectations of the 2013\nITRS roadmap with the more recent 2015 ITRS roadmap expectations as shown in Figure 2 [4].\nWhile previously it was expected that the physical gate length shrinkage would continue until at\nleast 2028, now it is believed that it will saturate in 2021 when the 9nm node it reached. Any\nfurther increase in circuit density will now come from 3D architectures. The circuit density\nimprovements by 3D stacking of similar circuits (functions) is “homogeneous integration”.\nThe same approach can be applied to the integration of digital circuits with other types of\ncircuits. These circuits may include functions that can be best implemented by a different\ntechnology node. They may include functions that are implemented in technologies that do not\nfollow the Moore’s Law (such as analog/radio frequency'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 27, 'start_token': 6750, 'end_token': 7050, 'text': ' with other types of\ncircuits. These circuits may include functions that can be best implemented by a different\ntechnology node. They may include functions that are implemented in technologies that do not\nfollow the Moore’s Law (such as analog/radio frequency (RF) circuits). They may indeed\ninclude functions that are not even electronic (such as optical, mechanical or acoustic). The\nintegration of these diverse function circuits is “heterogeneous integration”.\n3\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\nFigure 2: Physical Gate Length Shrinkage of Transistors in 2013 and 2015 [4]\n4\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\n3. HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION\nBefore we define what heterogeneous integration means and what technologies it contains, we\nmust clarify what it does not mean. Heterogeneous integration is not a continuation of the\nMoore’s Law through 3D integration. Moore’s Law is strictly for the rate of increase in digital\ncircuit integration density. Heterogeneous integration is for the integration of different functions.\nBoth activities appeared to merge recently with the introduction of 3D integration at the chip\nlevel. This is, however, is only an appearance based on the similarities in the integration\nmethods. While, integration density increases are still implemented at the chip level, functional\ndensity increases are implemented both at the chip level and in packages.\nAs mentioned above, the integration density improvements are beginning'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 28, 'start_token': 7000, 'end_token': 7300, 'text': ' an appearance based on the similarities in the integration\nmethods. While, integration density increases are still implemented at the chip level, functional\ndensity increases are implemented both at the chip level and in packages.\nAs mentioned above, the integration density improvements are beginning to saturate when\nconfined to 2D surfaces, and therefore the third dimension will be used to continue\nimprovements. This trend has already started with the stacking of memory chips and will spread\nto processor chips. This new path to integration density increase is called the More Moore\n(MM) roadmap.\nSystem in Package (SiP) integration has always been about functionality integration and it was\nused to integrate multi-functional chips in one location. There are many ways to increase the\ndensity of functions in a package, as we will review in detail below. Some of these involve\nstacking chips on each other whereas others involve stacking packages on top of each other.\nThere are also many choices in electrical signal routing between chips involving bond wires, ball\ngrid arrays, through Si vias (TSV), interposer layers etc. The increase in functionality density\nthrough heterogeneous integration is called More than Moore (MtM).\nFigure 3 is a simplified diagram that shows the use of 3D integration for both integration and\nfunctional density improvements. When similar technologies are integrated, such as dynamic\nrandom-access memory (DRAM) chips, the final assembly has the same function as the\nindividual chips but the integration density is increased'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 29, 'start_token': 7250, 'end_token': 7550, 'text': ' of 3D integration for both integration and\nfunctional density improvements. When similar technologies are integrated, such as dynamic\nrandom-access memory (DRAM) chips, the final assembly has the same function as the\nindividual chips but the integration density is increased. This is called 3D-Integrated Circuit (3D-\nIC). The integration density increase follows the MM roadmap. When diverse technology\ncomponents are integrated, heterogeneous integration results. The evolution of this integration\napproach is MtM.\nHeterogeneous\n3D-IC Integration\n3D Integration\nDiverse Technologies\nSame Technology\nFigure 3: 3D Integration of Similar or Diverse Technology Components follows MM and\nMtM Roadmaps\n5\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\nytisneD\nnoitargetnI\nytisneD\nlanoitcnuF\n4. WHY DO WE NEED HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION?\nIn 2016, ITRS 2.0 was introduced to capture this new reality in technology outlook. This is the\nfirst time ITRS is not based on the expected improvements in the transistor technology\nunderscoring the anticipation that the microelectronics product performance improvement will\nno longer be paced solely by gate length shrinkage. Most commercial (outside of data center\napplications) and military applications will benefit from the integration of “non-digital”\nfunctions with digital circuits. In this new approach, more functions are offered per'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 30, 'start_token': 7500, 'end_token': 7800, 'text': 'no longer be paced solely by gate length shrinkage. Most commercial (outside of data center\napplications) and military applications will benefit from the integration of “non-digital”\nfunctions with digital circuits. In this new approach, more functions are offered per given chip\narea in contrast to the previous approach where only increased performance of the same\nfunction was offered from the same chip area as the technology evolved. The road mapping\nactivities that govern such highly integrated heterogeneous systems is known as “More-than-\nMoore” (MtM). MtM shifts the intellectual epicenter of product development from the transistor\nlevel innovations to system-level design innovations. Heterogeneous Integration plays an\nimportant role in the implementation of MtM. This is especially true for military applications\nwhere it takes longer to field technology innovations in products.\nMilitary microelectronic sensor systems are complex systems that require the use of a range of\ndifferent technologies, as indicated in Figure 4. For various valid reasons, such as reliability,\ntesting, re-work and availability, components made using these technologies are integrated on\nprinted circuit boards (PCBs). Sometimes, several components can be integrated inside modules,\nwhich are custom packages. A sensor system constructed in this fashion has the usual\ncommunication bandwidth problems due to time delay caused by component interface networks.\nTo reduce delay, components must be placed closer to each other, and if possible, direct\nconnections must be made (rather than using bond wires).\nFigure 4: Many Different Technologies'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 31, 'start_token': 7750, 'end_token': 8050, 'text': 'communication bandwidth problems due to time delay caused by component interface networks.\nTo reduce delay, components must be placed closer to each other, and if possible, direct\nconnections must be made (rather than using bond wires).\nFigure 4: Many Different Technologies are used in the Implementation of Modern MEMS\nwhich can benefit from HI\n6\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\nThere are a number of drivers for integrating components together into a common package.\nThese include:\n(cid:120) Compact and disposable sensors\n(cid:120) Higher communication bandwidth\n(cid:120) Integrated data processing\n(cid:120) Improved size, weight, power, and cost (SWAP-C)\n(cid:120) Simplified supply chain management\nMany of the technology solutions in the form of heterogeneous integration on wafers or in\npackages developed in response to these drivers for commercial applications are also suitable for\nsome military systems. We will review all technology approaches currently available in open\nliterature and provide examples of improvements achieved by HI so far. The information\nreferenced in this document are from open literature. No proprietary information is included.\nFirst, we examine the potential impact of the HI on a typical product development strategy.\n7\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\n5. IMPACT OF HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION ON PRODUCT\nDEVELOPMENT\nThe impact of heterogeneous integration on future products of large organizations with their\nindependent research and development (R&D)'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 32, 'start_token': 8000, 'end_token': 8300, 'text': ' release; distribution is unlimited.\n5. IMPACT OF HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION ON PRODUCT\nDEVELOPMENT\nThe impact of heterogeneous integration on future products of large organizations with their\nindependent research and development (R&D) capabilities can be visualized by examining the\nschematic representation of the technology food chain for products, as shown in Figure 5. The\nscience and technology (S&T) base on the left side includes a variety of components ranging\nfrom materials, device concepts, fabrication methodology to circuit designs. This is an ever\nevolving area for many organizations with several diverse ideas being pursued in parallel. In the\ncase for commercial applications, Si digital IC’s make up a significant portion of this base. The\nSi IC technology is evolving as fast as it can (Moore’s Law and More Moore) for commercial\napplications thanks to huge investments made by several multi-international companies. Most\norganizations no longer maintain independent development in this area, but instead rely on\ntechnology roadmaps to anticipate the availability of performance levels at a given future time\nwithout necessarily investing in its evolution.
