A >100X reduction in overall\nsize was shown to be possible for the same wireless system through miniaturization.\n72\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\nFigure 90: Schematic View of the 24 GHz 3D Sensor Node Integration Platform\nThe'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 135, 'start_token': 33750, 'end_token': 34050, 'text': ' overall\nsize was shown to be possible for the same wireless system through miniaturization.\n72\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\nFigure 90: Schematic View of the 24 GHz 3D Sensor Node Integration Platform\nThe top RF module (on a glass substrate) with integrated antenna and the lower IF/baseband\nmodule on a silicon substrate [174].\nA compact quad-band microwave module developed by EPCOS integrates SiP module 2.4G and\n5G WLAN, Bluetooth, GPS, FM radio, and FM transmitter in a compact assembly measuring\n9.5xll.9x1.2mm3. Figure 91 shows the block diagram and a picture of the module [176]. This\nmodule is an example of 3D SiP integration featuring PoP assembly approach. The RF front-end\ncircuits are fabricated in a separate chip, whereas digital functions are confined to another.\nSeveral filter circuits were implemented in LTCC. All IC’s are integrated inside their own\npackage (WLP) and all packaged components are further integrated in another package with\n6-layer laminate interconnects and metal lids.\nFigure 91: Compact Quad-Band Microwave Module Implemented in 3D SiP PoP\nIntegration [176]\n13.7 Integrated Radar Systems\nCompact transceiver modules with integrated antennas were developed for commercial\nmillimeter-wave radar systems using embedded wafer level ball grid array (eWLB) [164, '}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 136, 'start_token': 34000, 'end_token': 34300, 'text': ' SiP PoP\nIntegration [176]\n13.7 Integrated Radar Systems\nCompact transceiver modules with integrated antennas were developed for commercial\nmillimeter-wave radar systems using embedded wafer level ball grid array (eWLB) [164, 165],\nwhich is a slight variation of the eWLP discussed above. At mm-wave frequencies, the circuit\nparasitic elements such as impedance mismatches are difficult to control when circuits are\nassembled. Chip-level assemblies are therefore well suited for these applications where such\nparasitic circuit elements can be minimized. The eWLB-based SiP design makes use of metal\nlines on RDL layers for the fabrication of external circuit elements such as high-Q inductors and\nantennas. The interconnect line lengths can be kept quite small on the RDL when transceiver\nchips are connected together and to the antenna. Since the fabrication of eWLB package is\nwafer-based, a large number of modules can be fabricated simultaneously with minimal\ndimensional variations. Based on these technological strengths, a 77-GHz four-channel\n73\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\ntransceiver module with four integrated antennas was fabricated in a eWLB package measuring\n8mm × 8mm [177]. In comparison, the same system realized on PCB with wire bonded bare\ntransceiver chips measured 4cm x 5cm. A picture of the integrated module is shown in Figure 92'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 137, 'start_token': 34250, 'end_token': 34550, 'text': ' package measuring\n8mm × 8mm [177]. In comparison, the same system realized on PCB with wire bonded bare\ntransceiver chips measured 4cm x 5cm. A picture of the integrated module is shown in Figure 92.\nThe module integrates four half-wave dipole antennas that are realized using thin-film RDL of\nthe eWLB. The antennas are connected to the transceiver chip using 100-(cid:525)(cid:3)(cid:71)(cid:76)(cid:73)(cid:73)(cid:72)(cid:85)(cid:72)(cid:81)(cid:87)(cid:76)(cid:68)(cid:79)(cid:3)coplanar\nstrip (CPS) lines realized in the RDL. These type of compact mm-wave modules are found in\nautomotive radar sensor applications [178].\nFigure 92: 77GHz, Four-channel Transceiver Module with Integrated Antennas\n(a) Picture of the eWLB module, (b) the single-chip transceiver, and (c) block diagram of the\nfour-channel transceiver [167].\nAnother compact radar system integrated in eWLB package is the 60 GHz frequency modulated\ncontinuous wave (FMCW) radar for industrial applications [179] and automotive radar\napplications [180]. The transceiver module had two dipole antennas in the package, as shown in\nFigure 93'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 138, 'start_token': 34500, 'end_token': 34800, 'text': ' the 60 GHz frequency modulated\ncontinuous wave (FMCW) radar for industrial applications [179] and automotive radar\napplications [180]. The transceiver module had two dipole antennas in the package, as shown in\nFigure 93. The single chip transceiver contained a VCO with a buffer amplifier, mixers, power\ndividers, a vector modulator and IQ receiver. The maximum power output was 3dBm.\nFigure 93: Compact Radar Module for 60 GHz FMCW Radar Applications\n(a) The integrated module in eWLB package with 2 dipole antennas, (b) single chip transceiver\nchip, and (c) block diagram of the module [179]\n13.8 Electro-optic Systems for Automobile Applications\nProbably the most ambitious heterogeneous integration attempt was made by the Tohoku\nUniversity for intelligent vehicle systems [181]. Two different types of interposer substrates\nwere developed for this demonstration for integration of electronic functions on one and the\noptical functions on the other. These two substrates were then integrated to form a single\nmodule, as shown schematically in Figure 94. The electronic interposer, measuring 15mm x\n74\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\n8mm includes TSVs for interconnects as well as micro-channels for cooling fluid. Logic and\nmemory chips were integrated on this substrate together with MEMS. The optical interposer,\nmeasuring '}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 139, 'start_token': 34750, 'end_token': 35050, 'text': ' public release; distribution is unlimited.\n8mm includes TSVs for interconnects as well as micro-channels for cooling fluid. Logic and\nmemory chips were integrated on this substrate together with MEMS. The optical interposer,\nmeasuring 27mmx11mm, contained vertical-cavity surface-emitting laser (VCSEL) optical\nsources and photodiode (PD) detectors. The optical signal is routed within the interposer. The\ninterconnect technologies used for this integration included micro-bumps and TSVs and Cu\nlateral interconnects [182, 183].\nFigure 94: Conceptual 3D Integrated Sensor Module for Intelligent Vehicle Applications\nThis heterogeneous integration includes both electrical and optical interposer substrates [181]\n13.9 Integrated Multi-Sensor Nodes\nA conceptual military application of highly miniaturized sensor nodes is illustrated in Figure 95\n[184]. In this application, each node contains multiple sensors heterogeneously integrated in a\n3D SiP similar to those shown in Figure 89 and Figure 90. All nodes dispersed in a field of\noperation communicates with each other and with a base unit to download gathered information.\nThe feasibility of such systems rely on batch fabrication of 3D integrated electronic and optical\nsystems in small volumes [184].\nFigure 95: Highly Integrated Sensor Nodes for Integrated Classification-and-Decision-\nInformation Extraction Capability from a Sensed Environment [184]\n75\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 140, 'start_token': 35000, 'end_token': 35300, 'text': '3D integrated electronic and optical\nsystems in small volumes [184].\nFigure 95: Highly Integrated Sensor Nodes for Integrated Classification-and-Decision-\nInformation Extraction Capability from a Sensed Environment [184]\n75\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\n14. SUMMARY AND RECOMMENDATIONS\nIEEE defines HI to be “the integration of separately manufactured components into a higher-\nlevel assembly that, in the aggregate, provides enhanced functionality and improved operating\ncharacteristics”. In this definition components are taken to mean any unit whether individual die,\nMEMS device, passive component and assembled package or sub-system that are integrated into\na single package. The operating characteristics should also be taken in its broadest meaning\nincluding characteristics such as system level performance and cost of ownership.\nHI is employed in the implementation of the MtM technology roadmap, which guides\ndevelopments to increase the “functionality density” of electronic systems. Functional density\ncan be increased by separately fabricating each function using the best available technology and\nthen integrating these components in a compact unit. Functional density can be also increased by\nintegrating different device types of devices (alternative materials, optical, mechanical etc.). The\nintegration can take place on a chip or in a package.\nHI makes use of the best available technologies and offers timely solutions to system-level\ninnovations. A survey of the current status of HI shows that the technical approaches used for\ncommercial products are highly specific to the applications. This is an indication that'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 141, 'start_token': 35250, 'end_token': 35550, 'text': 'HI makes use of the best available technologies and offers timely solutions to system-level\ninnovations. A survey of the current status of HI shows that the technical approaches used for\ncommercial products are highly specific to the applications. This is an indication that HI is more\nresponsive to systems-pull than to technology-push. For an R&D organization such as ours with\ndiverse technology portfolios and many system application opportunities, HI provides an\nimportant core competency platform that may act as a clearing house to match the system needs\nwith the advanced technology solutions. In a technology development “food chain” extending\nfrom basic research to systems design, HI represents a midpoint, where it is close enough to both\nends of the food chain to be responsive to innovations originating from either end of the chain. In\nits absence, the influence of scientific innovations take many decades to reach system designs.\nSimilarly, system designs innovations rarely extend all the way to basic science end of the chain\nto influence their direction.\nWe have provided a general categorization of HI technologies to aid in the review. Details of\nintegration methods under each category were examined and examples were provided. It was\nobserved that the technology maturity levels are not linearly dependent on the increasing\nintegration dimension, meaning that the availability of HI products seem to be relatively\nindependent of the dimensional complexity. Some of the earliest and the most successful HI\nproducts (accelerometers and digital light processors) are a result of 3D integration'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 142, 'start_token': 35500, 'end_token': 35800, 'text': 'integration dimension, meaning that the availability of HI products seem to be relatively\nindependent of the dimensional complexity. Some of the earliest and the most successful HI\nproducts (accelerometers and digital light processors) are a result of 3D integration. Some of the\nlatest high volume HI products for high bandwidth memory-CPU integration involves only 2.5D.\nOn the other hand, 2D integration of InP-CMOS circuits is still in development.\nCommercial products are the main drivers behind the HI technology development. However,\nDoD and specifically AF has invested consistently in the development of integration\ntechnologies for microwave and high speed circuit applications. Initially, these activities were\nconfined to integrating GaAs on Si substrates, but recently other device types (i.e. InP HBTs and\nGaN HEMTs) are being integrated on Si CMOS circuits. There are many direct military\napplications of the commercially developed HI technologies. In high speed computing\napplications, improvements in performance, size and cost due to the use of HI technologies will\ndirectly benefit military systems. For example, the ultra-high bandwidth FPGAs enabled by 2.5D\n76\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\nHI is a product that may find military applications. Other HI innovations in 3D SoC integration\nrelated to high speed imaging systems also have military applications. However, military-\nspecific applications may require unique HI technology development effort.'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 143, 'start_token': 35750, 'end_token': 36050, 'text': ' distribution is unlimited.\nHI is a product that may find military applications. Other HI innovations in 3D SoC integration\nrelated to high speed imaging systems also have military applications. However, military-\nspecific applications may require unique HI technology development effort. One of these may be\nthe development of highly integrated microwave systems. Most commercial HI technologies\naddress the complexity of interconnects in digital circuits, but the integration of microwave\ncircuits is more challenging. There has been some progress in microwave multi-chip modules for\ncommercial applications to reduce their size and cost by evolving them from 2D SiP to wafer\nlevel packaging (eWLP). This effort can be extended to full 3D SiP or 3D WLB by integrating\ndigital and microwave circuits together with antennas [185]. This is especially important for mm-\nwave systems, whose design and fabrication is highly sensitive to electrical discontinuities that\nare inherent in hybrid solutions.\nAs a final note, it is worth re-emphasizing that HI requires no new basic technology\ndevelopment. It can be highly responsive to systems-level innovations and can be instrumental in\nimplementing these innovations in a timely manner.\n77\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\n15. ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS\nThis technical review would not have been possible without the help and the critique of many\ncolleagues in the AFRL Sensors Directorate Aerospace Components & Subsystems Division\n(RYD). The contributions of the following'}, {'source_filename': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY.txt', 'title': 'HETEROGENEOUS INTEGRATION TECHNOLOGY', 'chunk_id': 144, 'start_token': 36000, 'end_token': 36300, 'text': '.\n15. ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS\nThis technical review would not have been possible without the help and the critique of many\ncolleagues in the AFRL Sensors Directorate Aerospace Components & Subsystems Division\n(RYD). The contributions of the following are gratefully acknowledged:\nPaul Watson; Integrated Circuits & Microsystems Branch (RYDI)\nSteve Hary; RYD\nRobert Fitch; Devices for Sensing Branch (RYDD)\nTony Quach; RYDI\nKelson Chabak; RYDD\nJim Gillespie; RYDD\nBrad Paul; RYDI\n78\nApproved for public release; distribution is unlimited.\n16. embeddings = []
prefix = 'Represent this sentence for retrieval: '
model = SentenceTransformer( (0): Transformer({'max_seq_length': 512, 'do_lower_case': True, 'architecture': 'BertModel'}) (1): Pooling({'word_embedding_dimension': 768, 'pooling_mode_cls_token': True, 'pooling_mode_mean_tokens': False, 'pooling_mode_max_tokens': False, 'pooling_mode_mean_sqrt_len_tokens': False, 'pooling_mode_weightedmean_tokens': False, 'pooling_mode_lasttoken': False, 'include_prompt': True}) (2): Normalize() )
chunk_filepath = 'FORAGER_corpus/heterogenous_integration/chunks/chunks.jsonl'
test_chunk_filepath = 'FORAGER_corpus/heterogenous_integration/chunks/test_chunks.jsonl'
faiss_db_filepath = 'FORAGER/vector_database/index_db.faiss'
def embed_chunks(data, m, n): 
37def embed_chunks(data, m, n):
38    """
39    Takes one chunk at a time from the new additions to chunks.jsonl file (or other inputted file) and embeds it using BGE model
40
41    Arguments: 
42        filepath: The file path of the JSONL document containing the chunks genereated by ingestor.py and chunker.py
43        m: Starting chunk entry
44        n: Ending chunk entry
45    Returns:
46        embeddings: A 2-D numpy array of vectors, each representing a chunk
47
48    Variable type info: 
49        chunk - dictionary (and each line in the JSONL file is a chunk)
50        list of chunks - Python list
51        text in chunk - one value in the chunk dictionary associated with "text" key
52        "embeddings" - 2-D array (specifically "np.ndarray") with all the vectors. Each vector has a length of 768 using the BGE model
53        Each element of "embeddings" is also of the type np.ndarray
54        The dimensions of the "embeddings" array are [(length of chunk file), 768]
55    """
56
57    global prefix
58    global embeddings
59
60    # Select chunks to embed
61    chunks_to_embed = data[m:n]
62    print(f"\033[1;94mEmbedding {len(chunks_to_embed)} chunks...\033[0m\n")
63
64    # Extract and prefix the text
65    texts = [prefix + d["text"] for d in chunks_to_embed] #The BGE model expects the prefix to come before what we want to embed
66    len_before_embed = len(embeddings)
67
68    # Embed each chunk and normalize the resulting vector. Convert to float32 for insertion into FAISS index
69    embeddings = model.encode(texts, normalize_embeddings = True).astype("float32")
70    print(f"\033[1;92m✅ {len(embeddings) - len_before_embed} chunks successfully embedded!\033[0m\n")
71
72    # Return the 2-D array of vectors
73    return embeddings

Takes one chunk at a time from the new additions to chunks.jsonl file (or other inputted file) and embeds it using BGE model

Arguments: filepath: The file path of the JSONL document containing the chunks genereated by ingestor.py and chunker.py m: Starting chunk entry n: Ending chunk entry Returns: embeddings: A 2-D numpy array of vectors, each representing a chunk

Variable type info: chunk - dictionary (and each line in the JSONL file is a chunk) list of chunks - Python list text in chunk - one value in the chunk dictionary associated with "text" key "embeddings" - 2-D array (specifically "np.ndarray") with all the vectors. Each vector has a length of 768 using the BGE model Each element of "embeddings" is also of the type np.ndarray The dimensions of the "embeddings" array are [(length of chunk file), 768]

def initial_embed_and_build(filepath): 
 75def initial_embed_and_build(filepath):
 76    """
 77    Run this once when first running FORAGER to embed the first set of chunks and create the FAISS database
 78
 79    Arguments:
 80        filepath: path to the JSONL file containing the chunks to be embedded
 81
 82    Returns:
 83        embeddings: 2-D array of vectors
 84        faiss_db: New FAISS index stored in "FORAGER/vector_database/index_db.faiss"
 85    """
 86    global faiss_db
 87    global chunks
 88
 89    print(f"Filepath being used: {filepath}\n")
 90    # Initially opens the JSONL file, making each line a dictionary
 91
 92    with open(filepath, "r", encoding="utf-8") as f:
 93        chunks = [json.loads(line) for line in f if line.strip()]
 94
 95    # Embed all chunks initially in the JSONL and put them into the FAISS database
 96    embeddings = embed_chunks(chunks, 0, len(chunks))
 97
 98    faiss_db.add(embeddings)
 99    print(f"\033[1;92m✅ {faiss_db.ntotal} chunks successfully inserted into new FAISS database!\033[0m\n")
100
101    # Write to the .faiss file to save the database
102    faiss.write_index(faiss_db, "FORAGER/vector_database/index_db.faiss")
103    print(f"\033[1;92m✅ FAISS database stored in {faiss_db_filepath}!\033[0m\n")
104    return embeddings, faiss_db

Run this once when first running FORAGER to embed the first set of chunks and create the FAISS database

Arguments: filepath: path to the JSONL file containing the chunks to be embedded

Returns: embeddings: 2-D array of vectors faiss_db: New FAISS index stored in "FORAGER/vector_database/index_db.faiss"

def add_to_FAISS(new_embedded_chunks): 
106def add_to_FAISS(new_embedded_chunks):
107    """
108    Function to embed and add chunks from newly uploaded documents into FAISS
109
110    Arguments:
111        new_embedded_chunks: New vectors to add to FAISS database
112    """
113
114    faiss_db.add(new_embedded_chunks)
115    faiss.write_index(faiss_db, faiss_db_filepath)

Function to embed and add chunks from newly uploaded documents into FAISS

Arguments: new_embedded_chunks: New vectors to add to FAISS database

def search_database(query, num_vectors): 
117def search_database(query, num_vectors):
118    """
119    Function to search the FAISS database for the closest k chunks to the query (based on dot product)
120    
121    Arguments:
122        query: User query or LLM response
123        num_vectors: Number of closest vectors to return
124    """
125
126    global prefix
127    global chunks
128    global embeddings
129
130    # Embed query
131    query_with_prefix = [prefix + query]
132    query_emb = model.encode(query_with_prefix, normalize_embeddings = True).astype("float32")
133
134    # Ensure k <= length of index
135    num_vectors = min(num_vectors, faiss_db.ntotal)
136
137    # Search index
138    scores, indices = faiss_db.search(query_emb, num_vectors)
139    # print(f"Scores: \n{scores}\n")
140    # print(f"indices: \n{indices}\n")
141
142    # Show results
143    print(f"\n\033[1;94mReturning the {num_vectors} most similar chunks for query: \"{query}\"\033[0m\n")
144    # print(chunks)
145    for rank, idx in enumerate(indices[0]):
146        print(f"{rank+1}. Source: {chunks[idx]["source_filename"]} \nChunk: {chunks[idx]["chunk_id"]} \nScore: {scores[0][rank]:.4f}\n")
147        print(f"{chunks[idx]["text"]}\n")

Function to search the FAISS database for the closest k chunks to the query (based on dot product)

Arguments: query: User query or LLM response num_vectors: Number of closest vectors to return

def print_vector_in_array(embeddings, n): 
149def print_vector_in_array(embeddings, n):
150    """
151    Prints a vector in the 2-D array of chunk vectors at entry n
152
153    Arguments:
154        embeddings: 2-D array of chunk vectors
155        n: row of array to print (which vector)
156    """
157    print(f"Printing embedded vector at entry {n}...")
158    print(f"{embeddings[n]}\n")

Prints a vector in the 2-D array of chunk vectors at entry n

Arguments: embeddings: 2-D array of chunk vectors n: row of array to print (which vector)

def print_vector_in_FAISS(n): 
160def print_vector_in_FAISS(n):
161    """
162    Prints a specified vector in the FAISS database
163
164    Arguments: 
165        n: id of vector to print
166    """
167    print(f"Printing vector in FAISS index at entry {n}...")
168    print(f"{faiss_db.reconstruct(n)}\n")

Prints a specified vector in the FAISS database

Arguments: n: id of vector to print

def clear_database(): 
170def clear_database():
171    """
172    Takes everything out of the FAISS database, if necessary.
173    """
174    faiss_db.reset()

Takes everything out of the FAISS database, if necessary.

test_query = 'What is heterogeneous integration?'
